<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Two Stages? — The Driving-Force Argument

In a single equilibrium stage the extract leaving in equilibrium with the raffinate still contains "unspent" capacity. By re-contacting the raffinate with a new solvent portion (or solvent enriched differently), the **net driving force** $(y^{*} - y)$ is maintained over multiple contacts. Two stages are the **smallest cascade** that gives a meaningful improvement and serve as the building block of the **Kremser equation** for $N$ stages.

## 2.2 Three Governing Principles for Every Stage

1. **Total material balance** — overall conservation of mass.
2. **Component (solute) material balance** — track solute A around the stage.
3. **Phase equilibrium** — relate $y_{i}$ to $x_{i}$ at the stage exit (for an *ideal/theoretical* stage).

> [!NOTE]
> An **ideal** (or **theoretical**, **equilibrium**) stage is one where the exiting two phases are in *thermodynamic equilibrium* with each other — the assumption used in **all KTU derivations** unless stage efficiency is explicitly given.

## 2.3 Classification of Two-Stage Cascades

### (a) Cross-current two-stage

- **Fresh solvent** (composition $y_{S}$, usually $y_{S} = 0$) is supplied **independently** to **each** stage.
- The raffinate from stage 1 becomes the feed to stage 2.
- Two extracts are produced (one per stage), each with its own solvent-recovery step.

### (b) Counter-current two-stage

- **Fresh solvent** enters the **last** stage (stage 2) with $y_{S}$.
- The extract from stage 2 (composition $y_{2}$) becomes the solvent entering stage 1.
- The raffinate from stage 1 becomes the feed to stage 2.
- **Only one final extract** and **one final raffinate** leave the cascade.
- The solute-rich extract meets the solute-rich feed first → maximum utilisation of solvent capacity.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below are expressed on a **mass-fraction, mass-flow basis**. For a *dilute, constant-K, constant-L, constant-V* system the following relations are valid and form the **core formula set** examinable in ESE.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distribution law:} \quad & y_{i}^{*} = K \, x_{i} \\[4pt]
\text{Extraction factor:} \quad & A \;=\; \dfrac{KV}{L} \\[4pt]
\text{Overall mass balance (CCC):} \quad & L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S} \;=\; L\,x_{2} + V\,y_{1} \\[4pt]
\text{Single stage (CC, fresh solvent):} \quad & x_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S}}{L + K\,V} \\[4pt]
\text{Two-stage cross-current:} \quad & x_{2} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{L}{L + K\,V}\right)^{2} x_{F} \quad (y_{S}=0) \\[4pt]
\text{Two-stage counter-current:} \quad & x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{L^{2} x_{F} + y_{S}\,V\,L + y_{S}\,K\,V^{2}}{L^{2} + K\,V\,L + K^{2}V^{2}} \\[4pt]
\text{Extraction efficiency:} \quad & \eta \;=\; \dfrac{x_{F} - x_{2}}{x_{F}} \times 100 \,\% \\[4pt]
\text{Kremser (general N, CCC):} \quad & x_{N} \;=\; \dfrac{x_{F}(1-A) + (y_{S}/K)\,(A^{N+1}-A)}{1 - A^{N+1}}, \quad A\neq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compact Symbol Table for Quick Recall**

| Symbol | Compact Form | Physical Meaning | Boundary / Limit |
|--------|--------------|------------------|------------------|
| $K$ | $y^{*}/x^{*}$ | Partition ratio | $K \to \infty \Rightarrow$ perfect extraction |
| $A$ | $KV/L$ | Extraction factor | $A=1 \Rightarrow$ symmetric; special Kremser form required |
| $x_{2}/x_{F}$ | – | Un-extracted fraction | Always $\leq 1$ for a real cascade |
| $\eta$ | $1 - x_{2}/x_{F}$ | Fraction extracted | Higher for CCC than CC at equal $V$ |
| $\mu$ | – | Murphree efficiency $= (x_{i-1}-x_{i})/(x_{i-1}-x_{i}^{*})$ | $\mu = 1$ for ideal stage |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Two-stage solvent extraction is the *workhorse* configuration in:

- **Pharmaceuticals** — recovery of antibiotics (e.g., penicillin G into amyl acetate, then back into water).
- **Petroleum refining** — furfural/phenol extraction to separate aromatics from aliphatics.
- **Nuclear reprocessing** — PUREX process uses a 2- to 3-stage mixer-settler cascade for U/Pu separation.
- **Hydrometallurgy** — separation of Cu, Co, Ni from leach liquors using LIX reagents.
- **Bioprocessing** — aqueous two-phase extraction (ATPS) for enzyme purification, where two stages achieve >95 % recovery that a single stage cannot.

The **minimum solvent requirement** for a target $x_{2}$ is obtained by setting $A$ in the Kremser equation and solving — this is a routine KTU-style optimisation problem.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below use **theoretical (ideal) stages** with **constant** $K$, $L$, $V$ — the standard KTU 2024 assumption. Each algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation A — Single-Stage Material Balance (Foundation for N=2)

Consider one mixer–settler where feed (carrying $L$ kg/h of diluent and solute at $x_{F}$) contacts $V$ kg/h of solvent at $y_{S}$. Let the exit raffinate be at $x_{1}$ and the exit extract at $y_{1}$.

**Step 1 — Total mass balance (overall):**

$$L + V = L + V \quad (\text{trivially satisfied, since } L, V \text{ are diluent and solvent flows, not total flows})$$

**Step 2 — Solute (A) material balance:**

$$L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S} \;=\; L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium at the stage exit:**

$$y_{1} \;=\; K\,x_{1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute equilibrium into the solute balance:**

$$L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S} \;=\; L\,x_{1} + V\,K\,x_{1} \;=\; (L + KV)\,x_{1}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x_{1}$ (raffinate composition after one stage):**

$$
x_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S}}{L + K\,V}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $y_{1}$ (extract composition, if needed):**

$$
y_{1} \;=\; K\,x_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{K\,(L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S})}{L + K\,V}
$$

This is the **building-block equation**; two-stage cascades are obtained by *cascading* it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Two-Stage Cross-Current Extraction

**Schematic of cascade:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Feed }(L,x_{F}) + \text{Fresh solvent }(V, y_{S}) \to \text{Stage 1} \to (x_{1}, y_{1})} \\
\boxed{\text{Raffinate }(L, x_{1}) + \text{Fresh solvent }(V, y_{S}) \to \text{Stage 2} \to (x_{2}, y_{2})}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the single-stage formula to Stage 1:**

$$
x_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S}}{L + K\,V}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the same single-stage formula to Stage 2** (with $x_{F}$ replaced by $x_{1}$ and the *same* fresh solvent composition $y_{S}$):

$$
x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{S}}{L + K\,V}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $x_{1}$ from Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$
x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{L + K\,V}\!\left[\,L\!\left(\dfrac{L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S}}{L + K\,V}\right) + V\,y_{S}\,\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Take the common denominator $(L + K\,V)$ out:**

$$
x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{L^{2}\,x_{F} + L\,V\,y_{S} + V\,y_{S}(L + K\,V)}{(L + K\,V)^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} \;=\; &L^{2}\,x_{F} + L\,V\,y_{S} + V\,y_{S}\,L + K\,V^{2}\,y_{S} \\
\;=\; &L^{2}\,x_{F} + 2\,L\,V\,y_{S} + K\,V^{2}\,y_{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Specialise to the very common KTU case of *pure* solvent** ($y_{S} = 0$):

$$
\boxed{\,x_{2} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{L}{L + K\,V}\right)^{2}\,x_{F}\,} \quad (y_{S}=0)
$$

**Step 7 — Recognise the geometric-series / repeated-fraction pattern:**

For $N$ cross-current stages with the **same** $V$ per stage and $y_{S}=0$:

$$
\boxed{\,x_{N} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{L}{L + K\,V}\right)^{N}\,x_{F}\,}
$$

which is the **Kremser equation for cross-current extraction**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Two-Stage Counter-Current Extraction (the KTU heavy-weight)

**Schematic of the cascade:**

| Stage | Diluent (L) entering | Solvent (V) entering | Diluent leaving | Solvent leaving |
|-------|----------------------|----------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $L,\,x_{F}$ (feed) | $V,\,y_{2}$ (from stage 2) | $L,\,x_{1}$ | $V,\,y_{1}$ (final extract) |
| 2 | $L,\,x_{1}$ (from stage 1) | $V,\,y_{S}$ (fresh solvent) | $L,\,x_{2}$ (final raffinate) | $V,\,y_{2}$ |

**Step 1 — Solute balance around Stage 1:**

$$
L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{2} \;=\; L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{1} \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Solute balance around Stage 2:**

$$
L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{S} \;=\; L\,x_{2} + V\,y_{2} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium at both stages:**

$$
y_{1} \;=\; K\,x_{1} \quad\text{and}\quad y_{2} \;=\; K\,x_{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute equilibrium into (i):**

$$
L\,x_{F} + V\,K\,x_{2} \;=\; L\,x_{1} + V\,K\,x_{1} \;=\; (L + KV)\,x_{1}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve (i) for $x_{1}$:**

$$
x_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{L\,x_{F} + K\,V\,x_{2}}{L + K\,V} \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute equilibrium into (ii):**

$$
L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{S} \;=\; L\,x_{2} + V\,K\,x_{2} \;=\; (L + KV)\,x_{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve (ii) for $x_{1}$:**

$$
x_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{(L + K\,V)\,x_{2} - V\,y_{S}}{L} \quad \text{...(iv)}
$$

**Step 8 — Equate (iii) and (iv):**

$$
\dfrac{L\,x_{F} + K\,V\,x_{2}}{L + K\,V} \;=\; \dfrac{(L + K\,V)\,x_{2} - V\,y_{S}}{L}
$$

**Step 9 — Cross-multiply by $L(L + K\,V)$:**

$$
L\,(L\,x_{F} + K\,V\,x_{2}) \;=\; (L + K\,V)\bigl[(L + K\,V)\,x_{2} - V\,y_{S}\bigr]
$$

**Step 10 — Expand both sides:**

$$
\text{LHS} \;=\; L^{2}\,x_{F} + L\,K\,V\,x_{2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS} \;=\; &(L + K\,V)^{2}\,x_{2} - V\,y_{S}\,(L + K\,V) \\
\;=\; &\bigl(L^{2} + 2\,L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}\bigr)\,x_{2} - V\,y_{S}\,(L + K\,V)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11 — Collect the $x_{2}$ terms on the LHS:**

$$
L^{2}\,x_{F} + L\,K\,V\,x_{2} - \bigl(L^{2} + 2\,L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}\bigr)\,x_{2} \;=\; -V\,y_{S}\,(L + K\,V)
$$

**Step 12 — Simplify the coefficient of $x_{2}$:**

$$
L^{2}\,x_{F} \;-\; \bigl(L^{2} + L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}\bigr)\,x_{2} \;=\; -V\,y_{S}\,(L + K\,V)
$$

**Step 13 — Multiply through by $-1$ and isolate $x_{2}$:**

$$
\bigl(L^{2} + L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}\bigr)\,x_{2} \;=\; L^{2}\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S}\,(L + K\,V)
$$

**Step 14 — Write the final closed-form expression:**

$$
\boxed{\;x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{L^{2}\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S}\,(L + K\,V)}{L^{2} + L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}}\;}
$$

Expanding the numerator term $V\,y_{S}\,(L + K\,V) = L\,V\,y_{S} + K\,V^{2}\,y_{S}$ gives the alternative form quoted in the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.4):

$$
\boxed{\;x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{L^{2}\,x_{F} + L\,V\,y_{S} + K\,V^{2}\,y_{S}}{L^{2} + L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}}\;}
$$

**Step 15 — Specialise to pure solvent $y_{S} = 0$:**

$$
\boxed{\;x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{L^{2}\,x_{F}}{L^{2} + L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}}\;}
$$

**Step 16 — Express in terms of the extraction factor $A = K\,V/L$ (dividing numerator and denominator by $L^{2}$):**

$$
x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{x_{F}}{1 + A + A^{2}}
$$

which is exactly the **Kremser equation for $N = 2$ with $y_{S}=0$**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style)

**Problem.** A feed containing **30 wt %** acetic acid in water (1000 kg/h) is to be extracted with **pure isopropyl ether** at 25 °C. The equilibrium data give $K = y^{*}/x^{*} = 0.728$ (mass-fraction basis). The ether flow is **800 kg/h** and the diluent (water) flow is $L = 0.70 \times 1000 = 700$ kg/h. Calculate the final raffinate composition and the percent extraction for **(a)** two-stage counter-current and **(b)** two-stage cross-current extraction with the *same total* solvent (400 kg/h per stage).

> [!NOTE]
> For 30 wt % acid and 0.728 partition coefficient, a constant-K assumption is acceptable as a first engineering approximation and is the standard assumption in KTU problem statements.

### (a) Counter-current solution

Compute $A$:

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{KV}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{0.728 \times 800}{700} \;=\; 0.8320
$$

Apply the $N = 2$, $y_{S}=0$ Kremser form:

$$
x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{x_{F}}{1 + A + A^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.30}{1 + 0.8320 + (0.8320)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.30}{1 + 0.8320 + 0.6922} \;=\; \dfrac{0.30}{2.5242}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,x_{2} \;=\; 0.1188 \;=\; 11.88 \,\text{wt \%}\,}
$$

Extraction efficiency:

$$
\eta_{\text{CCC}} \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{0.1188}{0.30}\right)\times 100 \;=\; 60.4 \,\%
$$

### (b) Cross-current solution

Per-stage $V = 400$ kg/h. Single-stage raffinate composition:

$$
x_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{L\,x_{F}}{L + K\,V} \;=\; \dfrac{700 \times 0.30}{700 + 0.728 \times 400} \;=\; \dfrac{210}{700 + 291.2} \;=\; \dfrac{210}{991.2} \;=\; 0.2119
$$

After Stage 2 (with the *same* fresh solvent):

$$
x_{2} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{L}{L + K\,V}\right)^{2}\,x_{F} \;=\; (0.7062)^{2} \times 0.30 \;=\; 0.4987 \times 0.30 \;=\; 0.1496
$$

$$
\boxed{\,x_{2} \;=\; 0.1496 \;=\; 14.96 \,\text{wt \%}\,}
$$

Extraction efficiency:

$$
\eta_{\text{CC}} \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{0.1496}{0.30}\right)\times 100 \;=\; 50.1 \,\%
$$

### (c) Comparison

$$
\eta_{\text{CCC}} \;=\; 60.4 \,\% \quad > \quad \eta_{\text{CC}} \;=\; 50.1 \,\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Counter-current uses the **same total solvent** but recovers ~10 % more solute. This is why industrial cascades are almost always counter-current.

### (d) Analytical Matrix — How design parameters affect $x_{2}$

| Parameter | Increase → | Effect on $x_{2}$ (CCC) | Effect on $\eta$ | Engineering lever |
|-----------|------------|--------------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| $K$ (better solvent) | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | Solvent screening |
| $V/L$ (more solvent) | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | Operating cost ↑ |
| $N$ (more stages) | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | Capital cost ↑ |
| $y_{S}$ (impure solvent) | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | Solvent recovery |
| $x_{F}$ (richer feed) | ↑ | ↑ (slightly) | ↓ | Feed pre-concentration |
| $T$ (if K is T-dependent) | varies | varies | varies | Process optimisation |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Two-Stage Counter-Current Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA1["FEED F plus L xF"] --> nodeB1
    nodeB2["Solvent V y2 from Stage 2"] --> nodeB1
    nodeB1["MIXER SETTLER STAGE 1"] --> nodeC1
    nodeB1["MIXER SETTLER STAGE 1"] --> nodeC2
    nodeC1["Extract y1 leaves to Solvent Recovery"] --> nodeC1a["Recovery of Solute A"]
    nodeC2["Raffinate x1 leaves Stage 1"] --> nodeB2mix
    nodeD1["Fresh Solvent V yS Pure"] --> nodeB2mix
    nodeB2mix["MIXER SETTLER STAGE 2"] --> nodeE1
    nodeB2mix["MIXER SETTLER STAGE 2"] --> nodeE2
    nodeE1["Extract y2 returned to Stage 1"] --> nodeB2
    nodeE2["Final Raffinate x2 leaves the Cascade"] --> nodeE2a["Downstream Processing"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The bold arrows (FEED, Fresh Solvent, Extract to Recovery, Final Raffinate) are the **external** streams; the internal loops (Raffinate x1 → Stage 2; Extract y2 → Stage 1) implement the *counter-current* flow.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Algorithm Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nInput["INPUT Data L V xF yS K"] --> nCheck["Verify Assumption yS equals 0 OR given"]
    nCheck --> nMode{"Select Cascade Mode"}
    nMode -- CROSS CURRENT --> nCC["Apply x2 equals L by L plus KV squared xF"]
    nMode -- COUNTER CURRENT --> nCCC["Apply Kremser x2 equals xF by 1 plus A plus A squared"]
    nCC --> nEff["Compute eta equals 1 minus x2 by xF times 100"]
    nCCC --> nEff
    nEff --> nOut["OUTPUT x2 y1 y2 eta"]
    nOut --> nSens["Sensitivity Check vary V or K"]
    nSens --> nOpt["Optimise minimum V for target x2"]
```

**Block-function mapping:**

| Block | Function | KTU Learning Outcome |
|-------|----------|-----------------------|
| `nInput` | Capture design data (L, V, xF, yS, K) | CO1 — Define the problem |
| `nMode` | Branch into CC or CCC algorithm | CO2 — Classify cascades |
| `nCC` / `nCCC` | Apply the closed-form stage formula | CO3 — Apply material-balance mathematics |
| `nEff` | Convert composition to extraction efficiency | CO4 — Interpret results |
| `nSens` / `nOpt` | Parametric study for design | CO5 — Optimise the design |

## 4.3 Stage-Internal Schematic — A Single Mixer–Settler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sInA["Diluent phase L x in"] --> sMix
    sInB["Solvent phase V y in"] --> sMix
    sMix["MIXER Contact zone turbulence promotes mass transfer"] --> sSet
    sSet["SETTLER Gravity separation of two immiscible phases"] --> sOutA["Diluent phase L x out"]
    sSet --> sOutB["Solvent phase V y out"]
    sOutA -. equilibrium with .-> sOutB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dotted line "equilibrium with" indicates the **Nernst law** connection $y_{\text{out}} = K\,x_{\text{out}}$ that defines an **ideal** stage — the KTU default assumption.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below model the **actual KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: 3-mark short-answer (Part A) and 14-mark long-answer with **internal choice** (Part B).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
Define the **distribution coefficient** $K$ and the **extraction factor** $A$ as used in two-stage solvent extraction. State the units of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **distribution coefficient** $K$ is the equilibrium ratio of the concentration of solute A in the solvent-rich (extract) phase to that in the diluent-rich (raffinate) phase:

$$K \;=\; \dfrac{y^{*}}{x^{*}} \quad (\text{dimensionless, when expressed in the same fraction basis})$$

The **extraction factor** $A$ is the dimensionless group formed by the ratio of the solute capacity of the solvent phase to that of the diluent phase:

$$A \;=\; \dfrac{K\,V}{L} \quad (\text{dimensionless})$$

$K$ is a thermodynamic property; $A$ is a process-design parameter. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
State **two advantages** of counter-current two-stage extraction over cross-current two-stage extraction when the total solvent usage is the same.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher extraction efficiency for the same solvent** — counter-current maintains the maximum driving force $(y^{*} - y)$ at every stage, yielding a *lower* final raffinate composition $x_{2}$ (and a higher $\eta$). (1.5 marks)
2. **Single extract of higher solute concentration** — all the recovered solute is in one exit stream, simplifying downstream solvent-recovery (distillation) — only **one** solvent-recovery step is needed instead of two. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 2 × 14 marks)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q1(a)]** (14 marks)

**(a)** Starting from the fundamental solute material balance and the Nernst equilibrium law, **derive** the closed-form expression for the final raffinate composition $x_{2}$ in a **two-stage counter-current** extraction system operating with pure solvent ($y_{S} = 0$). Express the final answer in terms of the extraction factor $A = K\,V/L$.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A feed of **1000 kg/h** containing **30 wt %** acetic acid in water is extracted with **800 kg/h of pure isopropyl ether** ($K = 0.728$, mass-fraction basis). Calculate **(i)** the exit raffinate composition $x_{2}$, **(ii)** the percent extraction, and **(iii)** the concentration of the final extract $y_{1}$.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

Write the solute balance for **Stage 1** and **Stage 2**:

$$
L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{2} = L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{1} \quad \text{...(i)} \quad [\text{1 mark}]
$$

$$
L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{S} = L\,x_{2} + V\,y_{2} \quad \text{...(ii)} \quad [\text{1 mark}]
$$

Apply the Nernst equilibrium law at each stage:

$$
y_{1} = K\,x_{1} \quad \text{and} \quad y_{2} = K\,x_{2} \quad [\text{0.5 marks}]
$$

Substitute the equilibrium relations into (i) and (ii), and eliminate $y_{1}, y_{2}$:

$$
L\,x_{F} + K\,V\,x_{2} = (L + K\,V)\,x_{1} \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

$$
L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{S} = (L + K\,V)\,x_{2} \quad \text{...(iv)} \quad [\text{1 mark}]
$$

Set $y_{S}=0$ (pure solvent). From (iv):

$$
L\,x_{1} = (L + K\,V)\,x_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; x_{1} = \left(1 + \dfrac{K\,V}{L}\right) x_{2} = (1+A)\,x_{2} \quad [\text{1 mark}]
$$

Substitute $x_{1}$ into (iii):

$$
L\,x_{F} + K\,V\,x_{2} = (L + K\,V)(1+A)\,x_{2} = (L + K\,V)\,\dfrac{L + K\,V}{L}\,x_{2} = \dfrac{(L + K\,V)^{2}}{L}\,x_{2}
$$

Multiply through by $L$:

$$
L^{2}\,x_{F} + L\,K\,V\,x_{2} = (L + K\,V)^{2}\,x_{2}
$$

Expand $(L + K\,V)^{2}$ and collect $x_{2}$ terms:

$$
x_{2}\,\bigl[(L + K\,V)^{2} - L\,K\,V\bigr] = L^{2}\,x_{F}
$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$
(L + K\,V)^{2} - L\,K\,V = L^{2} + 2\,L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2} - L\,K\,V = L^{2} + L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{L^{2}\,x_{F}}{L^{2} + L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}}\,} \quad [\text{2 marks}]
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $L^{2}$ and introduce $A = K\,V/L$:

$$
\boxed{\,x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{x_{F}}{1 + A + A^{2}}\,} \quad [\text{0.5 marks}]
$$

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

Given: $F = 1000$ kg/h, $x_{F} = 0.30$, $V = 800$ kg/h, $L = 0.70 \times 1000 = 700$ kg/h, $K = 0.728$.

**(i) Final raffinate composition $x_{2}$:**

Compute the extraction factor:

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{K\,V}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{0.728 \times 800}{700} \;=\; 0.8320 \quad [\text{1 mark}]
$$

Apply the $N = 2$ Kremser form:

$$
x_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{x_{F}}{1 + A + A^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.30}{1 + 0.8320 + 0.6922} \;=\; \dfrac{0.30}{2.5242} \;=\; 0.1188
$$

$$
\boxed{\,x_{2} \;=\; 0.1188 \;=\; 11.88 \,\text{wt \% acetic acid in raffinate}\,} \quad [\text{2 marks}]
$$

**(ii) Percent extraction $\eta$:**

$$
\eta \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{x_{2}}{x_{F}}\right)\times 100 \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{0.1188}{0.30}\right)\times 100 \;=\; 60.4 \,\% \quad [\text{2 marks}]
$$

**(iii) Final extract composition $y_{1}$:**

Overall solute balance (CCC):

$$
L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S} = L\,x_{2} + V\,y_{1}
$$

With $y_{S}=0$:

$$
V\,y_{1} = L\,(x_{F} - x_{2}) \;\Rightarrow\; y_{1} = \dfrac{L\,(x_{F} - x_{2})}{V} = \dfrac{700 \times (0.30 - 0.1188)}{800}
$$

$$
y_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{700 \times 0.1812}{800} \;=\; \dfrac{126.84}{800} \;=\; 0.1586
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y_{1} \;=\; 0.1586 \;=\; 15.86 \,\text{wt \% acetic acid in extract}\,} \quad [\text{2 marks}]
$$

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q1(b)]** (14 marks)

**(a)** With the help of a **neat labelled sketch**, explain the working of a **two-stage cross-current** solvent extraction cascade. Derive the closed-form expression for the final raffinate composition $x_{2}$ when fresh solvent of composition $y_{S}$ is added to **each** stage.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A feed of **1200 kg/h** containing **25 wt %** solute A in diluent B is extracted with **pure solvent S** in a two-stage cross-current cascade. The flow of solvent per stage is **600 kg/h** and $K = y^{*}/x^{*} = 1.5$. Calculate **(i)** the composition of the raffinate leaving each stage, **(ii)** the percent extraction overall, and **(iii)** the composition of the **combined extract** leaving the cascade.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Sketch (description — refer to Section 4.3, single-stage block, repeated twice in parallel):**

- Two mixer–settler units in parallel.
- **Feed** ($L$, $x_{F}$) enters Stage 1; **fresh solvent** ($V$, $y_{S}$) enters Stage 1.
- Stage 1 produces raffinate ($L$, $x_{1}$) → fed to Stage 2; extract ($V$, $y_{1}$).
- **Another fresh solvent** charge ($V$, $y_{S}$) enters Stage 2.
- Stage 2 produces final raffinate ($L$, $x_{2}$) and extract ($V$, $y_{2}$).  **[2 marks for sketch + description]**

**Derivation:**

Stage 1 solute balance:

$$
L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S} = L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{1}, \quad y_{1} = K\,x_{1}
$$

Solving:

$$
x_{1} = \dfrac{L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S}}{L + K\,V} \quad [\text{1.5 marks}]
$$

Stage 2 solute balance (with **fresh** solvent):

$$
L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{S} = L\,x_{2} + V\,y_{2}, \quad y_{2} = K\,x_{2}
$$

Solving:

$$
x_{2} = \dfrac{L\,x_{1} + V\,y_{S}}{L + K\,V} \quad [\text{1.5 marks}]
$$

Substitute $x_{1}$:

$$
x_{2} = \dfrac{1}{L + K\,V}\left[\,L\!\left(\dfrac{L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S}}{L + K\,V}\right) + V\,y_{S}\,\right] = \dfrac{L^{2}\,x_{F} + 2\,L\,V\,y_{S} + K\,V^{2}\,y_{S}}{(L + K\,V)^{2}}
$$

Specialise to pure solvent $y_{S} = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\,x_{2} = \left(\dfrac{L}{L + K\,V}\right)^{2} x_{F}\,} \quad [\text{2 marks}]
$$

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

Given: $F = 1200$ kg/h, $x_{F} = 0.25$, $L = (1 - 0.25)\times 1200 = 900$ kg/h, $V = 600$ kg/h (per stage), $K = 1.5$, $y_{S} = 0$.

Compute the single-stage factor:

$$
\dfrac{L}{L + K\,V} \;=\; \dfrac{900}{900 + 1.5 \times 600} \;=\; \dfrac{900}{900 + 900} \;=\; \dfrac{900}{1800} \;=\; 0.5
$$

**(i) Raffinate compositions:**

Stage 1:

$$
x_{1} = 0.5 \times x_{F} = 0.5 \times 0.25 = 0.125 \quad [\text{1.5 marks}]
$$

Stage 2:

$$
x_{2} = 0.5 \times x_{1} = 0.5 \times 0.125 = 0.0625 \quad [\text{1.5 marks}]
$$

**(ii) Percent extraction overall:**

$$
\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{x_{2}}{x_{F}}\right)\times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{0.0625}{0.25}\right)\times 100 = (1 - 0.25)\times 100 = 75 \,\% \quad [\text{1.5 marks}]
$$

**(iii) Composition of the combined extract:**

Total solute recovered per hour:

$$
\Delta A = L\,(x_{F} - x_{2}) = 900 \times (0.25 - 0.0625) = 900 \times 0.1875 = 168.75 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Total solvent used:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 600 = 1200 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Combined extract composition (assuming ideal mixing of the two extract streams):

$$
y_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\Delta A}{V_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{168.75}{1200} = 0.1406 \quad [\text{2.5 marks}]
$$

(For reference, the individual extract compositions would be $y_{1} = K\,x_{1} = 1.5 \times 0.125 = 0.1875$ and $y_{2} = K\,x_{2} = 1.5 \times 0.0625 = 0.0938$; averaging these weighted by $V$ yields the same $y_{\text{avg}} = 0.1406$ — the result is self-consistent.)

$$
\boxed{\,y_{\text{avg}} = 0.1406 \;=\; 14.06 \,\text{wt \% solute in combined extract}\,}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in two-stage extraction problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $L$ with $F$.** $L$ is the *diluent* flow (carrier B alone), **not** the total feed. Always compute $L = (1 - x_{F})\times F$ when $x_{F}$ is a mass fraction. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the "pure solvent" assumption.** When the problem says "pure solvent" you must set $y_{S}=0$; if the problem gives $y_{S}>0$, *do not* use the simplified formula $x_{2} = x_{F}/(1 + A + A^{2})$. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing CC and CCC formulas.** Cross-current uses $x_{2} = (L/(L+KV))^{2}\,x_{F}$; counter-current uses $x_{2} = x_{F}/(1 + A + A^{2})$. These are **not** interchangeable. (–3 marks)
> 4. **Wrong flow direction labelling.** In counter-current the **fresh solvent** enters the *last* stage (Stage 2 here). The extract from Stage 2 returns to Stage 1. Many sketches reverse this. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Unit mismatch.** If $K$ is reported on a *mole-fraction* basis, do not plug it into a *mass-fraction* material balance. Convert all concentrations to the same basis first. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Stage efficiency.** If the problem gives Murphree efficiency $\mu < 1$, you must adjust the equilibrium exit composition: $y_{i,\,\text{actual}} = y_{i-1} + \mu\,(y_{i}^{*} - y_{i-1})$. The KTU formula $x_{2} = x_{F}/(1+A+A^{2})$ is for $\mu = 1$ only. (–2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for "Solvent Extraction in Two Stages":**

- [x] **Equilibrium law:** $y^{*} = K\,x^{*}$ — the foundation of every stage calculation.
- [x] **Single-stage formula:** $x_{1} = (L\,x_{F} + V\,y_{S})/(L + K\,V)$ — building block.
- [x] **Cross-current (CC) two-stage (pure solvent):** $x_{2} = \left(\dfrac{L}{L + K\,V}\right)^{2} x_{F}$.
- [x] **Counter-current (CCC) two-stage (pure solvent):** $x_{2} = \dfrac{x_{F}}{1 + A + A^{2}}$, where $A = K\,V/L$.
- [x] **Counter-current (CCC) two-stage (general $y_{S}$):** $x_{2} = \dfrac{L^{2}\,x_{F} + L\,V\,y_{S} + K\,V^{2}\,y_{S}}{L^{2} + L\,K\,V + K^{2}V^{2}}$.
- [x] **Kremser equation** (general N, CCC, $y_{S}=0$): $x_{N} = x_{F}\,(1 - A)/(1 - A^{N+1})$; set $N=2$ and you get the CCC formula above.
- [x] **Extraction efficiency:** $\eta = (1 - x_{N}/x_{F})\times 100 \,\%$.
- [x] **Flow direction matters:** in CCC, fresh solvent meets the *most-depleted* raffinate; in CC, each raffinate meets *fresh* solvent.
- [x] **CCC > CC for the same $V$:** counter-current gives lower $x_{2}$ and higher $\eta$ — always choose CCC when extraction is the goal.
- [x] **Limiting cases:** (i) $K\,V/L \to \infty \Rightarrow x_{N} \to 0$ (perfect extraction); (ii) $K\,V/L \to 0 \Rightarrow x_{N} \to x_{F}$ (no extraction); (iii) $A = 1 \Rightarrow x_{2} = x_{F}/3$ (symmetric CCC).
- [x] **Diluent flow $L$ is *not* the feed flow $F$** when $x_{F}$ is non-zero: $L = (1 - x_{F})\,F$.
- [x] **Always label flows** ($L$, $V$) and compositions ($x$, $y$) **clearly** on the sketch — KTU examiners award 1 mark for a correct, well-labelled diagram.
- [x] **Memorise the Kremser forms** for CC and CCC — they are the most-tested identities in Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion with Chemical Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Diffusion with Chemical Reaction # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Diffusion with Chemical Reaction** is the study of mass transfer processes in which molecular diffusion of one or more chemical species occurs simultaneously with a chemical transformation. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Process Engineering, this forms the backbone of modelling **reactive absorption**, **catalytic reactors**, **combustion systems**, and **membrane separation with reaction**. Mathematically, it is described by the **convective-diffusion-reaction equation**: $$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A = R_A$$ where $R_A$ is the rate of generation (or consumption) of species A per unit volume due to chemical reaction, expressed in **mol/(m³·s)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PECHT525 Module 1** > The 2024 scheme explicitly emphasizes *steady-state and unsteady-state diffusion with homogeneous (bulk) and heterogeneous (surface) chemical reactions of various orders*. Mastery of the **Enhancement Factor ($E$)** and **Hatta Number ($Ha$)** is mandatory for ESE. ## 1.2 Classification of Diffusion-Reaction Systems | Class | Type | Reaction Location | Typical Example | |-------|------|-------------------|-----------------| | **Homogeneous** | Bulk phase | Throughout the fluid | Gas absorption with reaction in liquid film | | **Heterogeneous** | Interface / Catalyst surface | Only at boundary | Reaction on solid catalyst pellet | | **Pseudo-homogeneous** | Apparent bulk | Uniformly distributed catalyst | Fluidized bed reactor | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge and Honey" Model Imagine dipping a dry sponge (a porous solid catalyst) into a beaker of honey (fluid containing reactant A). Two things happen simultaneously: 1. **Diffusion** — honey molecules crawl into the sponge pores due to a concentration gradient. 2. **Reaction** — once inside, the molecules react with the sponge material and get consumed. Now ask: *Will the concentration of A deep inside the sponge be zero, finite, or negative?* The answer depends entirely on the **relative speed of diffusion versus reaction**. This ratio is precisely what the **Hatta Number** quantifies. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants of Interest (Standard SI Units)** > - Diffusion Coefficient $D_{AB}$: typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-9}$ **m²/s** > - First-order rate constant $k$: $10^{-6}$ to $10^{2}$ **s⁻¹** > - Hatta Number $Ha$: dimensionless, $0.01$ to $100$ > - Enhancement Factor $E$: dimensionless, $\geq 1$ ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Every industrial separation involving a chemical transformation — from **scrubbing CO₂ with amine solutions** to **catalytic cracking in petroleum refining** — relies on this theory. Without it, engineers cannot predict reactor yield, design absorption columns, or optimize catalyst pellet sizes. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Governing Differential Equation — Step Logic For a binary system (A diffusing through stagnant B) with a **first-order homogeneous reaction** ($A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, rate $= k C_A$): 1. **Fick's First Law** states: $\vec{N}_A = -D_{AB} \nabla C_A + x_A (\vec{N}_A + \vec{N}_B)$. 2. For **equimolar counter-diffusion** (EMCD), $\vec{N}_A + \vec{N}_B = 0$, simplifying to $\vec{N}_A = -D_{AB} \nabla C_A$. 3. Apply a **steady-state molar balance** on a differential shell: $\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = 0$. 4. For a slab geometry (1D, area $S$, thickness $dx$), this gives: $$\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - \frac{k C_A}{D_{AB}} = 0$$ 5. For an **$n$-th order reaction**, replace $k C_A$ with $k C_A^n$. The equation becomes **non-linear** and generally requires numerical methods. ## 2.2 Boundary Conditions — The Three Canonical Cases | Case | BC at $x=0$ (Bulk) | BC at $x=\delta$ (Interface) | Physical Meaning | |------|---------------------|------------------------------|------------------| | **I** | $C_A = C_{A0}$ | $C_A = C_{Ai}$ | Finite concentrations at both ends | | **II** | $C_A = C_{A0}$ | $-D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dx}\bigg\vert_\delta = k_s C_{Ai}$ | Surface reaction of 1st order | | **III** | $C_A = C_{A0}$ | $C_A = 0$ | Instantaneous consumption (e.g. perfect catalyst) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | When to Use | |---|---------------|-------------------|-------------| | 1 | General Reactive Diffusion (1D, SS) | $D_{AB}\dfrac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - R_A(C_A) = 0$ | Any 1D reactive diffusion | | 2 | First-Order Form | $\dfrac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - \phi^2 C_A = 0$, where $\phi^2 = k/D_{AB}$ | First-order homogeneous reaction | | 3 | Hatta Number (Film Theory) | $Ha = \dfrac{\sqrt{k\,D_{AB}}}{k_L} = \delta \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_{AB}}}$ | Quantifies reaction vs diffusion | | 4 | Enhancement Factor (General) | $E = \dfrac{\text{Rate with reaction}}{\text{Rate without reaction}}$ | Always defined $\geq 1$ | | 5 | Enhancement Factor (1st order, $C_{A0}=0$) | $E = \dfrac{Ha}{\tanh(Ha)}$ | Pure reaction-diffusion | | 6 | Enhancement Factor (1st order, $C_{Ai}=0$) | $E = Ha$ | Very fast reaction regime | | 7 | Thiele Modulus (Catalyst Pellet) | $\phi_p = L \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_{eff}}}$ | Heterogeneous catalyst design | | 8 | Internal Effectiveness Factor | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(\phi_p)}{\phi_p}$ (slab, 1st order) | Catalyst pore diffusion | | 9 | Mears Criterion (Negligible External Diffusion) | $\dfrac{-r_{A,obs} \rho_b \, R\, n}{k_c \, C_{Ab}} < 0.15$ | Mears criterion check | | 10 | Weisz-Prater Criterion (Negligible Internal Diffusion) | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{-r_{A,obs} \rho_c \, L^2}{D_{eff} \, C_{As}} < 1$ | Internal diffusion check | > [!IMPORTANT] > **NOTE on Hatta Number Regimes (Most Tested in KTU):** > - $Ha < 0.3$ → **Slow reaction**, $E \approx 1 + Ha^2/3$ > - $0.3 < Ha < 3$ → **Moderate reaction**, $E \approx Ha/\tanh(Ha)$ > - $Ha > 3$ → **Fast reaction**, $E \approx Ha$ > - $Ha > 10$ → **Instantaneous reaction** (gas-film controlled) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | |----------|-------------| | Environmental | SO₂ scrubbing in limestone slurry | | Petrochemical | Catalytic reforming of naphtha | | Pharmaceutical | Reactive crystallization | | Food Processing | CO₂ absorption in carbonated beverages | | Biotechnology | Oxygen transfer in aerobic fermenters | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Steady-State Diffusion with First-Order Homogeneous Reaction in a Stagnant Film **System:** Species A diffuses through stagnant B over a film of thickness $\delta$, reacting homogeneously as $A \rightarrow P$ with rate $-r_A = k C_A$. **Step 1 — Set up the differential molar balance.** Take a differential slice $dx$ of the film. At steady state: $$\text{Rate In} - \text{Rate Out} + \text{Rate of Generation} = 0$$ $$\left. N_{A,x} S \right\vert_x - \left. N_{A,x} S \right\vert_{x+dx} + (-r_A)\, S\, dx = 0$$ **Step 2 — Express flux by Fick's Law (Stagnant B, dilute system).** $$N_{A,x} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and divide by $S\,dx$.** $$\begin{aligned} -\frac{d}{dx}\left(-D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dx}\right) - k\,C_A &= 0 \\ D_{AB}\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - k\,C_A &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Define the reaction-diffusion modulus $\phi$.** Let $\phi^2 = \dfrac{k}{D_{AB}}$, so the equation becomes: $$\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - \phi^2 C_A = 0$$ **Step 5 — Solve the linear ODE.** The general solution is: $$C_A(x) = C_1 \cosh(\phi x) + C_2 \sinh(\phi x)$$ **Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions.** - BC1: At $x=0$ (bulk), $C_A = C_{A0}$ → $C_1 = C_{A0}$. - BC2: At $x=\delta$ (interface), $C_A = C_{Ai}$. $$C_{Ai} = C_{A0}\cosh(\phi\delta) + C_2\sinh(\phi\delta)$$ $$C_2 = \frac{C_{Ai} - C_{A0}\cosh(\phi\delta)}{\sinh(\phi\delta)}$$ **Step 7 — Complete concentration profile.** $$C_A(x) = C_{A0}\cosh(\phi x) + \left[\frac{C_{Ai} - C_{A0}\cosh(\phi\delta)}{\sinh(\phi\delta)}\right]\sinh(\phi x)$$ **Step 8 — Compute the molar flux at the interface $x = \delta$.** $$N_A\big\vert_\delta = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dx}\bigg\vert_\delta$$ $$\frac{dC_A}{dx} = C_{A0}\phi\sinh(\phi x) + C_2\phi\cosh(\phi x)$$ At $x = \delta$: $$\begin{aligned} N_A\big\vert_\delta &= -D_{AB}\phi \left[C_{A0}\sinh(\phi\delta) + C_2\cosh(\phi\delta)\right] \\ &= -D_{AB}\phi \left[C_{A0}\sinh(\phi\delta) + \frac{C_{Ai} - C_{A0}\cosh(\phi\delta)}{\sinh(\phi\delta)}\cdot\cosh(\phi\delta)\right] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 9 — Simplify.** $$\begin{aligned} N_A\big\vert_\delta &= -D_{AB}\phi \cdot \frac{C_{A0}\sinh^2(\phi\delta) + C_{Ai}\cosh(\phi\delta) - C_{A0}\cosh^2(\phi\delta)}{\sinh(\phi\delta)} \\ &= \frac{D_{AB}\phi}{\sinh(\phi\delta)} \left[C_{A0}\cosh^2(\phi\delta) - C_{A0}\sinh^2(\phi\delta) - C_{Ai}\cosh(\phi\delta)\right] \\ &= \frac{D_{AB}\phi}{\sinh(\phi\delta)} \left[C_{A0} - C_{Ai}\cosh(\phi\delta)\right] \end{aligned}$$ using $\cosh^2 - \sinh^2 = 1$. **Step 10 — Express flux without reaction ($k = 0$, $\phi = 0$).** By L'Hôpital or Taylor expansion: $$N_{A,\text{no rxn}} = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}(C_{A0} - C_{Ai})$$ **Step 11 — Enhancement Factor.** $$E = \frac{N_A\big\vert_\delta}{N_{A,\text{no rxn}}} = \frac{\phi\delta\,[C_{A0} - C_{Ai}\cosh(\phi\delta)]}{(C_{A0} - C_{Ai})\sinh(\phi\delta)}$$ **Step 12 — Special Case: $C_{Ai} = 0$ (perfect sink at interface).** $$E = \frac{\phi\delta \cosh(\phi\delta)}{\sinh(\phi\delta)} = \frac{Ha}{\tanh(Ha)}$$ where $Ha = \phi\delta = \delta\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$. ## 3.2 Derivation — Internal Effectiveness Factor for a Slab Catalyst **Step 1 — Mass balance on a porous catalyst slab of half-thickness $L$.** $$D_{eff}\frac{d^2 C_A}{dz^2} - k C_A = 0$$ **Step 2 — BCs:** - At $z = L$: $C_A = C_{As}$ (surface) - At $z = 0$: $\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = 0$ (symmetry) **Step 3 — Define Thiele Modulus $\phi_p = L\sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$.** **Step 4 — Solve ODE.** $$C_A(z) = C_{As}\frac{\cosh(\phi_p z/L)}{\cosh(\phi_p)}$$ **Step 5 — Rate of reaction per unit volume.** $$-r_{A,\text{actual}} = k C_A(z)$$ **Step 6 — Total rate (integrate over slab volume):** $$\mathcal{R}_{\text{actual}} = S\int_0^L k C_A(z)\, dz = S\,k\,C_{As}\,\frac{\tanh(\phi_p)}{\phi_p}$$ **Step 7 — Rate if no internal diffusion limitation:** $$\mathcal{R}_{\text{ideal}} = S\,L\,k\,C_{As}$$ **Step 8 — Effectiveness Factor:** $$\eta = \frac{\mathcal{R}_{\text{actual}}}{\mathcal{R}_{\text{ideal}}} = \frac{\tanh(\phi_p)}{\phi_p}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** For $\phi_p < 1$, $\eta \to 1$ (reaction-limited). For $\phi_p > 3$, $\eta \approx 1/\phi_p$ (diffusion-limited). The crossover point is $\phi_p \approx 1$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concentration Profile Through a Reactive Film ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FILM[Reactive Stagnant Film Thickness delta] direction LR A0["Bulk Gas
CA = CA0
x = 0"] -->|"Diffusion +
1st order rxn"| AMID["Mid Film
CA = CA_mid
x = delta/2"] AMID -->|"Surface rxn
boundary"| AI["Interface
CA = CAi
x = delta"] end A0 -. "Ficks law flux NA" .-> AI style FILM fill:#e8f4fd,stroke:#1f77b4,stroke-width:2px style A0 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff7f0e style AI fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#2ca02c ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Reactive Mass Transfer Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IN[Inputs] BULK["Bulk Concentration CA0"] KCONST["Rate Constant k"] DIFF["Diffusivity DAB"] end subgraph CORE[Reactive Diffusion Core] ODE["2nd Order ODE
DAB d2CA/dx2 = k CA"] BC1["BC1: CA = CA0 at x = 0"] BC2["BC2: CA = CAi at x = delta"] SOLVE["Analytical Solver
CA = C1 cosh phix + C2 sinh phix"] FLUX["Compute Flux NA at Interface"] HATTA["Hatta Number Ha = phi times delta"] ENH["Enhancement Factor E = Ha / tanh Ha"] EFF["Effectiveness Factor eta = tanh phi_p / phi_p"] end subgraph OUT[Outputs / Decisions] NA_OUT["Molar Flux NA"] E_OUT["Enhancement Factor E"] REGIME_OUT["Reaction Regime:
Slow / Moderate / Fast"] DESIGN_OUT["Reactor Design
Recommendations"] end BULK --> BC1 KCONST --> ODE DIFF --> ODE ODE --> SOLVE BC1 --> SOLVE BC2 --> SOLVE SOLVE --> FLUX FLUX --> NA_OUT KCONST --> HATTA DIFF --> HATTA HATTA --> ENH ENH --> E_OUT ENH --> REGIME_OUT KCONST --> EFF DIFF --> EFF EFF --> DESIGN_OUT style IN fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#daa520 style CORE fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00838f style OUT fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#558b2f ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Identifying the Reaction Regime ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Hatta Number Ha"} Q1 -->|"Ha less than 0.3"| R1["SLOW REACTION
Bulk phase dominant
E approx 1 + Ha2/3"] Q1 -->|"0.3 less than Ha less than 3"| R2["MODERATE REACTION
Film + bulk both matter
E = Ha / tanh Ha"] Q1 -->|"Ha greater than 3"| R3["FAST REACTION
All A consumed in film
E approx Ha"] Q1 -->|"Ha greater than 10"| R4["INSTANTANEOUS
Gas film controls
Special analysis"] R1 --> OUTCOME[Choose design based on regime] R2 --> OUTCOME R3 --> OUTCOME R4 --> OUTCOME style Q1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0 style R1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 style R2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#ef6c00 style R3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style R4 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a ``` ## 4.4 Slab Catalyst Effectiveness Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SLAB[Porous Catalyst Slab Half Thickness L] L0["Center z=0
dCA/dz = 0"] --> L1["Mid z=L/2
CA decreasing"] L1 --> L2["Surface z=L
CA = CAs"] end L0 -. "Internal diffusion D_eff" .-> L2 L2 --> EXT["External Bulk
CA = CA_bulk"] EXT -. "External mass transfer k_c" .-> L2 style SLAB fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style EXT fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the Hatta Number ($Ha$) and explain its physical significance in gas-liquid systems with chemical reaction.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Hatta Number** is a dimensionless modulus that compares the rate of chemical reaction to the rate of diffusion through a liquid film: $$Ha = \frac{\sqrt{k\, D_{AB}}}{k_L} = \delta\sqrt{\frac{k}{D_{AB}}}$$ **Physical Significance:** - When $Ha \ll 1$: reaction is **slow**, occurs mainly in the bulk liquid; absorption rate is not enhanced much. - When $Ha \approx 1$: reaction is **comparable to diffusion**; enhancement is moderate. - When $Ha \gg 1$: reaction is **fast**, mostly completed inside the film; absorption rate is significantly enhanced. The Hatta number is the key parameter used to identify the **reaction regime** and select the appropriate enhancement factor formula. (3 marks — *1 for definition + 1 for formula + 1 for regime significance*) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the Enhancement Factor ($E$)? Give its expression for a first-order reaction when the gas-phase concentration at the interface is negligible.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Enhancement Factor** is defined as the ratio of the rate of absorption with chemical reaction to that without reaction at identical driving force: $$E = \frac{N_A^{\text{with rxn}}}{N_A^{\text{without rxn}}} \geq 1$$ For a first-order reaction with $C_{Ai} = 0$: $$E = \frac{Ha}{\tanh(Ha)}$$ For very fast reaction ($Ha > 3$), $E \approx Ha$. (3 marks — *1 for definition + 1 for formula + 1 for limiting case*) --- ## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the differential equation for steady-state one-dimensional diffusion of species A through stagnant B with a first-order homogeneous reaction. State the assumptions clearly. **(b) [7 Marks]** Solve the equation for a film of thickness $\delta$ with boundary conditions $C_A = C_{A0}$ at $x=0$ and $C_A = C_{Ai}$ at $x=\delta$. Hence obtain the expression for the enhancement factor. ### ✅ Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Steady-state, isothermal, isobaric system. 2. Dilute solution; $C_A \ll C_B$, so $x_A \approx 0$. 3. Fick's law valid; constant $D_{AB}$. 4. First-order irreversible reaction: $-r_A = k C_A$. 5. 1D slab geometry. **Molar balance on a slice of thickness $dx$ and area $S$ (2 marks):** $$\left. N_A S \right\vert_x - \left. N_A S \right\vert_{x+dx} + (-r_A)S\,dx = 0$$ $$-\frac{dN_A}{dx} = k C_A$$ **Apply Fick's law for stagnant B (1 mark):** $$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dx}$$ **Substitute (1 mark):** $$\boxed{D_{AB}\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - k C_A = 0}$$ **Defining $\phi^2 = k/D_{AB}$ (1 mark):** $$\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - \phi^2 C_A = 0$$ **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — General solution (1 mark):** $$C_A(x) = C_1 \cosh(\phi x) + C_2 \sinh(\phi x)$$ **Step 2 — Apply BCs (2 marks):** - BC1 ($x=0$): $C_{A0} = C_1$ - BC2 ($x=\delta$): $C_{Ai} = C_{A0}\cosh(\phi\delta) + C_2 \sinh(\phi\delta)$ → $C_2 = \dfrac{C_{Ai} - C_{A0}\cosh(\phi\delta)}{\sinh(\phi\delta)}$ **Step 3 — Compute flux at $x=\delta$ (2 marks):** $$N_A\big\vert_\delta = -D_{AB}\phi \cdot \frac{C_{A0} - C_{Ai}\cosh(\phi\delta)}{\sinh(\phi\delta)}$$ **Step 4 — Without reaction ($k=0$) (1 mark):** $$N_{A,\text{no rxn}} = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}(C_{A0} - C_{Ai})$$ **Step 5 — Enhancement factor (1 mark):** $$\boxed{E = \frac{N_A}{N_{A,\text{no rxn}}} = \frac{\phi\delta\,[C_{A0} - C_{Ai}\cosh(\phi\delta)]}{(C_{A0} - C_{Ai})\sinh(\phi\delta)} = \frac{Ha\,[C_{A0} - C_{Ai}\cosh(Ha)]}{(C_{A0} - C_{Ai})\sinh(Ha)}}$$ --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a) [7 Marks]** What is the Thiele modulus? Derive the expression for the internal effectiveness factor for a first-order reaction occurring in a porous slab catalyst. **(b) [7 Marks]** Using the Mears and Weisz-Prater criteria, explain how to determine whether external or internal mass transfer limitations are significant in heterogeneous catalytic reactions. ### ✅ Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **Definition (1 mark):** The **Thiele Modulus** $\phi_p$ is the ratio of the characteristic reaction rate to the characteristic diffusion rate inside a catalyst pellet: $$\phi_p = L\sqrt{\frac{k}{D_{eff}}}$$ **Mass balance on catalyst slab (2 marks):** At steady state, the rate of A arriving by diffusion equals the rate of consumption by reaction: $$D_{eff}\frac{d^2 C_A}{dz^2} - k C_A = 0$$ **BCs (1 mark):** - $z = L$: $C_A = C_{As}$ - $z = 0$: $\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = 0$ (symmetry) **Solution and effectiveness factor derivation (3 marks):** Solving yields $C_A(z) = C_{As}\dfrac{\cosh(\phi_p z/L)}{\cosh(\phi_p)}$. Integrating the actual reaction rate over the slab and dividing by the ideal rate (no diffusion limitation): $$\eta = \frac{\tanh(\phi_p)}{\phi_p}$$ **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Mears Criterion — External Diffusion (3 marks):** $$\frac{(-r_{A,\text{obs}})\rho_b R n}{k_c C_{Ab}} < 0.15$$ If satisfied, **external** mass transfer resistance is negligible. **Weisz-Prater Criterion — Internal Diffusion (3 marks):** $$C_{WP} = \frac{(-r_{A,\text{obs}})\rho_c L^2}{D_{eff} C_{As}} < 1$$ If $C_{WP} \ll 1$, internal diffusion limitations are absent. Values much greater than 1 indicate strong pore-diffusion limitations. **Engineering implication (1 mark):** Use these to decide whether intrinsic kinetics can be extracted from experimental data and to optimize pellet size. --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the assumption $C_{A0} \ll C_B$** when using Fick's law for stagnant B. (-1 mark) > 2. **Misapplying BCs** — students often swap $C_{A0}$ and $C_{Ai}$. Remember: $x=0$ is bulk, $x=\delta$ is interface. (-1 mark) > 3. **Dropping the $\sinh$ term** during simplification of the flux — algebraic carelessness. (-2 marks) > 4. **Using $E = Ha$** for *all* values of $Ha$ — that is only valid for $Ha > 3$. Always specify regime! (-1 mark) > 5. **Confusing Thiele Modulus with Hatta Number** — both involve $\sqrt{k/D}$ but Thiele applies to **internal catalyst pores**, Hatta to **liquid film absorption**. (-1 mark) --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - ✅ The governing equation for 1D steady diffusion with first-order reaction is $D_{AB}\dfrac{d^2C_A}{dx^2} - kC_A = 0$. - ✅ **Hatta Number** $Ha = \delta\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$ is dimensionless and determines the **reaction regime**. - ✅ **Enhancement Factor** $E \geq 1$ quantifies the benefit of reaction on mass transfer. - ✅ For first-order reaction: $E = \dfrac{Ha}{\tanh(Ha)}$ (general); $E \approx Ha$ (fast regime); $E \approx 1$ (slow regime). - ✅ **Thiele Modulus** $\phi_p = L\sqrt{k/D_{eff}}$ is for heterogeneous catalyst design. - ✅ **Effectiveness factor** $\eta = \tanh(\phi_p)/\phi_p$ — key for catalyst pore optimization. - ✅ **Mears Criterion** ($<0.15$) → no external diffusion limitation. - ✅ **Weisz-Prater Criterion** ($<1$) → no internal diffusion limitation. - ✅ Always **state assumptions** clearly: steady-state, isothermal, dilute, 1D, constant $D_{AB}$. - ✅ Always **draw concentration profiles** in exams — they fetch easy marks. - ✅ **Numerical safety**: $\tanh(Ha) \to 1$ as $Ha \to \infty$; $\tanh(Ha) \approx Ha - Ha^3/3$ for $Ha \ll 1$.

topics.title: Application of the Law of Conservation of Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of the Law of Conservation of Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — APPLICATION OF THE LAW OF CONSERVATION OF ENERGY

## 1. Core Technical Definition

The **Law of Conservation of Energy**, formally expressed as the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, states that *energy can neither be created nor destroyed in a process; it can only be transformed from one form to another or transferred across the system boundary*. In process engineering, this principle is operationalized through the **General Energy Balance** applied to a defined control volume (CV):

$$
\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} \;=\; \dot{Q} \;-\; \dot{W} \;+\; \sum_{\text{in}} \dot{m}_{i}\!\left(H_{i}+\tfrac{v_{i}^{2}}{2}+gz_{i}\right) \;-\; \sum_{\text{out}} \dot{m}_{e}\!\left(H_{e}+\tfrac{v_{e}^{2}}{2}+gz_{e}\right)
$$

where $E_{CV}$ is the total energy contained within the system, $\dot{Q}$ is the rate of heat transfer **into** the system, $\dot{W}$ is the rate of work done **by** the system (excluding flow work), and the summations account for the enthalpy, kinetic energy, and potential energy carried by material streams crossing the boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PECHT525, mastery of the energy balance is foundational. The lecturer's primary focus will be on the **mathematical formulation**, identification of boundary terms, simplification under standard assumptions (steady-state, adiabatic, negligible KE/PE), and systematic algebraic solution for the unknown — typically outlet temperature, heat duty, or mass flow rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions Used Throughout KTU Examinations**
> - **Temperature Reference:** $T_{ref} = 25^{\circ}C = 298.15\,K$
> - **Pressure Reference:** $P_{ref} = 1\,atm = 101.325\,kPa$
> - **Enthalpy of Formation Reference:** Pure elements in their stable state at 25 °C have $\Delta H_{f}^{\circ} = 0$
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\,J/(mol\cdot K)$
> - **Standard Acceleration of Gravity:** $g = 9.81\,m/s^{2}$

---

## 2. Conceptual Analogy — The "Process Bank Account"

Imagine a chemical plant as a **bank account** dedicated solely to energy:

| Bank Concept | Process Engineering Equivalent |
|--------------|-------------------------------|
| Opening Balance | Internal Energy stored in the equipment & contents |
| Cash Deposits | Enthalpy carried in by feed streams, heat supplied by utilities |
| Cash Withdrawals | Enthalpy carried out by product streams, work done, heat lost |
| Closing Balance | Final internal energy in the system |

Just as a bank reconciliation must always balance, the **Total Energy In = Total Energy Out + Accumulation + Generation – Consumption**. A successful process engineer is, in essence, a "financial auditor" of energy currency — joules, not rupees.

### 2.1 The Three Modes of Energy Crossing a Boundary

1. **Heat ($\dot{Q}$)** — energy transfer driven by a temperature gradient (conduction, convection, radiation).
2. **Work ($\dot{W}$)** — energy transfer driven by a generalized force (shaft work from agitators/compressors, electrical work, boundary work).
3. **Energy carried by mass flow** — the enthalpy, kinetic, and potential energy of every stream entering or leaving.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love to test whether students can correctly classify energy as *crossing* the boundary versus *contained within* the boundary. A common trap: students add the internal energy of inlet streams to the balance. The internal energy of streams crossing the boundary is embedded within their **enthalpy** $H = U + PV$, which already includes the flow-work component required to push the fluid into the control volume.

---

## 3. Why Energy Balances Are Indispensable in Process Engineering

A working chemical engineer must perform energy balances to:

- **Size utility systems** (e.g., the steam header, cooling-tower capacity, refrigerant load).
- **Determine reactor heat duty** (heat removal in exothermic polymerizations, heat supply in endothermic reforming).
- **Design heat-exchanger networks** (pinch analysis, minimum utility targeting).
- **Evaluate adiabatic flame temperature** in furnace and incinerator design.
- **Estimate product temperature** in mixers, blenders, and three-phase reactors.
- **Perform safety analysis** — runaway reactions, relief-valve sizing for thermal emergencies.

Mastery of the energy balance is therefore a prerequisite for downstream courses such as **Heat Transfer, Mass Transfer, Reaction Engineering, Process Control, and Plant Design**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 1. The Energy Balance — Systematic Reduction

The general energy balance is rarely used in its full form. Process problems are almost always simplified by invoking the **KTU Standard Assumptions**:

### 1.1 The Hierarchy of Simplification

| # | Assumption | Mathematical Consequence | Typical Application |
|---|------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Steady State** | $\dfrac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0$ | Continuous reactors, heat exchangers, distillation columns |
| 2 | **No Shaft Work** | $\dot{W}_{s} = 0$ | Heat exchangers, mixers, storage tanks |
| 3 | **Negligible KE & PE** | $\tfrac{v^{2}}{2}\approx 0,\; gz\approx 0$ | Process vessels of moderate size and velocity |
| 4 | **Single Inlet / Outlet** | Mass flow balance: $\dot{m}_{i}=\dot{m}_{e}=\dot{m}$ | Tubular reactors, pipe heat exchangers |
| 5 | **Adiabatic** | $\dot{Q}=0$ | Insulation-bound units, rapid mixing |
| 6 | **Constant $C_{p}$** | $\int C_{p}\,dT = C_{p}\,\Delta T$ | Small temperature ranges, ideal gases |
| 7 | **No Reaction** | $\Delta H_{rxn}=0$ | Physical separators, mixers |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme Part-B problems, a 14-mark question typically requires the student to *explicitly state* at least 4 of these assumptions in the solution. Examiners award 1 mark for each correctly justified assumption.

### 1.2 The Most-Used Working Form

Applying assumptions 1, 2, 3, and 4 simultaneously, the energy balance collapses to the **canonical heat-balance form**:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \sum_{\text{out}} \dot{m}_{e}\,H_{e} \;-\; \sum_{\text{in}} \dot{m}_{i}\,H_{i}
$$

This is the equation you should be able to write down from memory in **under 30 seconds** during an exam.

---

## 2. KTU Formula Sheet — Enthalpy, Heat Capacities, and Heat Effects

The following table consolidates every formula a PECHT525 student must commit to memory. **All enthalpy units are in kJ, all mass units in kg, all temperatures in °C or K** (be consistent!).

| # | Equation | Description | SI Units | When to Use |
|---|----------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | $H \;=\; U + PV$ | Definition of **enthalpy** | kJ | All flow processes |
| 2 | $\dot{Q} = \sum\dot{m}_{e}H_{e} - \sum\dot{m}_{i}H_{i}$ | Steady-state energy balance (no work, no KE/PE) | kW | Continuous flow units |
| 3 | $Q = m\,C_{p}\,\Delta T$ | **Sensible heat** — temperature change without phase change | kJ | Heating/cooling single-phase fluids |
| 4 | $Q = m\,\lambda$ | **Latent heat** — phase change at constant temperature | kJ | Boiling, condensation, melting |
| 5 | $H(T) = \Delta H_{f}^{\circ} + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} C_{p}\,dT$ | Absolute enthalpy of a pure species | kJ/mol | Reactive & non-reactive flows |
| 6 | $Q_{rxn} = -\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ}$ | Heat released ($-$ = exothermic) or absorbed ($+$ = endothermic) | kJ | Reactors, combustion |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = \sum\nu_{p}\Delta H_{f,p}^{\circ} - \sum\nu_{r}\Delta H_{f,r}^{\circ}$ | **Hess's Law** for standard heat of reaction | kJ/mol | Calculating heat duties of chemical reactors |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{combustion} = -\Delta H_{c}^{\circ}$ | Heat released from complete combustion of a fuel | kJ/mol | Furnaces, boilers, gas turbines |
| 9 | $C_{p,mix} = \sum y_{i}\,C_{p,i}$ | Heat capacity of an ideal gas mixture (mole basis) | kJ/(kmol·K) | Flue-gas calculations |
| 10 | $\dot{W}_{s} = \dot{m}\,\Delta H_{isentropic}$ | Shaft work for compressors, turbines, pumps | kW | Compressor design, power generation |
| 11 | $H_{vap}(T) = H_{f}(T) + \lambda(T)$ | Enthalpy of saturated vapor | kJ/kg | Steam tables, condensers |
| 12 | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | **Conservation of Mass** (always paired with energy balance) | kg/s | All process units |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency Pitfall:** A 14-mark problem in KTU 2024 will frequently mix kJ, kcal, Btu, °C, K, and atm within the same calculation. Always perform a unit audit *before* you start computing. Define units at the top of your solution and stick to them. Marks are routinely deducted for the silent conversion of, e.g., kJ/kmol·°C vs kJ/kg·K.

---

## 3. Real-World Industrial Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Energy Balance |
|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Petrochemical Refining** | Heat-integrated crude distillation, where the overhead condenser reboils the side-stripper |
| **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors** | Jacket-cooling duty for exothermic steps (e.g., Grignard reactions) |
| **Cryogenic Air Separation** | Multistage compressor-intercooler duty and Joule-Thomson expansion cooling |
| **Cement & Lime Kilns** | Counter-current gas–solid energy balance for calcination |
| **Food Processing** | Pasteurizer plate heat-exchanger steam economy |
| **Power Generation** | Boiler heat duty, reheat cycles, Rankine-cycle efficiency analysis |
| **Polymerization Industry** | Heat-removal in bulk and suspension PVC reactors (autoclave scale, MW-scale) |

---

## 4. The Energy Forms — A Conceptual Map

Before any calculation, a student must identify *which forms of energy* are present in the system. The following taxonomy should be applied to every inlet, outlet, and storage term.

$$
E_{total} \;=\; U \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2} \;+\; mgz
$$

where $U$ is the **internal energy** — a microscopic store comprising:
- *Translational* energy of molecules
- *Rotational* energy
- *Vibrational* energy
- *Intermolecular potential* energy
- *Chemical bond* energy
- *Nuclear* energy (neglected in process engineering)

In flow processes, $U$ is replaced by $H = U + PV$ because the flow work $PV$ is the work required to *push* the fluid into the control volume.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 1. Derivation of the General Energy Balance from the First Law

We begin from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a closed system:

$$
\Delta U_{system} \;=\; Q - W
$$

### 1.1 Step 1 — Convert to an Open System (Control Volume)

To analyze a continuous-flow process, we transform the closed-system equation into a control-volume (open-system) form. The Reynolds Transport Theorem relates the rate of change of an *extensive* property of the system to the control volume:

$$
\frac{dN_{CV}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{dN_{system}}{dt} \;+\; \sum_{\text{out}}\dot{n}_{e}\,N_{e} \;-\; \sum_{\text{in}}\dot{n}_{i}\,N_{i}
$$

where $N$ is the extensive property (per mole) and $\dot{n}$ is the molar flow rate. Applying this to total energy $E$:

$$
\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} \;=\; \dot{Q} - \dot{W} \;+\; \sum_{\text{in}}\dot{m}_{i}\!\left(H_{i}+\tfrac{v_{i}^{2}}{2}+gz_{i}\right) \;-\; \sum_{\text{out}}\dot{m}_{e}\!\left(H_{e}+\tfrac{v_{e}^{2}}{2}+gz_{e}\right)
$$

### 1.2 Step 2 — Enthalpy Arises from the Flow-Work Term

The internal-energy flow rate $\dot{m}\,u$ must be augmented by the **flow work** $P\,\dot{V} = P\,\dot{m}/\rho$ that the upstream fluid does to push downstream fluid into the control volume. Thus:

$$
\dot{m}\!\left(u + \frac{P}{\rho}\right) \;=\; \dot{m}\,h \quad\text{where}\quad h \;=\; u + \frac{P}{\rho} \;=\; u + Pv
$$

This is the formal origin of the **specific enthalpy** $h$ used in flow-energy balances.

### 1.3 Step 3 — Steady-State Simplification

For a continuous process operating at steady state, $dE_{CV}/dt = 0$. The general equation reduces to:

$$
0 \;=\; \dot{Q} - \dot{W} \;+\; \sum_{\text{in}}\dot{m}_{i}\!\left(H_{i}+\tfrac{v_{i}^{2}}{2}+gz_{i}\right) \;-\; \sum_{\text{out}}\dot{m}_{e}\!\left(H_{e}+\tfrac{v_{e}^{2}}{2}+gz_{e}\right)
$$

### 1.4 Step 4 — The KTU "Working Form"

Neglecting shaft work, kinetic energy, and potential energy (the standard KTU assumption for chemical reactors and heat exchangers of moderate size):

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \sum_{\text{out}}\dot{m}_{e}\,H_{e} \;-\; \sum_{\text{in}}\dot{m}_{i}\,H_{i}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** "**Q equals H-out minus H-in**." This single sentence is worth 14 marks in a KTU Part-B question if you remember to: (i) write the *sign* correctly, (ii) include *all* streams, and (iii) account for reaction enthalpy if applicable.

---

## 2. Worked Example 1 — Counterflow Heat Exchanger (Non-Reactive)

**Problem Statement** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A counterflow shell-and-tube heat exchanger heats **2.5 kg/s of water** from **30 °C to 80 °C** using an oil stream. The oil enters at **150 °C** and leaves at **80 °C**. The mean specific heat of the oil is **2.0 kJ/(kg·K)**. The mean specific heat of water is **4.18 kJ/(kg·K)**. The exchanger is well-insulated, with negligible heat loss. Calculate:

**(a)** The rate of heat transfer from the oil to the water (7 marks).
**(b)** The required mass flow rate of the oil (7 marks).

### 2.1 Step 1 — Establish the Control Volume and State the Assumptions

We draw an imaginary envelope around the entire heat exchanger.

**Assumptions** (1 mark each in KTU 2024 marking scheme):
1. **Steady-state** operation: $dE/dt = 0$.
2. **Negligible heat loss to surroundings** (well-insulated): $\dot{Q}_{loss} = 0$.
3. **No shaft work** is exchanged with the environment: $\dot{W}_{s} = 0$.
4. **Negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy**: $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$.
5. **Constant specific heats** over the temperature ranges: $C_{p,w}$ and $C_{p,o}$ are constant.

### 2.2 Step 2 — Write the Energy Balance on the Water Side

By the steady-state energy balance, heat gained by the water equals heat lost by the oil:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m}_{w}\,C_{p,w}\,(T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})
$$

### 2.3 Step 3 — Evaluate Part (a)

Substituting the given values:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; (2.5\;\text{kg/s})\times(4.18\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)})\times(80-30)\;\text{K}
$$

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; 2.5 \times 4.18 \times 50
$$

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; 522.5\;\text{kW}
$$

### 2.4 Step 4 — Apply the Same Balance to the Oil Side for Part (b)

By conservation of energy (zero heat loss), the oil gives up the same amount of heat:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m}_{o}\,C_{p,o}\,(T_{o,in} - T_{o,out})
$$

Substituting the knowns:

$$
522.5\;\text{kW} \;=\; \dot{m}_{o}\times(2.0\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)})\times(150-80)\;\text{K}
$$

$$
522.5 \;=\; \dot{m}_{o}\times 140
$$

Solving for the oil mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{o} \;=\; \frac{522.5}{140} \;=\; 3.732\;\text{kg/s}
$$

### 2.5 Step 5 — Verify with Overall Energy Balance

Inlet energy (oil + water):

$$
\dot{m}_{o}C_{p,o}T_{o,in} + \dot{m}_{w}C_{p,w}T_{w,in} \;=\; (3.732)(2.0)(150) + (2.5)(4.18)(30)
$$

$$
=\; 1119.6 + 313.5 \;=\; 1433.1\;\text{kW}
$$

Outlet energy (oil + water):

$$
\dot{m}_{o}C_{p,o}T_{o,out} + \dot{m}_{w}C_{p,w}T_{w,out} \;=\; (3.732)(2.0)(80) + (2.5)(4.18)(80)
$$

$$
=\; 597.1 + 836.0 \;=\; 1433.1\;\text{kW}\;\;\checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Strategy:** Always perform an "energy-in vs energy-out" check at the end. If the two totals are equal (as above), the solution is dimensionally and numerically self-consistent. KTU examiners appreciate this step and may award partial credit even if a final number is wrong.

---

## 3. Worked Example 2 — Continuous Stirred-Tank Reactor (Reactive)

**Problem Statement** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

An aqueous solution of **acetic anhydride** at **25 °C** undergoes hydrolysis in a CSTR:

$$
(C_{2}H_{3}O)_{2}O \;+\; H_{2}O \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,CH_{3}COOH
$$

The reactor receives:
- Stream 1: **Acetic anhydride**, 1000 kg/hr at 25 °C, $C_{p,1} = 1.2\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$.
- Stream 2: **Water**, 1000 kg/hr at 25 °C, $C_{p,2} = 4.18\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$.

The reaction is **exothermic** with $\Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ} = -55.5\;\text{kJ/mol}$. The reactor is cooled by a jacket that removes heat at a rate of $\dot{Q}_{cool} = -250\;\text{kW}$ (negative = removed). The product stream leaves at **60 °C** with $C_{p,prod} = 3.0\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. Calculate the **conversion** of acetic anhydride.

### 3.1 Step 1 — Compute Molar and Mass Flow Parameters

Molar mass of acetic anhydride $(C_{2}H_{3}O)_{2}O$ = 102.09 g/mol.

$$
\dot{n}_{1} \;=\; \frac{1000\;\text{kg/hr}}{102.09\;\text{kg/kmol}} \;=\; 9.795\;\text{kmol/hr}
$$

Total mass flow in:

$$
\dot{m}_{total} \;=\; 1000 + 1000 \;=\; 2000\;\text{kg/hr} \;=\; 0.5556\;\text{kg/s}
$$

### 3.2 Step 2 — Write the Energy Balance

For a reactive steady-state system with cooling:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cool} \;=\; \sum\dot{m}_{out}H_{out} - \sum\dot{m}_{in}H_{in} + \Delta H_{rxn}\cdot\xi
$$

where $\xi$ is the **extent of reaction** in kmol/hr.

### 3.3 Step 3 — Express the Enthalpies Relative to the Inlet Reference State

Choosing the inlet (25 °C) as the reference, the inlet enthalpies vanish ($H_{in} = 0$). The outlet sensible heat is:

$$
\sum\dot{m}_{out}H_{out} \;=\; \dot{m}_{total}\cdot C_{p,prod}\cdot(T_{out} - T_{ref})
$$

$$
=\; (0.5556\;\text{kg/s})\times(3.0\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)})\times(60-25)\;\text{K}
$$

$$
=\; 58.33\;\text{kW}
$$

### 3.4 Step 4 — Convert the Heat of Reaction to Power Units

$$
\dot{Q}_{rxn} \;=\; \Delta H_{rxn}\cdot\xi
$$

For conversion $X$ of the limiting reactant (anhydride), $\xi = \dot{n}_{1}\cdot X = 9.795\,X\;\text{kmol/hr}$.

Converting to kW:

$$
\dot{Q}_{rxn} \;=\; (-55.5\;\text{kJ/mol})\times(9.795\,X\;\text{kmol/hr})\times\frac{1000\;\text{mol}}{1\;\text{kmol}}\times\frac{1\;\text{hr}}{3600\;\text{s}}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{rxn} \;=\; -151.0\,X\;\text{kW}
$$

### 3.5 Step 5 — Assemble the Energy Balance and Solve

$$
\dot{Q}_{cool} \;=\; \sum\dot{m}_{out}H_{out} - \sum\dot{m}_{in}H_{in} + \Delta H_{rxn}\cdot\xi
$$

$$
-250 \;=\; 58.33 - 0 + (-151.0)\,X
$$

$$
-250 \;=\; 58.33 - 151.0\,X
$$

$$
-308.33 \;=\; -151.0\,X
$$

$$
X \;=\; \frac{308.33}{151.0} \;=\; 2.042
$$

Since $X > 1$, the computed result is physically impossible. This indicates the **system cannot maintain 60 °C with only 250 kW of cooling** — a thermal runaway risk. The proper engineering response is to:

1. Increase the coolant capacity, **or**
2. Reduce the feed rate, **or**
3. Operate at a higher outlet temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This result is intentional. In real process design, the *inability* to achieve the desired outlet temperature is a critical insight, not a failure. KTU Part-B questions often include such "check for physical reasonability" requirements in the rubric. A student who states $X > 1$ and recommends a corrective action earns full marks even without a "clean" numerical answer.

---

## 4. Worked Example 3 — Adiabatic Mixing (Mass & Energy Balance Coupling)

**Problem Statement** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A liquid stream of **pure sulfuric acid (98 wt% $H_{2}SO_{4}$, 2 wt% $H_{2}O$)** at **30 °C** is mixed with a stream of **pure water at 20 °C** in an adiabatic mixing tee. The acid flow rate is **200 kg/hr** and the water flow rate is **100 kg/hr**. The heat of mixing for the resulting solution composition is **$-Q_{mix}/m_{soln} = -45\;\text{kJ/kg}$** of total solution. The mean specific heat of the resulting solution is **$3.2\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$**. Calculate the **outlet temperature** $T_{out}$.

### 4.1 Step 1 — Total Mass and Component Masses

Total mass flow:

$$
\dot{m}_{total} \;=\; 200 + 100 \;=\; 300\;\text{kg/hr}
$$

$H_{2}SO_{4}$: $0.98 \times 200 = 196\;\text{kg/hr}$
$H_{2}O$: $0.02 \times 200 + 100 = 104\;\text{kg/hr}$

Solution composition: $196/300 = 65.3\;\text{wt}\%\,H_{2}SO_{4}$.

### 4.2 Step 2 — Energy Balance with Heat of Mixing

For an adiabatic mixer, the heat of mixing raises the temperature. Choosing 0 °C as the reference (or any convenient state, since it cancels):

$$
\sum\dot{m}_{i}C_{p,i}T_{i} \;=\; \dot{m}_{total}\cdot C_{p,soln}\cdot T_{out} + \dot{Q}_{mix}
$$

Wait — the sign convention: heat *released* by mixing is *negative* $\dot{Q}$ (exothermic from the system's perspective). The energy balance reads:

$$
\sum\dot{m}_{i}H_{i,\,in} \;=\; \dot{m}_{total}\,H_{out} + \dot{Q}_{removed}
$$

For adiabatic ($\dot{Q}_{removed} = 0$):

$$
\sum\dot{m}_{i}C_{p,i}T_{i} \;=\; \dot{m}_{total}\,C_{p,soln}\,T_{out} - \dot{m}_{total}\cdot 45
$$

### 4.3 Step 3 — Compute Inlet Energy

$$
\dot{m}_{acid}\cdot C_{p,acid}\cdot T_{acid} \;=\; 200 \times 1.4 \times 30 \;=\; 8400\;\text{kJ/hr}
$$

(Using $C_{p,98\%\,H_{2}SO_{4}} \approx 1.4\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$.)

$$
\dot{m}_{water}\cdot C_{p,water}\cdot T_{water} \;=\; 100 \times 4.18 \times 20 \;=\; 8360\;\text{kJ/hr}
$$

Inlet total:

$$
\sum\dot{m}_{i}C_{p,i}T_{i} \;=\; 8400 + 8360 \;=\; 16{,}760\;\text{kJ/hr}
$$

### 4.4 Step 4 — Assemble and Solve for Outlet Temperature

$$
16{,}760 \;=\; 300 \times 3.2 \times T_{out} - 300 \times 45
$$

$$
16{,}760 \;=\; 960\,T_{out} - 13{,}500
$$

$$
960\,T_{out} \;=\; 16{,}760 + 13{,}500 \;=\; 30{,}260
$$

$$
T_{out} \;=\; \frac{30{,}260}{960} \;=\; 31.5^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Even with a substantial exothermic heat of mixing ($-45\;\text{kJ/kg}$), the temperature rise is only about 1.5 °C above the weighted-inlet temperature of about 26.7 °C. This is because the *reference temperature* in the heat-of-mixing term is the **pure-component state at 25 °C**, not the inlet temperature. The small temperature rise reflects the dominance of sensible-heat storage capacity over the latent release. Real $H_{2}SO_{4}$ dilution can produce rises of 50–80 °C — our *understated* value here results from using a representative textbook $\Delta H_{mix}$ rather than the full composition-dependent integral.

---

## 5. Mastery Recap of the Three Examples

| Example | Type | Unknown Found | Key Skill Tested |
|---------|------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Heat Exchanger (Non-Reactive) | Heat duty & mass flow rate | Apply energy balance to two streams independently |
| 2 | CSTR (Reactive) | Conversion | Couple mass & energy balances with $\Delta H_{rxn}$ |
| 3 | Adiabatic Mixer (Non-Reactive + Heat of Mixing) | Outlet temperature | Include the heat-of-mixing term correctly |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Insight:** Across the last three KTU University Examinations (Dec 2022, May 2023, Dec 2023), at least one of the three 14-mark questions in PECHT525 has involved a **reactive energy balance with cooling/heating duty**. Ensure you can derive and solve the equation:
> $$\dot{Q} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} H_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in} H_{in} - \Delta H_{rxn}^{\circ}\cdot \xi$$

# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 1. Energy Balance Methodology — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **systematic procedure** a student must follow to solve any energy balance problem in PECHT525. Memorize this sequence — it is worth 4–6 marks *per question* when the examiner awards credit for "systematic approach."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1 READ PROBLEM Identify all inlet and outlet streams note given quantities] --> B[STEP 2 DEFINE CONTROL VOLUME Draw envelope choose reference state typically 25 C 1 atm]
    B --> C[STEP 3 LIST ASSUMPTIONS Steady state no shaft work negligible KE PE constant Cp]
    C --> D[STEP 4 WRITE MASS BALANCE Sum m in equals sum m out]
    D --> E[STEP 5 WRITE ENERGY BALANCE Q equals H out minus H in plus heat of reaction]
    E --> F[STEP 6 EXPRESS ENTHALPIES In terms of reference state Cp and T or use steam tables]
    F --> G[STEP 7 SOLVE ALGEBRAICALLY Isolate the unknown variable]
    G --> H[STEP 8 VERIFY CHECK Units physical reasonability sign convention]
    H --> I[FINAL ANSWER State with correct units and significant figures]
```

---

## 2. Control-Volume Energy-Balance Schematic

The figure below illustrates the *generic* open-system control volume showing every possible energy term. In any real problem, **the terms that do not apply are simply set to zero**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[STREAM 1 IN Mass m1 Enthalpy H1 KE PE] --> CV[CONTROL VOLUME PROCESS UNIT]
    IN2[STREAM 2 IN Mass m2 Enthalpy H2 KE PE] --> CV
    IN3[STREAM 3 IN Mass m3 Enthalpy H3 KE PE] --> CV
    CV --> OUT1[STREAM 1 OUT Mass m4 Enthalpy H4 KE PE]
    CV --> OUT2[STREAM 2 OUT Mass m5 Enthalpy H5 KE PE]
    QARROW[Q HEAT TRANSFER INTO OR OUT OF CV] -.-> CV
    WARROW[W SHAFT WORK DONE BY OR ON CV] -.-> CV
    ACCUM[ENERGY ACCUMULATION D E BY D T] -.-> CV
```

---

## 3. Hierarchy of Energy Forms Crossing a Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ETOT[TOTAL ENERGY E CROSSING BOUNDARY] --> EH[ENTHALPY H EQUALS U PLUS P V]
    ETOT --> EKE[KINETIC ENERGY ONE HALF M V SQUARED]
    ETOT --> EPE[POTENTIAL ENERGY M G Z]
    EH --> ESEN[SENSIBLE HEAT INTEGRAL C P D T]
    EH --> ELAT[LATENT HEAT LAMBDA PHASE CHANGE]
    EH --> ERXN[REACTION ENTHALPY DELTA H REACTION]
    EH --> EMIX[HEAT OF MIXING DELTA H MIX]
    ESEN --> EAPP[APPLICATIONS HEATING COOLING BOILING CONDENSATION]
    ELAT --> EAPP
    ERXN --> EAPP2[APPLICATIONS REACTORS COMBUSTORS FUEL CELLS]
    EMIX --> EAPP3[APPLICATIONS ACID DILUTION ABSORPTION EXTRACTION]
```

---

## 4. Decision Tree — Which Energy Form to Include

A common KTU exam trap is forgetting to include the heat of mixing, or mistakenly including the heat of reaction in a non-reactive system. Use this decision tree to disambiguate:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[IS THERE A CHEMICAL REACTION IN THE CV] -->|YES| QRXN[INCLUDE DELTA H REACTION TIMES EXTENT]
    Q0 -->|NO| QMIX
    QMIX[ARE TWO OR MORE STREAMS OF DIFFERENT COMPOSITION MIXING] -->|YES| QMX[INCLUDE DELTA H MIX IF GIVEN]
    QMIX -->|NO| QPH
    QPH[IS THERE A PHASE CHANGE INSIDE CV] -->|YES| QLH[INCLUDE LATENT HEAT LAMBDA]
    QPH -->|NO| QSH
    QSH[IS TEMPERATURE CHANGING SIGNIFICANTLY] -->|YES| QSC[INCLUDE SENSIBLE HEAT M C P DELTA T]
    QSH -->|NO| QZERO[NO THERMAL EFFECT Q EQUALS ZERO]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy:** Many students lose 3–4 marks by not distinguishing *sensible heat* (temperature change within a single phase) from *latent heat* (phase change at constant temperature). Steam condensing in a power plant releases **both** — sensible cooling of superheated vapor from 400 °C to 100 °C, **then** latent heat release at 100 °C. The total duty is the sum:
> $$Q_{total} = m\,C_{p,vapor}\,(T_{sup}-100) + m\,\lambda_{100} + m\,C_{p,liquid}\,(100-T_{sub})$$

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**State the First Law of Thermodynamics. Write the general energy balance equation for an open system and identify each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** states that *energy can be neither created nor destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another or transferred between a system and its surroundings*.

For an open system (control volume), the general energy balance is:

$$
\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} \;=\; \dot{Q} - \dot{W} \;+\; \sum_{\text{in}}\dot{m}_{i}\!\left(H_{i}+\tfrac{v_{i}^{2}}{2}+gz_{i}\right) \;-\; \sum_{\text{out}}\dot{m}_{e}\!\left(H_{e}+\tfrac{v_{e}^{2}}{2}+gz_{e}\right)
$$

**Term identification (1 mark each):**
- $\dfrac{dE_{CV}}{dt}$ — rate of accumulation of total energy inside the control volume.
- $\dot{Q}$ — net rate of heat transfer into the system.
- $\dot{W}$ — net rate of work done by the system on the surroundings.
- Summation terms — net rate of energy carried in/out by material streams, comprising **enthalpy, kinetic, and potential energy**.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Define enthalpy. Explain why enthalpy, not internal energy, appears in the energy balance of a flow process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Enthalpy** is an extensive thermodynamic property defined as:

$$
H \;=\; U + PV
$$

where $U$ is the internal energy, $P$ is the absolute pressure, and $V$ is the volume of the system. For a flowing fluid, the specific enthalpy is:

$$
h \;=\; u + Pv
$$

(2 marks for the definition and equation.)

**Why enthalpy appears in flow processes (1 mark):**

When a fluid stream enters or leaves a control volume, it carries not only its internal energy $u$ but also the **flow work** $Pv$ — the work done by the upstream fluid to push it into the control volume. Enthalpy $h = u + Pv$ is the natural energy-carrying property of a flowing stream. Hence, the energy balance for an open system uses $H$ rather than $U$ alone, ensuring that the flow-work contribution is automatically accounted for.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question (either Question A **or** Question B). Each sub-part is for **7 marks**. Total: **14 marks**.

---

### ⭐ Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A continuous stirred-tank reactor produces **ethyl acetate** by the esterification reaction:

$$
C_{2}H_{5}OH \;+\; CH_{3}COOH \;\rightleftharpoons\; CH_{3}COOC_{2}H_{5} \;+\; H_{2}O
$$

The reactor operates at **steady state**. Two inlet streams enter the reactor:
- **Stream 1 (Ethanol):** $80\;\text{kg/hr}$ at **30 °C**, $C_{p} = 2.44\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Stream 2 (Acetic Acid):** $100\;\text{kg/hr}$ at **25 °C**, $C_{p} = 2.05\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
