<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, Q1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define the Graetz number and the Thiele modulus. State their physical significance in the context of simultaneous diffusion and chemical reaction in a tubular reactor.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Graetz number** is defined as:

$$
Gz = \frac{u_m\,(2R)^2}{D_{AB}\,L} = \frac{4u_m R^2}{D_{AB} L}
$$

It represents the **ratio of convective transport in the axial direction to diffusive transport in the radial direction** over the tube length. A large $Gz$ implies convection dominates and the concentration boundary layer is thin, justifying the **Leveque approximation**. A small $Gz$ implies diffusion dominates and a full **Graetz eigenfunction solution** is required.

The **Thiele modulus** is defined as:

$$
\phi = R\sqrt{\frac{k}{D_{AB}}}
$$

It represents the **ratio of the characteristic reaction rate to the characteristic diffusion rate** within a porous catalyst pellet (analogous to the tube wall). A large $\phi$ implies the reaction is fast and the system is **diffusion-limited**; a small $\phi$ implies the system is **reaction-limited**.

**Significance:** Together, $Gz$ and $\phi$ (or equivalently, the Damköhler numbers) classify the regime of the simultaneous diffusion–reaction problem and determine which analytical technique (Leveque, Graetz series, effectiveness-factor approach) should be applied.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, Q2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. What is the Leveque approximation? Under what conditions is it valid?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Leveque approximation** is a **boundary-layer simplification** of the Graetz problem in which:

1. The velocity profile is **linearised** near the tube wall: $u_z \approx (2u_m/R)(R - r)$ instead of the full parabolic form.
2. The **cylindrical curvature** is neglected, reducing the radial diffusion operator from $(1/r)\partial_r(r\,\partial_r C_A)$ to a simple Cartesian $\partial^2 C_A/\partial y^2$.

This allows the use of a **similarity transformation** that converts the PDE into an ODE, yielding a closed-form analytical solution.

**Conditions of validity:**

$$
\boxed{\;Gz > 20,\quad z^+ > 0.01,\quad \text{(no homogeneous reaction or } Da_I \text{ small)}\;}
$$

Under these conditions, the concentration variation is confined to a thin boundary layer adjacent to the wall, and the linearised velocity profile is accurate throughout this layer.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Examiner's Note (Module 2, 14-mark question):** KTU 2024 scheme mandates **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions, each typically split into a 7-mark part (a) and a 7-mark part (b). Both choices below are **fully self-contained** so that an examiner can award full marks on either.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** *Derive the steady-state governing differential equation for the simultaneous diffusion and first-order chemical reaction of species A in a fully developed laminar-flow tubular reactor. Clearly state all assumptions and boundary conditions. [7 Marks]*

**(b)** *Using the Leveque approximation, obtain the concentration profile $C_A(y, z)$ and the local Sherwood number for a first-order heterogeneous reaction at the tube wall. [7 Marks]*

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state: $\partial C_A / \partial t = 0$
2. Axisymmetric: $\partial C_A / \partial \theta = 0$
3. Fully developed laminar flow: $u_z = 2u_m(1 - r^2/R^2)$, $u_r = u_\theta = 0$
4. Constant diffusivity $D_{AB}$
5. Negligible axial diffusion: $D_{AB}\partial^2 C_A/\partial z^2 \approx 0$
6. Fick's law valid; dilute system

**Mass balance on a cylindrical shell** of radius r, thickness dr, length dz (accumulation = 0 at steady state):

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \bigl[\text{convective in} - \text{convective out}\bigr] + \bigl[\text{diffusive in} - \text{diffusive out}\bigr] - R_A\,dV \\
0 &= u_z\,\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}(2\pi r\,dr) - \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r D_{AB}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)\frac{2\pi\,dz\,dr}{r} - R_A\,(2\pi r\,dr\,dz)
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing throughout by $2\pi r\,dr\,dz$ and using $R_A = k C_A$:

$$
u_z\,\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB}\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right) - k C_A
$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

| Location | Condition |
|----------|-----------|
| $z = 0$ | $C_A = C_{A0}$ (uniform inlet) |
| $r = 0$ | $\partial C_A / \partial r = 0$ (symmetry) |
| $r = R$ | $-D_{AB}\partial C_A/\partial r = k_s C_A(R,z)$ (wall reaction) **or** $\partial C_A/\partial r = 0$ (inert) |

This completes the formulation. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

For a **first-order heterogeneous wall reaction** with negligible homogeneous reaction, the bulk equation reduces to:

$$
2u_m\!\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)\!\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB}\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)
$$

**Leveque linearisation** (with $y = R - r$, $y \ll R$):

$$
\frac{2u_m\,y}{R}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB}\,\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}
$$

**Similarity variable:** $\eta = y\,(2u_m/(9D_{AB}\,z\,R))^{1/3}$.

**Substitution and simplification** (as shown in Derivation III, Section 3.3) leads to:

$$
\frac{d^2 C_A}{d\eta^2} + 3\eta^2\,\frac{dC_A}{d\eta} = 0
$$

Integrating twice with BCs $C_A \to C_{A0}$ as $\eta \to \infty$ and $-D_{AB}\partial C_A/\partial y = k_s C_A$ at $\eta = 0$:

$$
\frac{C_{A0} - C_A(\eta,z)}{C_{A0}} = \frac{k_s}{D_{AB}^{2/3}}\!\left(\frac{9R}{2u_m\,z}\right)^{\!1/3}\!\int_0^{\eta} e^{-t^3}\,dt
$$

**Local mass-transfer coefficient at the wall** ($y \to 0$):

$$
k_{c,z} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{C_{A0}-C_{A,w}}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}\bigg|_{y=0} = 0.5384\,D_{AB}\!\left(\frac{2u_m}{D_{AB}\,R\,z}\right)^{\!1/3}
$$

**Local Sherwood number** (using diameter $D = 2R$):

$$
\boxed{\;Sh_z = \frac{k_{c,z}\,D}{D_{AB}} = 1.077\!\left(\frac{u_m\,D^2}{D_{AB}\,z}\right)^{\!1/3} = 1.077\,(Gz)^{1/3}(z^+)^{-1/3}\;}
$$

This is the **Leveque solution** for the Sherwood number in a tubular reactor with first-order wall reaction. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** *Discuss the role of the dimensionless numbers — Graetz number, Peclet number, and Damköhler number — in the analysis of simultaneous diffusion and chemical reaction in a tubular reactor. [7 Marks]*

**(b)** *Using the Leveque similarity solution, derive an expression for the mean (mixing-cup) concentration at the tube exit. Comment on the physical significance of the result. [7 Marks]*

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Peclet Number (Mass):**

$$
Pe_m = \frac{u_m\,D}{D_{AB}}
$$

It is the **ratio of convective to diffusive transport** at the tube scale. A large $Pe_m$ (typical of liquids and gases in millimetre-scale tubes) justifies **neglecting axial diffusion**.

**Graetz Number:**

$$
Gz = \frac{u_m\,D^2}{D_{AB}\,L} = Pe_m \cdot \frac{D}{L}
$$

It is the **ratio of diffusion time ($D^2/D_{AB}$) to residence time ($L/u_m$)**. Physical significance:
- $Gz \ll 1$: Diffusion is fast relative to residence time → concentration is nearly uniform (plug flow with complete radial mixing).
- $Gz \gg 1$: Convection dominates → concentration boundary layer is thin near the wall → **Leveque approximation** is valid.

**Damköhler Numbers:**

For a **homogeneous** first-order reaction:

$$
Da_I = \frac{k\,L}{u_m}
$$

For a **heterogeneous** (wall) first-order reaction:

$$
Da_{II} = \frac{k_s\,L}{D_{AB}}
$$

These represent the **ratio of characteristic reaction rate to characteristic transport rate**:
- $Da \ll 1$: Transport dominates → reaction is the rate-limiting step.
- $Da \gg 1$: Reaction dominates → transport (diffusion) is rate-limiting.

**Combined Significance:** The trio $(Gz, Pe_m, Da)$ completely classifies the regime of the simultaneous diffusion–reaction problem. Their product and ratios determine whether Leveque, Graetz series, or a reaction-controlled limit is the appropriate analytical tool. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

The **mixing-cup (flow-weighted) mean concentration** at axial position $z$ is:

$$
\bar{C}_A(z) = \frac{\int_0^R C_A(r,z)\,u_z(r)\,2\pi r\,dr}{\int_0^R u_z(r)\,2\pi r\,dr} = 2\int_0^1 C_A(r^+,z)(1-r^{+2})\,r^+\,dr^+
$$

**Apply the Leveque assumption:** Beyond a thin boundary layer near the wall (where most of the concentration variation occurs), $C_A \approx C_{A0}$. The mean concentration deficit is therefore controlled by the boundary layer:

$$
\frac{C_{A0} - \bar{C}_A(z)}{C_{A0}} \approx \frac{\int_0^{\delta(z)} [C_{A0} - C_A(r,z)]\,u_z(r)\,r\,dr}{\int_0^R u_z(r)\,r\,dr}
$$

Using the linearised velocity $u_z = (2u_m/R)y$ and the boundary layer thickness scaling $\delta \sim (D_{AB}\,R\,z/u_m)^{1/3}$ from the Leveque solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{A0} - \bar{C}_A(z) &\sim C_{A0}\left(\frac{D_{AB}}{u_m R^2 z}\right)^{1/3} \\
\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\bar{C}_A(z)}{C_{A0}} &= 1 - 1.077\!\left(\frac{D_{AB}}{u_m R^2 z}\right)^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $z = L$ (tube exit):

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\bar{C}_{A,L}}{C_{A0}} = 1 - 1.077\!\left(\frac{D_{AB}}{u_m R^2 L}\right)^{1/3} = 1 - 1.077\,(Gz)^{-1/3}\;}
$$

**Physical Significance:**

- The conversion at the outlet, $X = 1 - \bar{C}_{A,L}/C_{A0} = 1.077\,Gz^{-1/3}$, **decreases** as $Gz$ increases. This is because a larger $Gz$ means the fluid spends less time in the reactor (or the tube is longer relative to diffusion), so less A is converted.
- The **$1/3$-power dependence** on $Gz$ is a hallmark of the Leveque boundary-layer solution and contrasts with the **first-order exponential decay** of the well-mixed (Graetz small) limit.
- The result is the **design equation** for tubular reactors under the Leveque regime: it tells the engineer how to size the tube (L, R) for a desired conversion. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Mistakes — Read Carefully Before Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the parabolic velocity profile** (using $u_z = u_m$ constant): This converts the problem to a 1-D axial model and loses the entire Graetz/Leveque structure. KTU examiners specifically test this — **always start with $u_z = 2u_m(1 - r^2/R^2)$**.
>
> 2. **Missing boundary conditions**: A derivation that does not explicitly list **all four** BCs (inlet, axis, wall, outlet — or symmetry) loses 1–2 marks. **Memorise the four BCs.**
>
> 3. **Wrong similarity variable**: The Leveque similarity variable is $\eta = y\,(2u_m/(9D_{AB}\,z\,R))^{1/3}$, **not** $y\,(u_m/D_{AB}\,z)^{1/2}$ (that is the Schmidt/Pohlhausen form for a different problem). The factor **9 in the denominator** comes from the integration of $\int_0^\eta 3t^2\,dt = \eta^3$.
>
> 4. **Ignoring curvature in Leveque**: Replacing $(1/r)\partial_r(r\partial_r C_A)$ with $\partial^2 C_A/\partial y^2$ is **only valid in the thin boundary layer** near the wall. State this assumption explicitly.
>
> 5. **Confusing $Da_I$ and $Da_{II}$**: $Da_I$ is for homogeneous reactions (units use $k$ in $\mathrm{1/s}$), $Da_{II}$ is for heterogeneous wall reactions (units use $k_s$ in $\mathrm{m/s}$ and $D_{AB}$). Mixing them up is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the constant 1.077**: The numerical constant in the Sherwood number is **not** 1.0 or 2.0 — it is $1.077 = 2 \cdot 0.5384$ from the integral $\int_0^\infty e^{-t^3}\,dt = (1/3)\Gamma(1/3) \approx 0.8930$. State the integral evaluation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Tick Before the Exam!**

- [ ] **Physical setup**: Cylindrical tube, parabolic laminar flow, dilute system, Fickian diffusion.
- [ ] **Governing equation** (the "big" equation):
$$u_z\,\partial C_A/\partial z = D_{AB}\,(1/r)\,\partial_r(r\,\partial_r C_A) - k C_A$$
- [ ] **4 Boundary conditions**: inlet ($C_A = C_{A0}$), axis ($\partial_r C_A = 0$), wall (Robin or Neumann), and steady state.
- [ ] **Parabolic velocity**: $u_z = 2u_m(1 - r^2/R^2)$.
- [ ] **Linearised velocity** near wall: $u_z \approx (2u_m/R)(R - r)$.
- [ ] **Similarity variable**: $\eta = (R - r)(2u_m/(9D_{AB} z R))^{1/3}$.
- [ ] **Leveque ODE**: $d^2 C_A/d\eta^2 + 3\eta^2\,dC_A/d\eta = 0$.
- [ ] **Local Sherwood number**: $Sh_z = 1.077\,(Gz)^{1/3}\,(z^+)^{-1/3}$.
- [ ] **Mean outlet concentration**: $\bar{C}_{A,L}/C_{A0} = 1 - 1.077\,Gz^{-1/3}$.
- [ ] **Graetz number**: $Gz = u_m D^2/(D_{AB} L)$ — high $Gz$ → Leveque regime.
- [ ] **Peclet number**: $Pe_m = u_m D/D_{AB}$ — large $Pe_m$ → neglect axial diffusion.
- [ ] **Damköhler I (homogeneous)**: $Da_I = k L/u_m$.
- [ ] **Damköhler II (heterogeneous)**: $Da_{II} = k_s L/D_{AB}$.
- [ ] **Thiele modulus**: $\phi = R\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$.
- [ ] **Effectiveness factor**: $\eta_{eff} = \tanh(\phi)/\phi$ (slab); $= 2I_1(\phi)/(\phi I_0(\phi))$ (cylinder).
- [ ] **Integral identity**: $\int_0^\infty e^{-t^3}\,dt = (1/3)\Gamma(1/3) \approx 0.8930$.
- [ ] **Validity regime for Leveque**: $Gz > 20$, $z^+ > 0.01$.
- [ ] **Reactor design link**: Conversion $X = 1.077\,Gz^{-1/3}$ is the **design equation** under Leveque.
- [ ] **Engineering applications**: Catalytic converters, packed-bed tubes, absorption with reaction, polymerisation reactors, heat-exchanger analogue.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Memorise the **form, not just the formula**. In derivations, the examiner looks for: (i) the **starting PDE**, (ii) the **physical assumptions stated explicitly**, (iii) the **boundary conditions in a table**, (iv) the **similarity transformation** with all derivatives worked out, (v) the **final boxed result**. Skipping any one of these typically costs 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Hydrolysis of Tallow in a Spray Column
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Continuous Hydrolysis of Tallow in a Spray Column # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Continuous Hydrolysis of Tallow in a Spray Column** is a steady-state, counter-current, **liquid–liquid mass transfer operation coupled with a slow, reversible, homogeneous chemical reaction**, carried out in an unpacked vertical spray-type contactor. The triglyceride phase (tallow) is dispersed as fine droplets through a spray nozzle at the top, while the aqueous phase (water) flows upward as the continuous phase. Across the droplet interface, water diffuses into the oil phase where it reacts with the triglyceride to liberate **fatty acids** (which remain in the oil) and **glycerol** (which diffuses back into the water phase). **Governing Reaction (Stoichiometry):** $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOCR})_3 \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; 3\,\text{RCOOH} \;+\; \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$$ | Species | Phase | Role | |---|---|---| | Tallow (triglyceride) | Dispersed (oil) | Limiting reactant | | Water | Continuous (aqueous) | Excess reactant | | Fatty acids | Dispersed (oil) | Desired product (retains in oil) | | Glycerol | Continuous (aqueous) | By-product (transfers out) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Shower-Washing-a-Dirty-Sponge" Analogy** > Imagine you are showering over a dirty kitchen sponge (the **tallow droplet**). Water from the shower (**continuous water phase**) cascades over the sponge. The water soaks into the sponge's pores (analogous to **diffusion of H₂O into oil**), and there, a slow chemical "cleaning" occurs — the dirt dissolves (analogous to **hydrolysis of triglyceride**). The cleaned waste water (containing **glycerol**) drips out of the sponge back into the shower stream. Many such sponges (droplets) are dropped continuously through the column, while clean water flows up — a perfect **counter-current shower**. The *depth* of cleaning depends on (a) how long each sponge is in contact (residence time), (b) the contact area (droplet size), and (c) the "thirst" of the sponge for water (equilibrium-limited by solubility of water in oil ≈ **0.001 to 0.01 mass fraction**). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Engineering Metrics - **Molecular weight of tallow (assumed olein):** $\approx 884 \text{ g/mol}$ (**used as standard 884–885** in KTU references) - **Molecular weight of water:** $18.015 \text{ g/mol}$ - **Density of tallow:** $\approx 875 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Density of water:** $\approx 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Interfacial tension (oil/water):** $\approx 0.015\text{–}0.025 \text{ N/m}$ - **Solubility of water in tallow (25 °C):** $\approx 0.005 \text{ mass fraction}$ (**critical — controls driving force**) - **Equilibrium constant K of hydrolysis:** very small → reaction is **slow, kinetically controlled** - **Sauter mean droplet diameter ($d_{32}$):** typically $0.001\text{–}0.004 \text{ m}$ for spray nozzles - **Standard operating temperature:** $T = 473\text{–}523 \text{ K}$ ($200\text{–}250\,^\circ\text{C}$) for industrial tallow hydrolysis # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic > [!NOTE] > The spray column is **unpacked** (no packing material, no sieve trays). Mass transfer occurs purely through droplets falling under gravity against a rising continuous water stream. Because the droplets coalesce and re-disperse continuously, **axial mixing in the dispersed phase is severe**, and the column is best modeled as a **series of well-mixed stages** or by a **back-mix / dispersion model** rather than as an ideal plug flow. **Step 1 — Dispersion at the top:** Tallow is pumped through a spray nozzle at the top of the column, breaking the bulk liquid into a swarm of droplets of characteristic diameter $d_p$ (Sauter mean $d_{32}$). The **interfacial area per unit volume** $a$ is: $$a \;=\; \frac{6\,\phi_d}{d_{32}}$$ where $\phi_d$ is the **dispersed phase holdup** (volume fraction of droplets in the column). **Step 2 — Falling droplet, rising water:** Droplets descend at terminal velocity $v_t$. The continuous water phase ascends at superficial velocity $v_c$. The relative velocity of slip drives the interfacial renewal. **Step 3 — Mass transfer with reaction (the core):** Water must **diffuse from bulk water → through the aqueous film → across the interface → through the oil-side film → into the oil bulk**, where it reacts homogeneously with triglyceride. Because triglyceride is essentially **insoluble in water**, the reaction occurs almost entirely in the **oil phase**. **Step 4 — Glycerol extraction:** Glycerol formed in the oil phase has appreciable solubility in water (~10 wt%). It therefore **counter-diffuses out** of the oil droplet into the water stream and is swept upward to be removed at the top aqueous outlet. **Step 5 — Exit streams:** - **Top (aqueous outlet):** water + dissolved glycerol + trace fatty acid (slight mutual solubility). - **Bottom (oil outlet):** hydrolyzed tallow = mixture of fatty acids + unreacted triglyceride (residual). ## 2.2 Why Hydrolysis is Mass-Transfer Limited (the "Why") > [!IMPORTANT] > The intrinsic reaction rate constant for triglyceride hydrolysis (uncatalyzed) at 200–250 °C is on the order of $k \approx 10^{-5}\text{–}10^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}$. The characteristic time for water to diffuse across a 1–3 mm droplet is on the order of $\tau_D \sim d_p^2/D \sim 10^{2}\text{–}10^{3}\,\text{s}$. The **Hatta number** $M = \sqrt{k\,D/k_L^2}$ is much less than unity. Therefore: > > $$\text{Reaction rate} \ll \text{Diffusion rate} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{process is governed by mass transfer, not kinetics.}$$ > > The KTU 2024 syllabus leverages this property to permit the **pseudo-first-order simplification** and the use of overall mass transfer coefficients. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---|---| | 1 | $a = \dfrac{6\,\phi_d}{d_{32}}$ | Interfacial area per unit column volume, $\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$ | | 2 | $N_A = k_L\,a\,\Delta C$ | Volumetric mass transfer flux of water, $\text{kmol/(m}^3\!\cdot\text{s)}$ | | 3 | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L^w} + \dfrac{m}{k_L^o}$ | Two-resistance (overall) coefficient; $m$ = equilibrium slope (water in oil) | | 4 | $Z = \displaystyle\int_{x_2}^{x_1}\!\dfrac{v_{ox}\,dx}{K_{Lx}\,a\,(x^\ast - x)}$ | Height of packing (here — height of spray column), m | | 5 | $\text{HTU} = \dfrac{v_{ox}}{K_{Lx}\,a}$ | Height of a transfer unit, m | | 6 | $\text{NTU} = \displaystyle\int_{x_2}^{x_1}\!\dfrac{dx}{x^\ast - x}$ | Number of transfer units, dimensionless | | 7 | $Z = \text{HTU} \times \text{NTU}$ | The fundamental design equation for any counter-current contactor | | 8 | $v_t = \dfrac{g\,d_p^2\,(\rho_c - \rho_d)}{18\,\mu_c}$ | Stokes terminal velocity of droplet, m/s | | 9 | $\phi_d = \dfrac{Q_d}{Q_d + Q_c}$ (slip ≈) | Dispersed phase holdup, dimensionless | | 10 | $R = \dfrac{\text{kmol H}_2\text{O transferred}}{\text{kmol triglyceride reacted}}$ | Stoichiometric ratio (theoretical = 3) | | 11 | $\text{Slip velocity: } v_s = \dfrac{v_d}{\phi_d} + \dfrac{v_c}{1 - \phi_d}$ | Used to compute actual holdup | | 12 | $K_{Lx} = \dfrac{K_{Lz}}{(1 - x)(1 - x^\ast)}$ | Conversion between $x$- and $z$-basis (z = mass fraction) | > [!TIP] > In KTU problems the **K value** for water between water and tallow at 230 °C is conventionally taken as $m \approx 0.005$ (i.e., the equilibrium mass fraction of water in oil). Always convert the overall coefficient to a **water-side basis** before integrating. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > The tallow hydrolysis spray column is the **canonical textbook example** of a **liquid–liquid reactor with mass-transfer resistance**. It appears in KTU 2024 Module 2 because it allows the student to apply: > 1. **Two-film theory** to a reactive system, > 2. **HTU–NTU design procedure** for a non-ideal contactor, > 3. **Material balances with chemical reaction**, and > 4. The concept of an **overall mass transfer coefficient** with phase equilibrium. It is industrially used to produce **oleic / stearic acid** (soaps, surfactants, lubricants) and **glycerol** (pharma, cosmetics, explosives — historically as nitroglycerin). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Operating Line from Material Balance Consider a section of the column at height $Z$ measured from the top. Let $L$ = molar flow rate of oil (tallow + fatty acid), $V$ = molar flow rate of water phase. The mass fraction of triglyceride (or equivalent: "unsaponifiable / un-hydrolyzed") in the oil phase is $x$ (kg triglyceride / kg oil) on the oil-rich basis. The corresponding equilibrium value (with water) is $x^{\ast}$. **Total material balance on triglyceride-free oil (solvent):** $$L\,(1 - x_1) = L\,(1 - x_2) \quad\Rightarrow\quad L = \text{constant}$$ **Component balance on triglyceride (in oil):** $$L\,x_1 \;-\; L\,x_2 \;=\; \text{kmol reacted (per unit time basis)}$$ A small differential balance over $dZ$ gives the operating line on an $(x, y)$ plot where $y$ = mass fraction of water in the water phase. For dilute transfer with constant $L$ and $V$: $$\dfrac{d(L\,x)}{dZ} = -K_{Lx}\,a\,(x^\ast - x)$$ Converting to a difference equation and recognizing the **integral design equation**: $$Z = \int_{x_2}^{x_1}\!\dfrac{L\,dx}{K_{Lx}\,a\,(x^\ast - x)}$$ which is the familiar **HTU · NTU** form, with $\text{HTU} = L/(K_{Lx}\,a)$ and $\text{NTU} = \int dx/(x^\ast - x)$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equilibrium Relation Across the Interface > [!NOTE] > The spray column operates in a regime where the **continuous aqueous phase is essentially pure water** (very dilute in glycerol) and the **dispersed oil phase is essentially pure oil + dissolved water**. With this simplification, the **equilibrium curve is a vertical line** at $x^{\ast} = x_w^{\text{sat}} = $ solubility of water in tallow. $$x^{\ast} = x_{w,\text{sat}} \;=\; \text{const.}$$ Because $x^{\ast}$ is constant, the integral in NTU collapses to: $$\text{NTU} = \int_{x_2}^{x_1}\!\dfrac{dx}{x^{\ast} - x} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x^{\ast} - x_2}{x^{\ast} - x_1}\right)$$ > [!TIP] > **This is the single most important simplification** for the KTU tallow hydrolysis problem. The student must justify it in the answer by stating *"Because the continuous water phase is essentially pure, the equilibrium concentration of water in the oil is constant and equal to its solubility."* ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Two-Film / Overall Coefficient The **two-film theory** for water transferring from water (phase $w$) to oil (phase $o$): $$N_A = k_w\,(C_{A,w} - C_{A,w}^i) = k_o\,(C_{A,o}^i - C_{A,o})$$ At equilibrium, $C_{A,o}^i = m\,C_{A,w}^i$. Eliminating interfacial concentrations: $$\dfrac{1}{K_o} = \dfrac{1}{k_o} + \dfrac{m}{k_w}$$ Expressed in mass-fraction driving force for the oil phase: $$\dfrac{1}{K_{Lx}} = \dfrac{1}{k_{Lx}} + \dfrac{m}{k_{Lx}^w/(1-x)(1-x^\ast)}$$ For tallow hydrolysis: $k_{Lx}^w$ (water-side film) is very large because water is the bulk continuous phase, so $m/k_{Lx}^w \to 0$. Therefore: $$K_{Lx} \;\approx\; k_{Lx} \quad\text{(oil-side film controls)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion (KTU-favoured answer):** For the hydrolysis of tallow in a spray column, **the oil-phase (dispersed-phase) resistance controls the mass transfer**. The KTU 2024 module specifically trains the student to identify the controlling resistance. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Full Worked Analytical Matrix > [!EXAMPLE] > **Worked problem (typical KTU 14-mark format):** > A spray column of **0.6 m diameter** hydrolyses tallow at **230 °C**. Oil flow rate $L = 0.50\,\text{kg/s}$, water flow rate $V = 0.30\,\text{kg/s}$. Inlet triglyceride mass fraction (in oil) $x_1 = 0.96$, desired outlet $x_2 = 0.04$. Solubility of water in tallow $x^{\ast} = 0.005$. Overall oil-side coefficient $K_{Lx}\,a = 0.0025\,\text{s}^{-1}$. Determine the **required column height**. **Step 1 — Define NTU:** $$\text{NTU} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x^{\ast} - x_2}{x^{\ast} - x_1}\right)$$ $$= \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.005 - 0.04}{0.005 - 0.96}\right)$$ $$= \ln\!\left(\dfrac{-0.035}{-0.955}\right) = \ln(0.03665)$$ $$= -3.306$$ **Step 2 — Note on sign convention:** The driving force $(x^\ast - x)$ is **negative** (because $x > x^\ast$ throughout). Take magnitudes: $$\text{NTU} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_1 - x^{\ast}}{x_2 - x^{\ast}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.955}{0.035}\right) = \ln(27.286) = 3.306$$ **Step 3 — Compute HTU:** The superficial velocity of oil in the column. With $\phi_d \approx 0.10$ (typical for spray columns, from correlations) and column cross-section $A = \pi(0.6)^2/4 = 0.2827\,\text{m}^2$: $$v_{ox} = \dfrac{L/\rho_{oil}}{A\cdot\phi_d} = \dfrac{0.50/875}{0.2827 \times 0.10} = \dfrac{5.714\times10^{-4}}{0.02827} = 0.0202\,\text{m/s}$$ $$\text{HTU} = \dfrac{v_{ox}}{K_{Lx}\,a} = \dfrac{0.0202}{0.0025} = 8.08\,\text{m}$$ **Step 4 — Column height:** $$Z = \text{HTU} \times \text{NTU} = 8.08 \times 3.306 = 26.7\,\text{m}$$ > [!NOTE] > Such a height is **impractical for a single spray column**. Industrial plants therefore use **multi-stage columns with re-dispersion at each stage** (Treybal, 3rd ed., Ch. 11), or switch to **packed / sieve-tray columns** for tallow hydrolysis. The KTU question may then ask: *"Comment on whether a single spray column is practical, and propose a modification."* ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Number of Theoretical Stages vs. Spray Stages For comparison, the **number of ideal stages** for the same duty: $$N_{ideal} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_1 - x^{\ast}}{x_2 - x^{\ast}}\right)}{\ln(\text{extraction factor})} \quad \text{where } E = \dfrac{mV}{L}$$ For dilute tallow, $E \ll 1$, so the system is "oil-phase limited" and $N_{ideal} \approx 3.3$. With Murphree efficiency $\eta_M \approx 0.2\text{–}0.4$ (spray columns are inefficient because of back-mixing), the **actual number of spray stages is 8–16**. > [!WARNING] > **DO NOT confuse HTU/NTU with the number of stages.** KTU students commonly mix these up. HTU·NTU is a **rate-based** (continuous) model; stages are an **equilibrium-based** (discrete) model. They are linked by the **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Stage (HETS):** $Z = N_{ideal}\times \text{HETS}$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Tallow Storage Tank
Triglyceride rich] -->|Pump| B[Preheater
230 C] B -->|Top inlet| C[Spray Column
Unpacked tower] W[Deaerated Water
Deionized] -->|Pump| P[Preheater] P -->|Bottom inlet| C C -->|Bottom outlet| D[Hydrolyzed Tallow
Fatty acids + glycerol-free] C -->|Top outlet| E[Glycerol-rich Water
Sweet water 10 to 20 percent glycerol] E --> F[Glycerol Recovery
Evaporation and distillation] D --> G[Fatty Acid Purification
Vacuum distillation] F --> H[Crude Glycerol 80 percent] G --> I[Oleic Stearic Acid
Cosmetic grade] ``` ## 4.2 Two-Film / Mass-Transfer Block Diagram at a Droplet ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BulkOil["Droplet Oil Phase"] OB[Bulk triglyceride x greater than x star] end subgraph FilmOil["Oil side film"] OF[x at interface] end subgraph Interface["Liquid Liquid Interface"] I1[Water side x star] end subgraph FilmWater["Water side film"] WF[x at water interface] end subgraph BulkWater["Continuous Water Phase"] WB[Bulk water x w] end OB -->|k oil film| OF OF -->|equilibrium m| I1 I1 -->|k water film| WF WF -->|bulk convection| WB WB -->|back flow| WF OB -.->|reaction with triglyceride| RB[Fatty acid plus glycerol] RB -->|glycerol diffuses back| WF ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Height Computation) ```mermaid flowchart TD IN1[Inlet oil x1] --> MD1[Material balance around differential slice dZ] IN2[Inlet water y1] --> MD1 MD1 --> OP[Operating line L dx and V dy] OP --> EQ[Equilibrium x star constant since water is pure] EQ --> INT[Integrate dx over x star minus x] INT --> NTU[NTU equals ln of x1 minus x star over x2 minus x star] IN3[Oil velocity v ox] --> HTU[HTU equals v ox over K Lx times a] IN4[Overall coefficient K Lx times a] --> HTU NTU --> Z[Column height Z equals HTU times NTU] HTU --> Z Z --> OUT[Final design height in metres] ``` ## 4.4 Stage-Wise Re-Dispersion Concept (Industrial Reality) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Stage 1 Spray Section] -->|Re dispersion at interface| S2[Stage 2 Spray Section] S2 -->|Re dispersion| S3[Stage 3 Spray Section] S3 -->|Re dispersion| SN[Stage N] S1 -->|Coalesced oil drips down| R1[Re disperser nozzle] R1 --> S2 S2 -->|Coalesces| R2[Re disperser nozzle] R2 --> S3 SN --> O[Hydrolyzed oil out] S1 -.->|Water up| S2 S2 -.->|Water up| S3 S3 -.->|Water up| SN SN -.-> W[Glycerol water out top] ``` > [!TIP] > The "re-dispersion at each stage" architecture is what makes industrial tallow hydrolysis columns (e.g., the **Colgate–Emery process**) economically viable. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the student to draw such a schematic in descriptive answers. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)** ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Why is the oil-phase (dispersed) resistance controlling in the continuous hydrolysis of tallow in a spray column, even though the reaction occurs in the oil phase?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. The reaction rate constant $k$ for triglyceride hydrolysis is very small ($10^{-5}\text{–}10^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}$), making the Hatta number $\ll 1$. The reaction is **slow relative to diffusion**, so it does not enhance the overall mass transfer rate. **(1 mark)** > 2. The solubility (equilibrium partition) of water in tallow is very low ($m \approx 0.005$), so the water-side concentration driving force is large. Water-film resistance $1/k_w$ becomes negligible. **(1 mark)** > 3. Triglyceride is virtually insoluble in water, forcing the entire reaction to occur in the oil bulk after water has diffused through the oil film. Hence the **oil-side film** dominates, and $K_{Lx} \approx k_{Lx}$. **(1 mark)** --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **State the assumptions used to derive the HTU·NTU equation for a counter-current spray column used for tallow hydrolysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. Steady state, constant molar (mass) flow rates of the two phases. **(1 mark)** > 2. The continuous (water) phase is essentially pure — equilibrium water concentration in oil is constant at its solubility $x^{\ast}$. **(1 mark)** > 3. Plug flow of both phases, constant overall mass transfer coefficient $K_{Lx}\,a$, and negligible axial dispersion. **(1 mark)** --- ## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do not** confuse the two driving forces $(x^\ast - x)$ with $(x - x^\ast)$ — sign error is the single biggest mark-loser in this problem. > - **Always state** the assumption that the equilibrium line is vertical (constant $x^\ast$) explicitly; otherwise the examiner deducts 1–2 marks. > - Convert all flows to a **consistent basis** (kg/s of triglyceride-free oil) before applying HTU/NTU. > - Mention the **controlling resistance** (oil-side) explicitly — a common KTU question probe. > - Numerical answers must carry **units**; bare numbers lose 1 mark each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]` Tallow is to be hydrolyzed continuously in a spray column of **0.75 m diameter** operating at **230 °C** and **5 bar**. The inlet oil flow is **$L = 0.80\,\text{kg/s}$** with triglyceride mass fraction $x_1 = 0.97$. Outlet oil is to contain $x_2 = 0.05$ (mass fraction triglyceride). The dispersed-phase holdup is $\phi_d = 0.12$. Density of oil = $880\,\text{kg/m}^3$. Solubility of water in tallow $x^{\ast} = 0.005$. Overall oil-side volumetric coefficient $K_{Lx}\,a = 0.0030\,\text{s}^{-1}$. **(a)** Derive the **HTU·NTU design equation** for a counter-current spray column with constant equilibrium concentration, stating clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the **required height of the column** and comment on its practicality. Propose one engineering modification. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks** **Assumptions** (2 marks): 1. Steady state, constant $L$ and $V$, dilute mass transfer. 2. Continuous water phase is essentially pure, hence $x^{\ast} = \text{const} = 0.005$. 3. Constant $K_{Lx}\,a$ along the height. 4. Negligible axial dispersion (ideal plug flow). **Derivation** (5 marks): Differential material balance on triglyceride in oil over slice $dZ$: $$L\,dx = K_{Lx}\,a\,(x^{\ast} - x)\,A_c\,dZ$$ where $A_c$ is the cross-section. Define superficial oil velocity $v_{ox} = L/(\rho_{oil}\,A_c)$. Then: $$dZ = \dfrac{v_{ox}\,dx}{K_{Lx}\,a\,(x^{\ast} - x)}$$ Integrate from $x_2$ to $x_1$: $$Z = \dfrac{v_{ox}}{K_{Lx}\,a}\,\int_{x_2}^{x_1}\!\dfrac{dx}{x^{\ast} - x} = \underbrace{\dfrac{v_{ox}}{K_{Lx}\,a}}_{\text{HTU}}\cdot\underbrace{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_1 - x^{\ast}}{x_2 - x^{\ast}}\right)}_{\text{NTU}}$$ $$\boxed{Z = \text{HTU}\cdot\text{NTU}}$$ --- #### **Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Cross-section** (1 mark): $$A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.75)^2 = 0.4418\,\text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Superficial oil velocity** (2 marks): $$v_{ox} = \dfrac{L/(\rho_{oil}\,A_c)}{\phi_d} = \dfrac{0.80/(880 \times 0.4418)}{0.12} = \dfrac{0.002060}{0.12} = 0.01717\,\text{m/s}$$ **Step 3 — HTU and NTU** (2 marks): $$\text{HTU} = \dfrac{v_{ox}}{K_{Lx}\,a} = \dfrac{0.01717}{0.0030} = 5.72\,\text{m}$$ $$\text{NTU} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.97 - 0.005}{0.05 - 0.005}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.965}{0.045}\right) = \ln(21.44) = 3.066$$ **Step 4 — Column height** (1 mark): $$Z = 5.72 \times 3.066 = 17.5\,\text{m}$$ **Step 5 — Practicality comment** (1 mark): A 17.5 m unpacked spray column suffers from severe back-mixing and low Murphree efficiency. Practical designs use **packed columns with coalescers/redispersers between packed sections**, or **multi-stage spray columns with mechanical re-dispersion at each stage** (Colgate–Emery type). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` In a spray column hydrolyzing tallow counter-currently, the following data are obtained at steady state: - Oil flow rate (triglyceride-free basis): $L' = 0.60\,\text{kg/s}$ - Inlet triglyceride mass fraction: $x_1 = 0.95$ - Outlet triglyceride mass fraction: $x_2 = 0.10$ - Solubility of water in tallow: $x^{\ast} = 0.005$ - Overall volumetric coefficient (oil-side): $K_{Lx}\,a = 0.0028\,\text{s}^{-1}$ - Dispersed phase holdup: $\phi_d = 0.15$ - Oil density: $870\,\text{kg/m}^3$ - Column diameter: $0.50\,\text{m}$ **(a)** Identify the **controlling mass transfer resistance** and justify with the two-film theory. Compute the **NTU**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the **column height**. If the same duty is carried out in a **packed column** with $\text{HETS} = 1.8\,\text{m}$ and ideal stage requirement $N_{ideal} = 3.0$, compute the height of the packed column and compare. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks** **Identification of controlling resistance** (3 marks): Using the two-film theory: $$\dfrac{1}{K_{Lx}} = \dfrac{1}{k_{Lx}} + \dfrac{m}{k_{Lx}^w}$$ - Water-side film resistance $m/k_{Lx}^w \to 0$ (water is pure, large bulk concentration gradient, $k_{Lx}^w$ is large). - Oil-side resistance dominates, $K_{Lx} \approx k_{Lx} \approx 0.0028\,\text{s}^{-1}/a$ × $a$ → **oil phase controls**. **NTU calculation** (4 marks): $$\text{NTU} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_1 - x^{\ast}}{x_2 - x^{\ast}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.95 - 0.005}{0.10 - 0.005}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.945}{0.095}\right) = \ln(9.947) = 2.298$$ --- #### **Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Superficial velocity** (1 mark): $$A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.5)^2 = 0.1963\,\text{m}^2$$ $$v_{ox} = \dfrac{0.60/(870 \times 0.1963)}{0.15} = \dfrac{0.003516}{0.15} = 0.02344\,\text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — HTU and Z for spray** (2 marks): $$\text{HTU} = \dfrac{0.02344}{0.0028} = 8.37\,\text{m}$$ $$Z_{\text{spray}} = 8.37 \times 2.298 = 19.24\,\text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Packed column** (2 marks): $$Z_{\text{packed}} = N_{ideal}\times \text{HETS} = 3.0 \times 1.8 = 5.4\,\text{m}$$ **Step 4 — Comparison** (2 marks): The packed column requires only **5.4 m** vs. **19.2 m** for the spray column — a **~72 % reduction**. Packed columns achieve higher mass transfer efficiency because the packing provides larger, continuously renewed interfacial area and reduces back-mixing. Industrial tallow hydrolysis plants (post-1950s) therefore universally use **packed columns** (e.g., with Raschig rings, Pall rings, or structured Mellapak) rather than spray columns. --- ## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Reaction:** Tallow + 3 H₂O ⇌ 3 Fatty Acid + Glycerol; occurs in **oil phase**. - [x] **Configuration:** Tallow dispersed (top), water continuous (bottom), counter-current. - [x] **Controlling resistance:** **Oil-side (dispersed-phase) film** — because triglyceride is oil-soluble, water has very low solubility in oil, and water-side resistance is negligible. - [x] **Hatta number ≪ 1** → reaction does NOT enhance mass transfer; treat as **purely mass-transfer-limited**. - [x] **Equilibrium line is VERTICAL** at $x^{\ast} = x_{w,\text{sat}} \approx 0.005$ (since water is essentially pure). - [x] **Design equation:** $Z = \text{HTU} \times \text{NTU}$, where $\text{HTU} = v_{ox}/(K_{Lx}\,a)$ and $\text{NTU} = \ln[(x_1 - x^\ast)/(x_2 - x^\ast)]$. - [x] **Interfacial area:** $a = 6\phi_d/d_{32}$; **holdup** $\phi_d \approx 0.10\text{–}0.20$ in spray columns. - [x] **NTU formula** reduces to log form **only** because $x^{\ast} = \text{const}$. - [x] **Spray columns are inefficient** for tallow hydrolysis — back-mixing and coalescence are severe. Industrial columns are **packed** or **multi-stage with re-dispersion** (Colgate–Emery process). - [x] **Units check:** $K_{Lx}\,a$ in s⁻¹, $v_{ox}$ in m/s → HTU in m, NTU dimensionless → Z in m. - [x] **Operating temperature:** 200–250 °C (high T increases $k$ and $x^{\ast}$). - [x] **Glycerol** leaves with the water phase; **fatty acids** leave with the oil phase. - [x] **Watch for sign errors** in the driving force — use $(x - x^\ast)$ form, not $(x^\ast - x)$, when $x > x^\ast$. - [x] **Conversion to overall:** For tallow, $K_{Lx} \approx k_{Lx}$ (oil side controls) — explicitly state this for full KTU marks.

topics.title: Formulation of Partial Differential Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Formulation of Partial Differential Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** is a mathematical equation that involves an unknown function of two or more independent variables, together with its partial derivatives with respect to those variables. A PDE of order $n$ contains at least one partial derivative of order $n$, and no partial derivative of order greater than $n$ appears in the equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT525 Module 2)**
> A *Partial Differential Equation* is a relation connecting an unknown function $u(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ and its partial derivatives $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x_i}, \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}, \dots$ with the independent variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ and known constants or functions.

### 1.2 Basic Terminology

- **Dependent Variable:** $u = u(x, y, t)$ — the function we seek.
- **Independent Variables:** $x, y, t, \dots$ — the variables with respect to which differentiation is performed.
- **Order of a PDE:** The order of the highest partial derivative appearing in the equation.
- **Degree of a PDE:** The power of the highest order partial derivative, **after the equation has been rationalized** (cleared of fractions and radicals).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are recording the **temperature** of a long, thin metal rod at every point along its length and at every instant. The temperature $u(x, t)$ depends on **two** things: the spatial position $x$ and the time $t$. The rate at which heat flows along the rod is governed by a relation that ties together *how temperature changes with time* and *how temperature curves along its length*. Because two distinct rates of change (time and space) are linked in a single equation, the model is naturally a PDE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PDEs in Process Engineering?**
> Every transport phenomenon (heat conduction, mass diffusion, momentum transfer, wave propagation, fluid flow) is fundamentally multivariable. The governing physical laws — Fourier's Law, Fick's Law, Newton's Law of Viscosity — when combined with conservation principles, inevitably produce PDEs. In KTU's PECHT525, PDEs are the language used to describe **steady-state and transient behaviour** of chemical process equipment.

### 1.4 Standard Canonical PDEs in Engineering

| Name | PDE Form | Engineering Meaning |
|------|----------|---------------------|
| **Laplace Equation** | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$ | Steady-state temperature / potential distribution |
| **Poisson Equation** | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} = f(x,y)$ | Steady-state with source term |
| **Heat Equation** | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha\, \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}$ | 1-D transient heat conduction |
| **Wave Equation** | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2}\, \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Vibration of string / sound waves |
| **Continuity Equation** | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ | Mass conservation in a flow field |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of PDEs

A general second-order PDE in two independent variables $x$ and $y$ can be written as:

$$A(x,y)\, \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + 2B(x,y)\, \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x \partial y} + C(x,y)\, \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} + \text{lower order terms} = 0$$

The **discriminant** $\Delta = B^{2} - AC$ classifies the PDE:

- **Elliptic ($\Delta < 0$):** Laplace and Poisson equations — *equilibrium problems*.
- **Parabolic ($\Delta = 0$):** Heat equation — *diffusion / time-marching problems*.
- **Hyperbolic ($\Delta > 0$):** Wave equation — *vibration / propagation problems*.

### 2.2 Two Principal Methods of Formulation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — High-Yield Topic**
> The syllabus emphasizes **two methods of forming PDEs**:
> 1. **Elimination of Arbitrary Constants**
> 2. **Elimination of Arbitrary Functions**

---

#### Method 1 — Elimination of Arbitrary Constants

When a complete integral $f(x, y, z, a, b) = 0$ is given with $n$ arbitrary constants, the resulting PDE will be of **order $n$**.

**Procedure:**
1. Differentiate the given relation **partially** $n$ times with respect to each independent variable $x$ and $y$.
2. From the original equation and the generated derivatives, **eliminate** the $n$ arbitrary constants.
3. The resulting relation is the desired PDE.

---

#### Method 2 — Elimination of Arbitrary Functions

When a relation $f(u, x, y, z, F_1, F_2, \dots) = 0$ contains **arbitrary functions**, the resulting PDE will be of an order equal to the **number of arbitrary functions**.

**Procedure:**
1. Differentiate the given relation partially with respect to each independent variable as many times as the number of arbitrary functions.
2. Eliminate the arbitrary functions using the original equation and the derived equations.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Remarks |
|---------|---------------|---------|
| **Order of PDE** | Highest order of partial derivative present | Determines complexity |
| **Degree of PDE** | Power of highest-order derivative (after rationalization) | Defined only for polynomial PDEs |
| **Linear PDE** | Dependent variable and its derivatives appear to power 1 | Coefficients may depend on independent variables |
| **Quasi-linear** | Linear in highest-order derivatives | Coefficients may depend on $u$ and lower derivatives |
| **Non-linear** | Non-linear in highest-order derivatives | Hardest to solve |
| **Eliminating $n$ constants** | PDE order $= n$ | Need $n$ partial derivative equations |
| **Eliminating $m$ functions** | PDE order $= m$ | Differentiate $m$ times and combine |
| **Homogeneous Linear PDE** | $L[u] = 0$ where $L$ is linear operator | **Superposition principle** applies |
| **Canonical 1st-order Linear PDE** | $P\,\partial u/\partial x + Q\,\partial u/\partial y = R$ | Solvable by Lagrange's method |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Classification by Linearity**
> KTU examiners frequently test whether a student can identify a PDE as *linear, quasi-linear, or non-linear*. The classification is based on the **highest-order derivative** only.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Process Engineering

- **Heat Exchanger Design:** The transient temperature profile along a tube is described by the 1-D heat equation; PDE formulation is the first step before solving with separation of variables or Laplace transforms.
- **Reactor Modelling:** Coupled PDE systems describe simultaneous mass and heat transport in catalytic fixed-bed reactors.
- **Distillation Columns:** The PDE governing composition profiles helps in predicting separation efficiency.
- **Fluid Mechanics:** Navier-Stokes equations (a system of non-linear PDEs) govern momentum transfer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Examples Aligned to KTU Module 2 Pattern**
> Every step below is shown exhaustively — no algebraic jumps are permitted by KTU valuation norms.

---

### **Example 1 — Eliminate Arbitrary Constants**

**Given:** $z = (x + a)(y + b)$, where $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary constants.

**Step 1 —** Differentiate partially with respect to $x$ (treating $y, a, b$ as constants):

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = (y + b)$$

**Step 2 —** Differentiate partially with respect to $y$ (treating $x, a, b$ as constants):

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = (x + a)$$

**Step 3 —** We have three equations:
- (i) $z = (x + a)(y + b)$
- (ii) $z_x = (y + b)$
- (iii) $z_y = (x + a)$

**Step 4 —** Eliminate $a$ and $b$. From (ii), $b = z_x - y$. From (iii), $a = z_y - x$.

**Step 5 —** Substituting $a$ and $b$ into (i):

$$z = (x + z_y - x)(y + z_x - y)$$

$$z = z_y \cdot z_x$$

**Step 6 —** Required PDE:

$$\boxed{\,pq - z = 0, \quad \text{where } p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x},\ q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}\,}$$

---

### **Example 2 — Eliminate Arbitrary Constants (Higher Order)**

**Given:** $z = ax + by + a^{2} + b^{2}$, with constants $a, b$.

**Step 1 —** Partial differentiation once with respect to $x$:

$$p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = a \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = p$$

**Step 2 —** Partial differentiation once with respect to $y$:

$$q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = b \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = q$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute $a = p$ and $b = q$ into $z = ax + by + a^{2} + b^{2}$:

$$z = p \cdot x + q \cdot y + p^{2} + q^{2}$$

**Step 4 —** Required PDE:

$$\boxed{\,z = px + qy + p^{2} + q^{2}\,}$$

This is a **Clairaut-type PDE** — a favourite KTU question.

---

### **Example 3 — Eliminate ONE Arbitrary Function**

**Given:** $z = f(x + y)$, where $f$ is an arbitrary function.

**Step 1 —** Differentiate partially with respect to $x$:

$$p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = f'(x + y) \cdot \frac{\partial(x+y)}{\partial x} = f'(x + y)$$

**Step 2 —** Differentiate partially with respect to $y$:

$$q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = f'(x + y) \cdot \frac{\partial(x+y)}{\partial y} = f'(x + y)$$

**Step 3 —** Equate the two expressions:

$$p = q$$

**Step 4 —** Required PDE:

$$\boxed{\,p - q = 0 \;\; \text{or equivalently} \;\; \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = 0\,}$$

The order of the PDE equals the number of arbitrary functions, which is **1**.

---

### **Example 4 — Eliminate TWO Arbitrary Functions**

**Given:** $z = f(x) + g(y)$, where $f$ and $g$ are two arbitrary functions.

**Step 1 —** Differentiate $z$ with respect to $x$ (twice — once because of $f$, once because of $g$; but $g(y)$ vanishes on $x$-differentiation):

$$p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = f'(x)$$

**Step 2 —** Differentiate $p$ again with respect to $x$:

$$r = \frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial x^{2}} = f''(x)$$

**Step 3 —** Differentiate $z$ with respect to $y$:

$$q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = g'(y)$$

**Step 4 —** Differentiate $q$ again with respect to $y$:

$$t = \frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial y^{2}} = g''(y)$$

**Step 5 —** The sum $r + t$ contains **no arbitrary function**:

$$r + t = f''(x) + g''(y)$$

**Step 6 —** Required PDE:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial y^{2}} = 0\,}$$

This is the famous **2-D Laplace equation**!

---

### **Example 5 — Eliminate Two Arbitrary Functions (Product Form)**

**Given:** $z = f(x) \cdot g(y)$.

**Step 1 —** Partial differentiation with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = f'(x) \cdot g(y) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 —** Partial differentiation with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = f(x) \cdot g'(y) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 —** Differentiate (i) again with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial x^{2}} = f''(x) \cdot g(y) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4 —** Differentiate (ii) again with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial y^{2}} = f(x) \cdot g''(y) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 5 —** Divide (i) by (ii):

$$\frac{\partial z / \partial x}{\partial z / \partial y} = \frac{f'(x) \cdot g(y)}{f(x) \cdot g'(y)}$$

This gives the ratio $\dfrac{p}{q}$.

**Step 6 —** Multiply (i) and (ii):

$$pq = f'(x)\, f(x)\, g'(y)\, g(y)$$

**Step 7 —** Required PDE (after combining):

$$\boxed{\,pq \cdot rs - p\,s\,r - q\,r\,s + r\,s = 0\,}$$

where $r = z_{xx}$, $s = z_{xy}$, $t = z_{yy}$.

---

### **Example 6 — Eliminate Two Functions in Sum**

**Given:** $z = f(2x + y) + g(2x - y)$.

**Step 1 —** $p = z_x = 2 f'(2x + y) + 2 g'(2x - y)$.

**Step 2 —** $q = z_y = f'(2x + y) - g'(2x - y)$.

**Step 3 —** $r = z_{xx} = 4 f''(2x+y) + 4 g''(2x-y)$.

**Step 4 —** $t = z_{yy} = f''(2x+y) + g''(2x-y)$.

**Step 5 —** Forming $r - 4t$:

$$r - 4t = 4[f''+g''] - 4[f''+g''] = 0$$

**Step 6 —** Required PDE:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial x^{2}} - 4\,\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial y^{2}} = 0\,}$$

This is the **1-D Wave equation** with wave speed $c = 2$.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Selection

| Form of Given Relation | Strategy | Resulting PDE Order |
|------------------------|----------|---------------------|
| $f(x,y,z,a,b) = 0$ with $n$ constants | Differentiate $n$ times, eliminate constants | $n$ |
| $z = f(ax+by) + g(ax-by)$ | Cross-derivative elimination | 2 |
| $z = F(u,v)$ where $u,v$ are linear in $x,y,z$ | Lagrange's linear PDE method | 1 |
| $z = f(x) + g(y)$ | $z_{xx} + z_{yy} = 0$ pattern | 2 |
| $z = f(x) \cdot g(y)$ | Multiplicative elimination | 2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — PDE Formulation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Relation] --> B{Type of Given Relation}
    B -- Arbitrary Constants --> C[Method 1: Eliminate Constants]
    B -- Arbitrary Functions --> D[Method 2: Eliminate Functions]
    C --> E[Differentiate n times partially]
    D --> F[Differentiate m times partially]
    E --> G[Generate n+1 Equations]
    F --> H[Generate m+1 Equations]
    G --> I[Algebraic Elimination]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final PDE Obtained]
    J --> K[Classify: Order, Degree, Linear Type]
    K --> L[End]
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of PDEs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[PDE] --> O{Order}
    O -- 1st order --> O1[Linear First Order]
    O -- 1st order --> O2[Non-linear First Order]
    O -- 2nd order --> O3[Elliptic]
    O -- 2nd order --> O4[Parabolic]
    O -- 2nd order --> O5[Hyperbolic]
    O3 --> E1[Laplace / Poisson]
    O4 --> E2[Heat Equation]
    O5 --> E3[Wave Equation]
    P --> Q{Linearity}
    Q -- Linear --> L1[Coefficients depend only on independent vars]
    Q -- Quasi-linear --> L2[Linear in highest derivatives only]
    Q -- Non-linear --> L3[Non-linear in highest derivatives]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Method-of-Elimination Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        IN1[Given Function with n arbitrary constants]
        IN2[Given Function with m arbitrary functions]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Process Stage]
        PR1[Differentiate n times wrt x and y]
        PR2[Differentiate m times wrt x and y]
        PR3[Form system of n+1 or m+1 equations]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        OUT1[Eliminate constants or functions]
        OUT2[Final PDE of order n or m]
    end
    IN1 --> PR1 --> PR3 --> OUT1 --> OUT2
    IN2 --> PR2 --> PR3 --> OUT1 --> OUT2
```

### 4.4 Physical Realisation — Mapping to Engineering Phenomena

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PHENOM[Physical Phenomenon] --> H1[Heat Conduction]
    PHENOM --> H2[Wave Propagation]
    PHENOM --> H3[Fluid Flow]
    PHENOM --> H4[Mass Diffusion]
    H1 --> R1[Heat Equation - Parabolic]
    H2 --> R2[Wave Equation - Hyperbolic]
    H3 --> R3[Laplace / Navier-Stokes - Elliptic]
    H4 --> R4[Diffusion Equation - Parabolic]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> Students frequently:
> - Confuse **order** with **degree** of a PDE.
> - Forget to **rationalize** (clear fractions & radicals) before assigning degree.
> - Misjudge whether to differentiate once or twice for eliminating arbitrary **functions** (the number of arbitrary **functions** dictates the required order of differentiation).
> - Lose **2 marks** in Part B by not writing the final PDE in standard $p, q, r, s, t$ notation.
> - Omit the **classification step** (linear / quasi-linear / non-linear) when explicitly asked.

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define a Partial Differential Equation. Classify the equation $\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2} \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}$ by order, degree, and linearity.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** is an equation containing one or more partial derivatives of an unknown function of two or more independent variables.

For the given equation:

- **Order:** $2$ (highest derivative is $\partial^{2}/\partial t^{2}$).
- **Degree:** $1$ (highest derivative appears to power 1).
- **Linearity:** **Linear** — the dependent variable $u$ and all its derivatives appear to the first power, and coefficients ($c^{2}$ and $1$) depend only on constants, not on $u$.

This is the classical **1-D wave equation** — *hyperbolic* in nature.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Form the PDE by eliminating the arbitrary function from $z = f(x^{2} + y^{2})$.*

**Model Answer:**

Given $z = f(x^{2} + y^{2})$.

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$$p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = f'(x^{2} + y^{2}) \cdot 2x$$

Differentiating with respect to $y$:

$$q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = f'(x^{2} + y^{2}) \cdot 2y$$

Eliminating $f'(x^{2} + y^{2})$ by dividing:

$$\frac{p}{q} = \frac{2x}{2y} = \frac{x}{y}$$

Required PDE:

$$\boxed{\,yp - xq = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad y\frac{\partial z}{\partial x} - x\frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = 0\,}$$

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Form the PDE by eliminating the arbitrary constants $a$ and $b$ from
$z = a x^{2} + b y^{2}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Form the PDE by eliminating the arbitrary functions $f$ and $g$ from
$z = f(x + ct) + g(x - ct)$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Given: $z = a x^{2} + b y^{2}$, with two arbitrary constants. The resulting PDE must be of order 2.

**Step 1:** Differentiate partially with respect to $x$:

$$p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = 2ax$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $p$ with respect to $x$ again:

$$r = \frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial x^{2}} = 2a$$

So $a = \dfrac{r}{2}$.

**Step 3:** Differentiate partially with respect to $y$:

$$q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = 2by$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $q$ with respect to $y$ again:

$$t = \frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial y^{2}} = 2b$$

So $b = \dfrac{t}{2}$.

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the original equation $z = ax^{2} + by^{2}$:

$$z = \frac{r}{2} x^{2} + \frac{t}{2} y^{2}$$

**Step 6:** Final PDE in standard notation:

$$\boxed{\,2z = x^{2}\, r + y^{2}\, t \quad \text{i.e.,}\quad x^{2}\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial x^{2}} + y^{2}\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial y^{2}} = 2z\,}$$

This PDE is **linear** and **second-order**.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $z = f(x + ct) + g(x - ct)$, with two arbitrary functions. The resulting PDE must be of order 2.

**Step 1:** Differentiate with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = f'(x + ct) + g'(x - ct)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial t} = c f'(x + ct) - c g'(x - ct)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $\partial z / \partial t$ again with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2} f''(x + ct) + c^{2} g''(x - ct) = c^{2}\left[f'' + g''\right]$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $\partial z / \partial x$ again with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial x^{2}} = f''(x + ct) + g''(x - ct) = f'' + g''$$

**Step 5:** Compare:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2} \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial x^{2}}$$

**Step 6:** Required PDE:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2}\, \frac{\partial^{2} z}{\partial x^{2}}\,}$$

This is the **1-D wave equation** describing vibration of a stretched string with wave speed $c$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Form the PDE by eliminating the constants $a$ and $b$ from
$z = \log(x^{2} + y^{2}) + ax + by$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Form the PDE by eliminating the arbitrary function from
$z = f(xy) + g(x/y)$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Given $z = \log(x^{2} + y^{2}) + ax + by$.

**Step 1:** Differentiate with respect to $x$:

$$p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = \frac{2x}{x^{2} + y^{2}} + a$$

Hence $a = p - \dfrac{2x}{x^{2} + y^{2}}$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $y$:

$$q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = \frac{2y}{x^{2} + y^{2}} + b$$

Hence $b = q - \dfrac{2y}{x^{2} + y^{2}}$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into $z = \log(x^{2} + y^{2}) + ax + by$:

$$z = \log(x^{2} + y^{2}) + x\left(p - \frac{2x}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\right) + y\left(q - \frac{2y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$z = \log(x^{2} + y^{2}) + xp + yq - \frac{2x^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}} - \frac{2y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}$$

$$z = \log(x^{2} + y^{2}) + xp + yq - 2$$

**Step 5:** Final PDE:

$$\boxed{\,z = xp + yq + \log(x^{2} + y^{2}) - 2\,}$$

This is a **first-order linear PDE** (in $p$ and $q$).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given $z = f(xy) + g(x/y)$, two arbitrary functions. PDE must be of order 2.

**Step 1:** Differentiate with respect to $x$:

$$p = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = y f'(xy) + \frac{1}{y} g'(x/y)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $y$:

$$q = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = x f'(xy) - \frac{x}{y^{2}} g'(x/y)$$

**Step 3:** Form $yp$ and $(y^{2}/x) q$ to eliminate $g'$:

$$y p = y^{2} f'(xy) + g'(x/y)$$

$$\frac{y^{2}}{x} q = y^{2} f'(xy) - g'(x/y)$$

**Step 4:** Adding:

$$yp + \frac{y^{2}}{x} q = 2 y^{2} f'(xy)$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate $yp + \dfrac{y^{2}}{x} q$ with respect to $x$ (to eliminate $f'$):

$$y r + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x} q\right) = 2 y^{2}\!\left[y f''(xy)\right]$$

After full expansion and simplification, the resulting PDE is:

$$\boxed{\,x^{2}\, r - 2xy\, s - y^{2}\, t + 2x\, p = 0\,}$$

where $r = z_{xx}$, $s = z_{xy}$, $t = z_{yy}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to differentiate **after the first elimination** to remove the **second** arbitrary function. Always remember — *number of arbitrary functions = order of PDE* — and you must differentiate enough times to algebraically eliminate every single arbitrary function.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- [x] **Definition:** A PDE involves partial derivatives of a function of multiple variables.
- [x] **Order:** Highest order of partial derivative present.
- [x] **Degree:** Power of highest-order derivative, *after rationalization*.
- [x] **Linearity:** Check the *highest-order* derivative only.
- [x] **Method 1 (constants):** Given $n$ arbitrary constants $\Rightarrow$ PDE of order $n$. Generate $n+1$ equations including the original.
- [x] **Method 2 (functions):** Given $m$ arbitrary functions $\Rightarrow$ PDE of order $m$. Each function elimination usually needs at least one differentiation step.
- [x] **Standard shorthand:** $p = z_x$, $q = z_y$, $r = z_{xx}$, $s = z_{xy}$, $t = z_{yy}$.
- [x] **Canonical forms:** Laplace (elliptic), Heat (parabolic), Wave (hyperbolic).
- [x] **Engineering relevance:** Heat conduction $\to$ parabolic; Steady temperature $\to$ elliptic; Vibrations $\to$ hyperbolic.
- [x] **Final PDE format:** Always present the answer in standard $p, q, r, s, t$ notation unless otherwise asked.
- [x] **Sanity check:** Verify by substituting the original relation back — the original should satisfy the derived PDE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsteady State Heat Conduction in One Dimension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unsteady State Heat Conduction in One Dimension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Unsteady State (Transient) Heat Conduction** is the mode of heat transfer in which the temperature at any point inside a solid medium varies with **time** as well as position. Mathematically, it is governed by the partial differential equation obtained by combining Fourier's law of heat conduction with the first law of thermodynamics applied to a differential control volume:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \nabla^{2} T + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{\rho \, c_{p}}$$

For one-dimensional conduction (no internal heat generation), this reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_{p}}$ is the **thermal diffusivity** (m²/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Whenever the temperature field $T(x,t)$ is a function of *both* space *and* time, the process is classified as **unsteady (transient)**. This is in direct contrast to **steady-state** conduction, where $\partial T / \partial t = 0$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea-Cooling Experiment"

Imagine pouring a fresh cup of boiling tea into a ceramic mug sitting on a wooden table.

* **At t = 0:** The tea is uniformly at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$.
* **At t = 5 min:** The liquid near the wall is cooler, but the centre is still hot. A **temperature gradient** exists.
* **At t = 30 min:** The whole mug is at room temperature, and the gradient has vanished.

During the middle phase (between 5 and 30 minutes), the temperature is changing **both in space and in time** — this is exactly unsteady-state heat conduction. The thermal energy is being "stored" within the mug while simultaneously being "lost" to the surroundings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** Steady state = the system has "forgotten" its initial condition. Unsteady state = the system is **still remembering** its initial condition while being driven toward a new equilibrium.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics (Bolded)

* **Thermal diffusivity, $\alpha$** — measures how quickly a material conducts heat *relative* to how quickly it stores heat. Units: **m²/s**.
* **Thermal conductivity, $k$** — Units: **W/(m·K)**.
* **Specific heat capacity, $c_p$** — Units: **J/(kg·K)**.
* **Density, $\rho$** — Units: **kg/m³**.
* **Convective heat transfer coefficient, $h$** — Units: **W/(m²·K)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Order-of-Magnitude Reference:** Metals have $\alpha \sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s (fast), while insulators like rubber have $\alpha \sim 10^{-7}$ m²/s (slow). For a 5 cm metal slab to fully equilibrate, $t \sim L^2/\alpha \sim (0.05)^2 / 10^{-5} \sim 250$ seconds.

---

### 1.4 Three Classical Regimes of Unsteady Conduction

| Regime | When it applies | Defining parameter |
|---|---|---|
| **Lumped Capacitance** | Internal conduction is infinitely fast | Bi $\ll$ 1 |
| **Semi-Infinite Solid** | Heat has not yet penetrated the far face | Fo small OR depth probed is small |
| **Bounded Bodies (Slab / Cylinder / Sphere)** | Heat has reached the far boundary | Fo $> 0.2$ and Bi $> 0.1$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Foundation — Why Do We Need Unsteady Analysis?

In real process engineering, almost all industrial start-ups, shut-downs, batch operations, and thermal cycling involve transients. Examples include:

1. **Start-up of a reformer furnace** in a petrochemical plant.
2. **Sterilization of canned food** in an autoclave (pasteurization kinetics).
3. **Cooling of ingots** during steel manufacturing.
4. **Annealing of glass** in optical lens production.

In each case, the engineer must predict **how long** the process will take, what the **maximum thermal stress** will be, and whether the **final temperature uniformity** is acceptable.

---

### 2.2 The Governing PDE — Logical Derivation Outline

Step-by-step logic to obtain the 1-D unsteady heat equation:

1. Apply **Fourier's law** at faces of a thin slab of thickness $dx$:
   - Heat in (left face) = $-k A \left. \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right|_x$
   - Heat out (right face) = $-k A \left. \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right|_{x+dx}$

2. Net heat accumulated = difference between inflow and outflow.

3. Equate accumulated energy to the rate of change of internal energy:
   - $\rho \, c_p \, A \, dx \, \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = $ net conduction in.

4. Take the limit $dx \to 0$ to obtain the **general 1-D heat equation**:
   $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

---

### 2.3 Non-Dimensionalization — The Heart of KTU Problems

To make the PDE dimensionless, define:

$$\theta = \frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}}, \quad X = \frac{x}{L}, \quad \text{Fo} = \frac{\alpha t}{L^{2}}, \quad \text{Bi} = \frac{h L}{k}$$

The dimensionless form of the PDE becomes:

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \text{Fo}} = \frac{\partial^{2} \theta}{\partial X^{2}}$$

subject to:

* IC: $\theta(X, 0) = 1$
* BC1: $-k \left. \dfrac{\partial \theta}{\partial x} \right|_{x=L} = h \, \theta(L, t)$
* BC2: $\left. \dfrac{\partial \theta}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} = 0$ (symmetry)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why non-dimensionalize?** Because the resulting solution depends **only** on Bi, Fo, and geometry — *not* on $k$, $\rho$, $c_p$, $h$, or $L$ individually. This allows us to build universal charts (Heisler charts).

---

### 2.4 Three-Digit Watch-Callouts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lumped Capacitance** — assumes $T(x,t) = T(t)$ only, i.e., temperature is **spatially uniform** within the body at every instant. Valid only when $Bi < 0.1$.
>
> **Semi-Infinite Solid** — the body is so deep that the heat pulse never reaches the far boundary during the time of interest.
>
> **Bounded Geometry** — use the eigenfunction (series) solution; Heisler charts are graphical aids.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Where it is used | Critical assumptions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$ | Definition of thermal diffusivity | Pure material property |
| 2 | $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h L_c}{k}$ | Ratio of internal conduction to surface convection resistance | $L_c$ = characteristic length |
| 3 | $\text{Fo} = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L_c^{2}}$ | Dimensionless time | $> 0.2$ for "long time" |
| 4 | $\dfrac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} = \exp(-\text{Bi} \cdot \text{Fo})$ | Lumped capacitance solution | $\text{Bi} < 0.1$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{T - T_s}{T_i - T_s} = \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$ | Semi-infinite solid with step change | Constant surface $T_s$ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_i} = A_1 e^{-\zeta_1^{2}\text{Fo}} \cos(\zeta_1 X) + \dots$ | Plane wall series solution | Bounded body |
| 7 | $\zeta_n \tan \zeta_n = \text{Bi}$ | Eigenvalue equation for plane wall | $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ |
| 8 | $1 - \zeta_n^{2} = \dfrac{\text{Bi}}{\text{Bi}+1}$ relation for $n=1$ | First eigenvalue approximation | Valid for moderate Bi |
| 9 | $A_n = \dfrac{4 \sin \zeta_n}{2\zeta_n + \sin(2\zeta_n)}$ | Coefficient for plane wall | First term dominates |
| 10 | $q''(t) = \dfrac{k(T_s - T_{\infty})}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha t}}$ | Instantaneous heat flux, semi-infinite | Step change at $x=0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always compute $\text{Bi}$ first to decide which regime applies. If $\text{Bi} < 0.1$, use lumped (Eq. 4). If not, choose the appropriate bounded-body solution.

---

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

* **Safety analysis** of nuclear reactor fuel rods during LOCA (loss-of-coolant accident).
* **Design of regenerators** in glass furnaces.
* **Optimization of batch sterilization** time in food processing — governed by the slowest-heating point's lethality.
* **Thermal management** of Li-ion battery packs to prevent thermal runaway.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Lumped Capacitance Solution

**Physical setup:** A small solid body of volume $V$, surface area $A_s$, initial temperature $T_i$, suddenly immersed in a fluid at $T_{\infty}$ with convective coefficient $h$. Assume $T$ is uniform inside the body.

**Energy balance over an infinitesimal time $dt$:**

$$\rho V c_p \, dT = -h A_s \, (T - T_{\infty}) \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to separate variables:**

$$\frac{dT}{T - T_{\infty}} = -\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Define the time constant (thermal time constant):**

$$\tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s} = \frac{\rho c_p L_c}{h} = \frac{L_c^{2}}{\alpha \, \text{Bi}}$$

where the characteristic length is $L_c = V / A_s$.

**Step 3 — Integrate from $T_i$ at $t=0$ to $T$ at time $t$:**

$$\int_{T_i}^{T} \frac{dT'}{T' - T_{\infty}} = -\frac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back $\tau = L_c^{2}/(\alpha \, \text{Bi})$ and $\text{Fo} = \alpha t / L_c^{2}$:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T(t) - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} = \exp(-\text{Bi} \cdot \text{Fo})\;}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The temperature decays exponentially. A larger Bi (better surface convection relative to internal conduction) accelerates cooling. A larger Fo (more time has passed) drives the system toward $T_{\infty}$.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Semi-Infinite Solid with Constant Surface Temperature

**Setup:** A semi-infinite body occupying $x \geq 0$, initial temperature $T_i$, surface at $x=0$ suddenly raised to $T_s$ and held constant.

**Governing PDE:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

**Initial and boundary conditions:**

* IC: $T(x, 0) = T_i$
* BC1: $T(0, t) = T_s$
* BC2: $T(x \to \infty, t) = T_i$

**Step 1 — Introduce the similarity variable:**

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}$$

Under this change of variables, the PDE reduces to an **ordinary** differential equation:

$$\frac{d^{2} \theta}{d \eta^{2}} + 2 \eta \, \frac{d \theta}{d \eta} = 0$$

where $\theta(\eta) = T - T_i$.

**Step 2 — First integration:**

Let $p = d\theta/d\eta$. Then:

$$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2 \eta p = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dp}{p} = -2 \eta \, d\eta$$

Integrating:

$$\ln p = -\eta^{2} + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = A \, e^{-\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Second integration:**

$$\theta(\eta) = A \int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}} du + B$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions:**

* At $\eta = 0$ (i.e., $x = 0$): $\theta = T_s - T_i$, so $B = T_s - T_i$.
* As $\eta \to \infty$: $\theta \to 0$, so:
  $$0 = A \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}} du + (T_s - T_i) = A \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} + (T_s - T_i)$$
  $$\Rightarrow A = -\frac{2(T_s - T_i)}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

**Step 5 — Final solution:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T(x,t) - T_s}{T_i - T_s} = \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)\;}$$

**Step 6 — Instantaneous surface heat flux:**

$$q''(t) = -k \left. \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} = \frac{k (T_s - T_i)}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha t}}$$

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Plane Wall Series Solution (Separation of Variables)

**Setup:** Plane wall of thickness $2L$, initially at $T_i$, suddenly exposed to fluid at $T_{\infty}$ with convection $h$ on both faces.

**Dimensionless PDE in $\theta$, $X$, Fo:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} \theta}{\partial X^{2}} = \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \text{Fo}}$$

**Boundary and initial conditions:**

* IC: $\theta(X, 0) = 1$
* BC at $X = 0$: $\partial \theta / \partial X = 0$ (symmetry)
* BC at $X = 1$: $-\partial \theta / \partial X = \text{Bi} \, \theta$

**Step 1 — Assume separated solution:**

$$\theta(X, \text{Fo}) = F(X) \, G(\text{Fo})$$

Substituting into the PDE:

$$\frac{F''(X)}{F(X)} = \frac{G'(\text{Fo})}{G(\text{Fo})} = -\zeta^{2}$$

(constant of separation is taken negative so that $G$ decays in time).

**Step 2 — Time ODE:**

$$G'(\text{Fo}) = -\zeta^{2} G \quad \Rightarrow \quad G(\text{Fo}) = e^{-\zeta^{2} \text{Fo}}$$

**Step 3 — Space ODE:**

$$F''(X) + \zeta^{2} F(X) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F(X) = C_1 \cos(\zeta X) + C_2 \sin(\zeta X)$$

**Step 4 — Apply BC at $X = 0$:**

$$F'(0) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = 0$$

So $F(X) = C_1 \cos(\zeta X)$.

**Step 5 — Apply BC at $X = 1$:**

$$-F'(1) = \text{Bi} \, F(1)$$

$$C_1 \zeta \sin \zeta = \text{Bi} \, C_1 \cos \zeta$$

$$\boxed{\;\zeta \tan \zeta = \text{Bi}\;}$$

This transcendental equation has **infinitely many real roots** $\zeta_1 < \zeta_2 < \dots < \zeta_n$.

**Step 6 — Superposition (series solution):**

$$\theta(X, \text{Fo}) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \cos(\zeta_n X) \, e^{-\zeta_n^{2} \text{Fo}}$$

**Step 7 — Apply IC to determine $A_n$:**

At $\text{Fo} = 0$:

$$1 = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \cos(\zeta_n X)$$

By orthogonality of $\cos(\zeta_n X)$:

$$A_n = \frac{\int_{0}^{1} \cos(\zeta_n X) \, dX}{\int_{0}^{1} \cos^{2}(\zeta_n X) \, dX}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate integrals:**

$$\int_{0}^{1} \cos(\zeta_n X) \, dX = \frac{\sin \zeta_n}{\zeta_n}$$

$$\int_{0}^{1} \cos^{2}(\zeta_n X) \, dX = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \frac{\sin(2\zeta_n)}{2\zeta_n}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\;A_n = \frac{4 \sin \zeta_n}{2 \zeta_n + \sin(2\zeta_n)}\;}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\theta}{\theta_i} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \, \cos(\zeta_n X) \, \exp(-\zeta_n^{2} \, \text{Fo})\;}$$

**For large Fo (long time), only the first term survives:**

$$\frac{\theta}{\theta_i} \approx A_1 \cos(\zeta_1 X) \, e^{-\zeta_1^{2} \text{Fo}}$$

This is the basis of the **Heisler chart** for plane walls.

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Cylinder and Sphere Eigenvalue Equations

By analogous separation of variables in cylindrical and spherical coordinates:

| Geometry | Eigenvalue Equation | First Coefficient |
|---|---|---|
| Infinite cylinder (radius $R$) | $\zeta_n J_0(\zeta_n) / J_1(\zeta_n) = \text{Bi}$ | $A_1 = \dfrac{2 J_1(\zeta_1)}{\zeta_1 [J_0^{2}(\zeta_1) + J_1^{2}(\zeta_1)]}$ |
| Solid sphere (radius $R$) | $1 - \zeta_n \cot \zeta_n = \text{Bi}$ | $A_1 = \dfrac{4 [\sin \zeta_1 - \zeta_1 \cos \zeta_1]}{2 \zeta_1 - \sin(2\zeta_1)}$ |

where $J_0$ and $J_1$ are Bessel functions of the first kind.

---

### 3.5 Summary of Analytical Matrix

| Problem Type | Math Method | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Bi $\ll$ 1 | Lumped ODE | Exponential decay |
| Semi-infinite | Similarity transformation | Error function |
| Bounded body | Eigenfunction expansion | Infinite series |
| Heisler chart usage | Tabulated Bi vs $\zeta_1$, $A_1$ | Direct graphical reading |
