## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Biot number and Fourier number. State their physical significance and the criteria under which the lumped capacitance method is valid.**

**Model Answer:**

* **Biot number, Bi = h Lc / k**: It is the ratio of internal conduction resistance to external convective resistance. Physically, it compares the temperature drop inside the solid to the temperature drop across the fluid boundary layer.
* **Fourier number, Fo = α t / Lc²**: It is the dimensionless time. It represents the ratio of the actual time elapsed to the characteristic thermal diffusion time $L_c^{2}/\alpha$.
* **Criterion for lumped capacitance:** The method is valid when **Bi < 0.1**, meaning the internal conduction resistance is negligible compared to the surface convection resistance, so the body has nearly uniform temperature at any instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocation:** 1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for the lumped criterion explanation.

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Derive an expression for the temperature distribution in a semi-infinite solid with its surface suddenly raised to a constant temperature $T_s$.**

**Model Answer:**

For a semi-infinite body occupying $x \geq 0$, with $T(x,0) = T_i$ and $T(0,t) = T_s$, applying the similarity transformation $\eta = x/(2\sqrt{\alpha t})$ reduces the heat equation to:

$$\frac{d^{2}\theta}{d\eta^{2}} + 2\eta \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = 0$$

Integrating twice with BCs and using $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-u^2} du = \sqrt{\pi}/2$, the solution is:

$$\frac{T(x,t) - T_s}{T_i - T_s} = \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocation:** 1 mark for setup, 1 mark for similarity reduction, 1 mark for the final closed-form result.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Starting from the general 1-D heat conduction equation, derive the **lumped capacitance** formula for a solid body cooling in a fluid. Clearly state the assumptions involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel ball of diameter **8 cm** initially at a uniform temperature of **600 °C** is suddenly quenched in an oil bath at **30 °C**. The convective heat transfer coefficient is **500 W/m²·K**. The properties of steel are: $k = 45 \text{ W/m·K}$, $\rho = 7800 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 480 \text{ J/kg·K}$. Estimate: **(i)** the time required for the centre of the ball to cool to **100 °C**, and **(ii)** the instantaneous rate of heat removal from the ball at that instant. **(7 Marks)**

##### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. Energy balance on the lumped body (assume uniform temperature $T(t)$):

$$\rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = -h A_s (T - T_{\infty})$$

2. Assume constant $h$, $T_{\infty}$, and properties. Separate variables:

$$\frac{dT}{T - T_{\infty}} = -\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} dt$$

3. Integrate from $T_i$ at $t=0$ to $T$ at time $t$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau}, \quad \text{where } \tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s}$$

4. Substitute $\tau = L_c / (h/(\rho c_p)) = L_c^2/(\alpha \cdot \text{Bi})$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} = \exp(-\text{Bi} \cdot \text{Fo})\;}$$

5. **Assumptions (1 mark each):** (i) Uniform internal temperature, (ii) Constant $h$, (iii) Constant properties, (iv) Bi $< 0.1$, (v) No internal heat generation, (vi) Negligible radiation.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given data:
* Diameter $D = 0.08$ m, so $R = 0.04$ m.
* For a sphere: $L_c = V/A_s = R/3 = 0.04/3 = 0.01333$ m.
* $T_i = 600^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_{\infty} = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$, $h = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$.
* $k = 45$, $\rho = 7800$, $c_p = 480$.

**Step 1 — Check Bi validity:**

$$\text{Bi} = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{500 \times 0.01333}{45} = 0.148$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Warning #1:** $\text{Bi} = 0.148 > 0.1$, so the **lumped capacitance method is borderline invalid**. For KTU, students should report this and either use Heisler chart or note the approximation. Strict interpretation: use Heisler for sphere. Here we proceed with the exponential for the demonstration, then estimate.

**Step 2 — Compute thermal diffusivity:**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{45}{7800 \times 480} = 1.202 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply lumped formula (approx):**

$$\frac{T - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} = \frac{100 - 30}{600 - 30} = \frac{70}{570} = 0.1228$$

$$\exp(-\text{Bi} \cdot \text{Fo}) = 0.1228 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Bi} \cdot \text{Fo} = -\ln(0.1228) = 2.097$$

$$\text{Fo} = \frac{2.097}{0.148} = 14.17$$

$$t = \frac{\text{Fo} \cdot L_c^{2}}{\alpha} = \frac{14.17 \times (0.01333)^2}{1.202 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{14.17 \times 1.778 \times 10^{-4}}{1.202 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{\;t \approx 209.5 \text{ seconds} \approx 3.5 \text{ minutes}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Heat removal rate at $t = 209.5$ s:**

$$q = h A_s (T - T_{\infty}) = h (4\pi R^2)(70)$$

$$A_s = 4 \pi (0.04)^2 = 0.02011 \text{ m}^2$$

$$q = 500 \times 0.02011 \times 70 = \boxed{\;704 \text{ W}\;}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Alternative]

**(a)** Derive the temperature distribution equation for a **plane wall of thickness $2L$** subjected to sudden convective cooling on both sides, using the method of separation of variables. Clearly state the eigenvalue equation and the coefficient formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A brick wall of thickness **20 cm**, initially at **30 °C**, is suddenly exposed on both sides to hot combustion gases at **800 °C** with $h = 25 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. The brick properties are $k = 0.7 \text{ W/m·K}$, $\rho = 1600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 840 \text{ J/kg·K}$. Determine the **temperature at the centre** after **4 hours**. Use the **Heisler chart** approach (with first-term approximation). **(7 Marks)**

##### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The dimensionless PDE and BCs (using $\theta = (T - T_\infty)/(T_i - T_\infty)$, $X = x/L$, Fo = $\alpha t/L^2$) are:

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \text{Fo}} = \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial X^2}$$

IC: $\theta(X,0) = 1$, BC at $X = 0$: $\partial \theta/\partial X = 0$, BC at $X = 1$: $-\partial \theta/\partial X = \text{Bi} \cdot \theta$.

Assume $\theta = F(X)G(\text{Fo})$. Separation gives:

$$\frac{F''}{F} = \frac{G'}{G} = -\zeta^2$$

Yielding $G = e^{-\zeta^2 \text{Fo}}$ and $F = C_1 \cos(\zeta X)$. The boundary conditions produce:

$$\boxed{\;\zeta \tan \zeta = \text{Bi}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;A_n = \frac{4 \sin \zeta_n}{2 \zeta_n + \sin(2\zeta_n)}\;}$$

And the final solution:

$$\frac{\theta}{\theta_i} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \cos(\zeta_n X) \exp(-\zeta_n^2 \text{Fo})$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given:
* $2L = 0.20$ m, so $L = 0.10$ m.
* $T_i = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_{\infty} = 800^{\circ}\text{C}$, $h = 25 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$.
* $k = 0.7$, $\rho = 1600$, $c_p = 840$.

**Step 1 — Characteristic length and dimensionless groups:**

$$L_c = L = 0.10 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Bi} = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{25 \times 0.10}{0.7} = 3.571$$

$$\alpha = \frac{0.7}{1600 \times 840} = 5.208 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Fo:**

$$t = 4 \text{ hours} = 14400 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{Fo} = \frac{\alpha t}{L^2} = \frac{5.208 \times 10^{-7} \times 14400}{(0.10)^2} = 0.75$$

**Step 3 — Read from Heisler chart for plane wall at $X = 0$ (centre):**

For Bi = 3.57, the Heisler chart gives:

* $\zeta_1 \approx 1.4575$ rad (solving $\zeta \tan \zeta = 3.57$)
* $A_1 \approx 1.2735$

**Step 4 — First-term approximation:**

$$\theta_0 / \theta_i = A_1 e^{-\zeta_1^2 \text{Fo}} = 1.2735 \times \exp(-(1.4575)^2 \times 0.75)$$

$$\zeta_1^2 = 2.124, \quad \zeta_1^2 \cdot \text{Fo} = 1.593$$

$$\theta_0 / \theta_i = 1.2735 \times e^{-1.593} = 1.2735 \times 0.2032 = 0.2588$$

**Step 5 — Compute actual temperature:**

$$\theta_0 = T_0 - T_{\infty}, \quad \theta_i = T_i - T_{\infty} = 30 - 800 = -770^{\circ}\text{C}$$

$$T_0 - 800 = 0.2588 \times (-770) = -199.3^{\circ}\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_0 \approx 600.7^{\circ}\text{C}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Bi validity:** When Bi $\gg 0.1$, lumped is invalid — must use Heisler chart or series. *(2 marks penalty if missed)*
> 2. **Sign convention:** $\theta_i = T_i - T_{\infty}$ is negative here (initial temp is below ambient). Students often forget this sign. *(1 mark penalty)*
> 3. **Fo check:** Always verify Fo $> 0.2$ before using long-time (one-term) Heisler approximation. *(1 mark penalty)*
> 4. **Series truncation:** For Fo $> 0.2$, only $n = 1$ needed. For small Fo, keep at least 4 terms. *(2 marks penalty)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unsteady State Heat Conduction in 1-D:**

* [ ] **PDE:** $\partial T / \partial t = \alpha \, \partial^2 T / \partial x^2$ for 1-D, no source.
* [ ] **Biot number, Bi = h Lc / k** — measures internal vs external resistance.
* [ ] **Fourier number, Fo = α t / Lc²** — dimensionless time.
* [ ] **Lumped valid if Bi < 0.1** — formula is $\exp(-\text{Bi} \cdot \text{Fo})$.
* [ ] **Semi-infinite solution:** uses error function $\text{erf}(\eta)$ where $\eta = x/(2\sqrt{\alpha t})$.
* [ ] **Penetration depth** in semi-infinite: $\delta \approx 4\sqrt{\alpha t}$ (≈ 95 % of temp change).
* [ ] **Plane wall eigenvalue equation:** $\zeta \tan \zeta = \text{Bi}$.
* [ ] **Cylinder eigenvalue equation:** $\zeta J_0(\zeta)/J_1(\zeta) = \text{Bi}$.
* [ ] **Sphere eigenvalue equation:** $1 - \zeta \cot \zeta = \text{Bi}$.
* [ ] **First coefficient for plane wall:** $A_n = 4 \sin \zeta_n / [2\zeta_n + \sin(2\zeta_n)]$.
* [ ] **Long-time (Fo > 0.2) approximation:** keep only $n = 1$ term.
* [ ] **Centerline (X = 0) simplification:** $\cos(0) = 1$, so $\theta_0 / \theta_i = A_1 e^{-\zeta_1^2 \text{Fo}}$.
* [ ] **Midplane symmetry:** for plane wall of half-thickness $L$, use $L_c = L$.
* [ ] **Cylinder/sphere characteristic length:** $L_c = R/2$ (cylinder) and $L_c = R/3$ (sphere).
* [ ] **Surface heat flux (semi-infinite):** $q''(t) = k(T_s - T_i)/\sqrt{\pi \alpha t}$.
* [ ] **Time constant (lumped):** $\tau = \rho V c_p / (h A_s)$.
* [ ] **Heisler chart** is the graphical alternative to the series solution.
* [ ] **Total heat transferred** can be obtained by integrating $q''(t)$ or via energy balance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Transfer with Axial Symmetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mass Transfer with Axial Symmetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mass Transfer with Axial Symmetry** (also referred to as *radial mass transfer* or *one-dimensional diffusion in curvilinear coordinates*) describes the transport of a chemical species by molecular diffusion in geometries where the concentration field $C(r, t)$ depends **only on the radial distance $r$ from the axis or centre and time $t$**. The system possesses *rotational symmetry* about a central axis (cylinder) or *spherical symmetry* about a point (sphere), so the gradient in the azimuthal ($\theta$) and axial ($z$) directions vanishes identically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Axial symmetry in mass transfer implies that the molar flux vector $\vec{N}_A$ has only a radial component $N_{A,r}$, and the governing continuity equation for species A reduces to a one-dimensional form in the radial coordinate $r$, after eliminating the angular terms by invoking $\partial C_A/\partial \theta = 0$ and $\partial C_A/\partial z = 0$.*

The **general continuity equation for species A** in a binary system, in the absence of homogeneous chemical reaction, is:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A = 0$$

For a **Fickian binary mixture with constant diffusivity $D_{AB}$** and negligible bulk flow (equimolar counter-diffusion), this becomes the classical **diffusion equation**:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A$$

When the Laplacian $\nabla^2$ is expanded in **cylindrical coordinates** $(r, \theta, z)$:

$$\nabla^2 = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2}{\partial z^2}$$

Applying the *axial symmetry* postulate ($\partial/\partial \theta = 0$ and $\partial/\partial z = 0$):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left[\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial r^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right]}$$

For **spherical coordinates** $(r, \theta, \phi)$ with full spherical symmetry:

$$\nabla^2 = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\right)$$

Which yields:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left[\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right]$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a long, thin bamboo stick (a cylinder). If you soak one end in dye, the colour spreads inward along the radial direction at every cross-section equally — there is no reason for the dye to spread more on one angular side than another. Because the stick is much longer than its diameter, end-effects (axial diffusion) are negligible. The system has *axial symmetry* — every cross-section looks identical, and within a cross-section the colour depends only on how far you are from the centre.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Examples of Axial Symmetry:**
> - **Catalytic pellets** (cylindrical or spherical): Diffusion of reactants into porous catalyst pores.
> - **Tubular membrane separators**: Radial diffusion of gases through hollow fibre walls.
> - **Drying of spherical droplets**: Water vapour diffusing outward through stagnant air.
> - **Drug release from spherical beads**: Pharmaceutical controlled-release kinetics.
> - **Spherical or cylindrical grains in a packed-bed reactor**: Internal diffusion in Fischer–Tropsch catalysts.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Molecular Diffusivity $D_{AB}$**: Typical range $\mathbf{10^{-5} \text{ to } 10^{-1} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}}$ for gases; $\mathbf{10^{-6} \text{ to } 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}}$ for liquids.
- **Characteristic Length $L_c$**: For a cylinder of radius $R$, $L_c = R$. For a sphere of radius $R$, $L_c = R$.
- **Fourier Number for Mass Transfer (Mass Fourier Number)** $\mathbf{Fo_m = D_{AB} t / L_c^2}$: Dimensionless time governing unsteady diffusion.
- **Biot Number for Mass Transfer** $\mathbf{Bi_m = k_c L_c / D_{AB}}$: Ratio of convective to diffusive resistance at the surface.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Reduction Mechanism — Why Axial Symmetry Matters

The Laplacian in three dimensions is *anisotropic* in cylindrical coordinates because of the $1/r$ and $1/r^2$ geometric factors. These factors arise from the fact that the area element of a cylindrical shell is $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$ (linear in $r$), while that of a spherical shell is $dA = 4\pi r^2$ (quadratic in $r$). Consequently:

> **For a cylinder:** area $\propto r$ → governing equation has form $\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}(r \frac{dC}{dr})$.
> **For a sphere:** area $\propto r^2$ → governing equation has form $\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}(r^2 \frac{dC}{dr})$.

These geometric factors make cylindrical and spherical diffusion fundamentally different from planar (Cartesian) diffusion, where the cross-sectional area is constant.

### 2.2 Classification of Problems

Mass transfer problems with axial symmetry are classified along three axes:

| Axis | Option 1 | Option 2 |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Geometry** | Cylinder ($\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}(r \frac{dC}{dr}) = 0$) | Sphere ($\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}(r^2 \frac{dC}{dr}) = 0$) |
| **Time-dependence** | Steady state ($\partial C/\partial t = 0$) | Unsteady state ($\partial C/\partial t \neq 0$) |
| **Boundary condition** | Constant surface conc. (Dirichlet) | Constant surface flux (Neumann) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises **every governing equation and solution** a KTU 2024 student must memorise for this topic.

| # | Geometry / Regime | Governing PDE | General Solution | Key Constants / Roots |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steady cylinder | $\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dC}{dr}\right)=0$ | $C(r) = A_1 \ln(r) + A_2$ | — |
| 2 | Steady sphere | $\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2\frac{dC}{dr}\right)=0$ | $C(r) = \frac{A_1}{r} + A_2$ | — |
| 3 | Unsteady cylinder | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\left(\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial r^2}+\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial C}{\partial r}\right)$ | $C(r,t) = \sum_n A_n J_0(\lambda_n r) \exp(-\lambda_n^2 D t)$ | $\lambda_n$ are roots of $J_0(\lambda R) = 0$; $\lambda_1 = 2.405/R$ |
| 4 | Unsteady sphere (linearised) | Substitution $u = rC$ gives $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = D\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial r^2}$ | $u(r,t) = \sum_n B_n \sin(n\pi r/R)\exp(-n^2\pi^2 D t/R^2)$ | $n = 1,2,3,\dots$ |
| 5 | Molar flux (radial) | $N_{A,r} = -D_{AB}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}$ | (Apply to solution of #1, #2) | Fick's First Law |
| 6 | Mass Biot Number | $Bi_m = \frac{k_c R}{D_{AB}}$ | — | Surface-resistance gauge |
| 7 | Mass Fourier Number | $Fo_m = \frac{D_{AB} t}{R^2}$ | — | Dimensionless time |
| 8 | Avg. unaccomplished change (sphere) | $\frac{\bar{C}-C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{6}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^2}\exp(-n^2\pi^2 Fo_m)$ | — | Used for batch sorption |
| 9 | Avg. unaccomplished change (cylinder) | $\frac{\bar{C}-C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{4}{\lambda_n^2 R^2}\exp(-\lambda_n^2 D t)$ | — | $J_0$ Bessel function used |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Alert:** In cylindrical problems the eigenvalues $\lambda_n$ are *not* integer multiples of $\pi$. They are the roots of the Bessel function $J_0$ and must be looked up from tables: $\lambda_1 R = 2.4048$, $\lambda_2 R = 5.5201$, $\lambda_3 R = 8.6537$. This is a frequent KTU mistake.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Specific Application |
|---|---|
| **Heterogeneous Catalysis** | Effectiveness factor $\eta$ of porous catalyst pellets uses the *Thiele modulus* $\phi = R\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$, derived from these equations. |
| **Membrane Separations** | Hollow-fibre gas permeation: design equation $J = (D_{AB}/\delta)\ln(C_{\text{inner}}/C_{\text{outer}})$. |
| **Pharmaceutical Engineering** | Drug release from spherical beads follows Higuchi/Hixon-Crowell models built on sphere diffusion. |
| **Food Processing** | Salt/fumigation penetration into cylindrical sausages or spherical fruits. |
| **Petroleum Engineering** | Pressure-pulse decay in cylindrical core samples; diffusion of hydrocarbons through reservoir rock. |

### 2.5 Conditions for Validity

The axially symmetric mass transfer model assumes:

1. **Constant diffusivity** $D_{AB}$ (independent of concentration).
2. **No bulk flow** OR *equimolar counter-diffusion* (so convective term vanishes).
3. **No homogeneous reaction** in the bulk (or reaction is treated separately via Thiele modulus).
4. **Isothermal system** (no thermal coupling with energy equation).
5. **Fick's First Law** is valid (binary, dilute, no pressure diffusion, no thermal diffusion).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Diffusion in a Hollow Cylinder

**Physical Setup:** Consider a long cylindrical tube of inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$. Species A diffuses radially outward. The inner wall is held at concentration $C_{A,1}$ and the outer wall at $C_{A,2}$, with $C_{A,1} > C_{A,2}$.

**Governing Equation (Steady-State):**

$$\frac{D_{AB}}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dC_A}{dr}\right) = 0$$

**Step 1 — Algebraic Simplification:**

Since $D_{AB} \neq 0$, the equation reduces to:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dC_A}{dr}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — First Integration:**

Integrate both sides with respect to $r$:

$$r\frac{dC_A}{dr} = A_1$$

**Explanation:** The integration constant $A_1$ emerges because the derivative of a constant is zero; this constant will later be fixed by the boundary conditions.

**Step 3 — Separation of Variables:**

Divide both sides by $r$:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dr} = \frac{A_1}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Second Integration:**

Integrate with respect to $r$:

$$C_A(r) = A_1 \ln(r) + A_2$$

**Explanation:** $\int \frac{1}{r} dr = \ln(r) + \text{const}$, giving the characteristic logarithmic profile unique to cylindrical geometries.

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**

$$\text{BC}_1: \quad C_A = C_{A,1} \text{ at } r = r_1 \implies C_{A,1} = A_1 \ln(r_1) + A_2$$

$$\text{BC}_2: \quad C_A = C_{A,2} \text{ at } r = r_2 \implies C_{A,2} = A_1 \ln(r_2) + A_2$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $A_1$ (Subtraction):**

Subtract BC$_2$ from BC$_1$:

$$C_{A,1} - C_{A,2} = A_1 \ln(r_1/r_2)$$

$$\boxed{A_1 = \frac{C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}}{\ln(r_1/r_2)}}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $A_2$ (Substitution):**

$$A_2 = C_{A,1} - A_1 \ln(r_1)$$

**Step 8 — Final Concentration Profile:**

$$\boxed{C_A(r) = C_{A,1} - \left(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}\right)\frac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Step 9 — Molar Flux via Fick's Law:**

$$N_{A,r} = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dr} = -D_{AB}\left(\frac{A_1}{r}\right)$$

$$\boxed{N_{A,r} = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})}{r \ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Step 10 — Logarithmic-Mean Area for Flux in terms of area:**

Total molar flow through the cylinder (per unit length, axial direction) is $\dot{n}_A = N_{A,r} \cdot 2\pi r$:

$$\dot{n}_A = \frac{2\pi D_{AB}(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}) \cdot 2\pi L}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

This can be rewritten using the **logarithmic mean area** $A_{lm} = 2\pi L (r_2 - r_1)/\ln(r_2/r_1)$ as:

$$\dot{n}_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}) A_{lm}}{r_2 - r_1}$$

This is the standard cylindrical form analogous to $q = kA\Delta T / \Delta x$ in heat transfer.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Diffusion in a Hollow Sphere

**Physical Setup:** Species A diffuses radially through a spherical shell of inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$, with $C_{A,1}$ at the inner surface and $C_{A,2}$ at the outer.

**Governing Equation:**

$$\frac{D_{AB}}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2\frac{dC_A}{dr}\right) = 0$$

**Step 1 — Reduce:**

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2\frac{dC_A}{dr}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — First Integration:**

$$r^2\frac{dC_A}{dr} = A_1$$

**Step 3 — Separation:**

$$\frac{dC_A}{dr} = \frac{A_1}{r^2}$$

**Step 4 — Second Integration:**

$$C_A(r) = -\frac{A_1}{r} + A_2 = \frac{B_1}{r} + A_2$$

where $B_1 = -A_1$.

**Step 5 — Boundary Conditions:**

$$C_A(r_1) = C_{A,1} \implies C_{A,1} = \frac{B_1}{r_1} + A_2$$

$$C_A(r_2) = C_{A,2} \implies C_{A,2} = \frac{B_1}{r_2} + A_2$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $B_1$:**

$$C_{A,1} - C_{A,2} = B_1\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) = B_1\frac{r_2 - r_1}{r_1 r_2}$$

$$\boxed{B_1 = \frac{(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}) r_1 r_2}{r_2 - r_1}}$$

**Step 7 — Final Concentration Profile:**

$$\boxed{C_A(r) = C_{A,2} + \frac{(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}) r_1 r_2}{r_2 - r_1}\left(\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)}$$

**Step 8 — Molar Flux:**

$$N_{A,r} = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dr} = \frac{D_{AB} B_1}{r^2} = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}) r_1 r_2}{(r_2 - r_1) r^2}$$

**Step 9 — Total Molar Flow Through Sphere (over entire surface):**

$$\dot{n}_A = N_{A,r} \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{4\pi D_{AB} r_1 r_2 (C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})}{r_2 - r_1}$$

Notice that for a sphere, the $r^2$ in the numerator and denominator **cancel exactly**, making the total molar rate **independent of $r$** — as required by steady-state mass conservation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Unsteady-State Diffusion in a Sphere (Linearisation Trick)

**Physical Setup:** A solid sphere of radius $R$, initially at uniform concentration $C_0$, is suddenly immersed in a fluid that maintains the surface concentration at $C_s$ (with $C_s > C_0$). Find $C(r, t)$.

**Governing PDE:**

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left(\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Multiply by $r$ to obtain the "convection-free" form:**

Multiply every term by $r$:

$$r\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left(r\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial r^2} + 2\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right) = D_{AB}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)$$

Wait — we need $\frac{\partial^2(rC_A)}{\partial r^2}$. Let us check:

$$\frac{\partial(rC_A)}{\partial r} = C_A + r\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2(rC_A)}{\partial r^2} = 2\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r} + r\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial r^2}$$

So:

$$r\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2(rC_A)}{\partial r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Define new variable $u(r,t) \equiv r \cdot C_A(r,t)$:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial r^2}$$

This is the **standard 1-D Cartesian heat/diffusion equation**! The spherical geometry has been transformed into a planar one.

**Step 3 — Boundary and Initial Conditions for $u$:**

| Condition | On $C_A$ | Transformed to $u = rC_A$ |
|---|---|---|
| Initial: $t=0$, $0 \le r \le R$ | $C_A = C_0$ | $u(r,0) = rC_0$ |
| Surface: $r = R$, $t > 0$ | $C_A = C_s$ | $u(R,t) = R C_s$ |
| Centre: $r = 0$, $t > 0$ | $C_A$ finite | $u(0,t) = 0$ (since $r \to 0$) |

**Step 4 — Non-dimensionalise:**

Let $\theta \equiv \frac{u - R C_s}{0 - R C_s} = 1 - \frac{u}{R C_s}$, so $\theta$ satisfies $\theta(0,t)=1$ at $r=0$ and $\theta(R,t) = 0$.

Equivalently, in terms of $C_A$:

$$\theta(r,t) = \frac{C_A(r,t) - C_s}{C_0 - C_s}$$

**Step 5 — Separation of Variables:**

Try $\theta(r,t) = F(r) G(t)$. Substituting into $\partial u/\partial t = D \partial^2 u/\partial r^2$:

$$\frac{1}{D}\frac{G'}{G} = \frac{F''}{F} = -\alpha^2$$

(Choosing negative sign for bounded physical solution.)

$$G(t) = e^{-\alpha^2 D t}, \quad F(r) = A\sin(\alpha r) + B\cos(\alpha r)$$

**Step 6 — Apply BC at $r=0$:**

$u(0,t) = 0 \implies \theta(0,t) = 1 - 0 = 1$, which means **constant**, requiring $F(0) = 0 \implies B = 0$. So:

$$F(r) = A\sin(\alpha r)$$

**Step 7 — Apply BC at $r=R$:**

$\theta(R,t) = 0 \implies F(R) = 0 \implies \sin(\alpha R) = 0 \implies \alpha R = n\pi$, giving $\alpha_n = n\pi/R$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$.

**Step 8 — General Series Solution:**

$$\theta(r,t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_n \sin\left(\frac{n\pi r}{R}\right)\exp\left(-\frac{n^2\pi^2 D_{AB} t}{R^2}\right)$$

**Step 9 — Determine $B_n$ from Initial Condition $\theta(r,0) = 1$:**

$$1 = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_n \sin\left(\frac{n\pi r}{R}\right)$$

Multiply by $\sin(m\pi r / R)$ and integrate over $[0,R]$, using orthogonality $\int_0^R \sin(n\pi r/R)\sin(m\pi r/R) dr = (R/2)\delta_{nm}$:

$$B_n = \frac{2}{R}\int_0^R \sin\left(\frac{n\pi r}{R}\right) dr = \frac{2}{R}\left[-\frac{R}{n\pi}\cos\left(\frac{n\pi r}{R}\right)\right]_0^R$$

$$B_n = \frac{2}{n\pi}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right] = \frac{2}{n\pi}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]$$

This yields $B_n = \frac{4}{n\pi}$ for odd $n$, and $B_n = 0$ for even $n$.

**Step 10 — Final Solution:**

$$\boxed{\frac{C_A - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{2R}{\pi r}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\sin\left(\frac{n\pi r}{R}\right)\exp\left(-\frac{n^2\pi^2 D_{AB} t}{R^2}\right)}$$

**Step 11 — Average Concentration in Sphere:**

Using the average $\bar{C} = \frac{3}{R^3}\int_0^R C(r,t) r^2 \, dr$ and simplification:

$$\boxed{\frac{\bar{C}_A - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{6}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^2}\exp\left(-\frac{n^2\pi^2 D_{AB} t}{R^2}\right)}$$

This is the celebrated **Newman solution for a sphere** — frequently quoted in KTU exams.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Unsteady-State Diffusion in a Long Cylinder (Bessel Function Method)

**Physical Setup:** A long solid cylinder of radius $R$, initially at $C_0$, suddenly placed in a fluid maintaining surface concentration $C_s$.

**Governing PDE:**

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left(\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial r^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Separation of Variables:**

Try $C(r,t) = C_s + F(r)G(t)$, where $F(R) = 0$ and $F(0)$ finite:

$$F(r)G'(t) = D_{AB}\left[F''(r) + \frac{1}{r}F'(r)\right]G(t)$$

$$\frac{G'}{D_{AB} G} = \frac{F'' + (1/r)F'}{F} = -\lambda^2$$

**Step 2 — Temporal ODE:**

$$G(t) = \exp(-\lambda^2 D_{AB} t)$$

**Step 3 — Spatial ODE (Bessel's Equation of Order Zero):**

$$F''(r) + \frac{1}{r}F'(r) + \lambda^2 F(r) = 0$$

This is Bessel's equation of order $\nu = 0$. Its bounded solution at $r = 0$ is:

$$F(r) = J_0(\lambda r)$$

where $J_0$ is the **Bessel function of the first kind, order zero**.

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Condition $F(R) = 0$:**

$$J_0(\lambda R) = 0$$

The roots are $\lambda_n R = \beta_n$ where $\beta_1 = 2.4048, \beta_2 = 5.5201, \beta_3 = 8.6537, \beta_4 = 11.7915, \ldots$

**Step 5 — General Series Solution:**

$$C(r,t) = C_s + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n J_0(\lambda_n r)\exp(-\lambda_n^2 D_{AB} t)$$

**Step 6 — Orthogonality of Bessel Functions:**

$$\int_0^R r J_0(\lambda_n r) J_0(\lambda_m r) dr = \frac{R^2}{2}[J_1(\lambda_n R)]^2 \delta_{nm}$$

**Step 7 — Coefficient from Initial Condition $C(r,0) = C_0$:**

$$A_n = \frac{2(C_0 - C_s)}{R^2 [J_1(\lambda_n R)]^2}\int_0^R r J_0(\lambda_n r) dr$$

Using $\int_0^R r J_0(\lambda_n r) dr = \frac{R}{\lambda_n} J_1(\lambda_n R)$:

$$A_n = \frac{2(C_0 - C_s)}{R \lambda_n J_1(\lambda_n R)}$$

**Step 8 — Final Solution:**

$$\boxed{\frac{C(r,t) - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{2}{R}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{J_0(\lambda_n r)}{\lambda_n J_1(\lambda_n R)}\exp(-\lambda_n^2 D_{AB} t)}$$

**Step 9 — Average Concentration:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\bar{C} - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{4}{R^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{\lambda_n^2}\exp(-\lambda_n^2 D_{AB} t)}$$

This is the **Newman solution for a long cylinder**, used in drying of wood, curing of concrete cylinders, etc.

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Planar vs Cylindrical vs Spherical

| Property | Planar (Slab) | Cylinder | Sphere |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geometric factor in Laplacian | $\frac{d^2 C}{dx^2}$ | $\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dC}{dr}\right)$ | $\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2\frac{dC}{dr}\right)$ |
| Steady profile | Linear | Logarithmic | $1/r$ |
| Unsteady eigenfunctions | $\sin, \cos$ | Bessel $J_0$ | $\sin, \cos$ (via $u=rC$ substitution) |
| Eigenvalues | $n\pi/L$ | $\lambda_n R = 2.4048, 5.5201, \dots$ | $n\pi/R$ |
| Time to reach 99% equilibrium $\propto$ | $L^2/D$ | $R^2/D$ | $R^2/D$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solution Strategy for Axial Symmetry Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START: Problem Statement]) --> B0{Identify Geometry?}
    B0 -- Cylinder --> C1[Define r1, r2, BC1, BC2]
    B0 -- Sphere --> C2[Define r1, r2, BC1, BC2]
    C1 --> D0{Steady or Unsteady?}
    C2 --> D0
    D0 -- Steady --> E1[Integrate twice]
    E1 --> F1[Apply BCs to get A1, A2]
    F1 --> G1[Compute C r via Ficks Law]
    G1 --> G2[Total molar flow via N times A]
    D0 -- Unsteady --> H1{Non-dimensionalise}
    H1 --> H2[Tau equals Dt over L2, Xi equals r over R]
    H2 --> I1{Separable?}
    I1 -- Yes Cylinder --> J1[Bessel eigenfunction J0]
    I1 -- Yes Sphere --> J2[Substitute u equals rC, get planar form]
    J1 --> K1[Determine lambda n roots of J0 lambda R equals 0]
    J2 --> K2[Eigenvalues alpha n equals n pi over R]
    K1 --> L1[Build series solution C r t]
    K2 --> L1
    L1 --> M1[Apply initial condition to find coefficients]
    M1 --> M2[Final analytical expression for C r t]
    M2 --> N0{Compute observable?}
    N0 -- Local concentration --> O1[Evaluate at desired r, t]
    N0 -- Average concentration --> O2[Integrate over volume]
    N0 -- Flux at surface --> O3[Differentiate at r equals R]
    O1 --> P0([END: Answer])
    O2 --> P0
    O3 --> P0
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Boundary Layer in a Cylindrical Pellet

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Outer radius r2, Conc CA2] -->|Radial flux| B[Cylindrical shell r]
    B -->|Ficks Law| C[Inner radius r1, Conc CA1]
    subgraph CrossSection
    D[Concentric rings of area 2 pi r dr] --> E[Concentration profile C r]
    end
    A -. Inlet conditions .-> CrossSection
    C -. Outlet conditions .-> CrossSection
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Diffusion Pathways in Spherical Catalyst

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Reactor Bulk, Conc CA bulk] -->|Film diffusion kc| S1[Outer surface r equals R, Conc CA s]
    S1 -->|Internal pore diffusion D eff| S2[Internal radial diffusion]
    S2 -->|Surface reaction k| S3[Active site at r equals 0]
    subgraph Geometry
    G1[Spherical pellet radius R] --> G2[Thiele modulus phi equals R sqrt k over D]
    end
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Solution Form

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Problem Type]) --> Q1{Constant surface conc or flux?}
    Q1 -- Constant conc Dirichlet --> Q2{Steady state?}
    Q1 -- Constant flux Neumann --> Q3{Diffusion through cylinder or sphere?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Log or 1 over r profile]
    Q2 -- No --> R2[Series with Bessel or sin eigenfunctions]
    Q3 --> R3[Apply Ficks law to flux at boundary]
    R1 --> End([Answer])
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECHT525]`

> **Define "axial symmetry" in the context of mass transfer. Write the general unsteady-state mass transfer equation in cylindrical coordinates and simplify it for a system with axial symmetry.**

**Model Answer:**

*Axial symmetry in mass transfer refers to a transport situation where the concentration distribution $C_A(r,t)$ depends only on the radial distance $r$ from a central axis (or centre for spheres) and time $t$, with no dependence on angular ($\theta$) or axial ($z$) coordinates.*

The general unsteady-state diffusion equation in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ is:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}\right]$$

Applying axial symmetry ($\partial C_A/\partial \theta = 0$, $\partial C_A/\partial z = 0$):

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left[\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial r^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right]$$

This is the **working equation** for all radially symmetric mass transfer problems.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECHT525]`

> **State and justify the boundary conditions used in the analytical solution of unsteady-state diffusion in a solid sphere of radius $R$ held at constant surface concentration $C_s$.**

**Model Answer:**

For a solid sphere initially at uniform concentration $C_0$, with surface suddenly exposed to a fluid maintaining $C_s$ at $r = R$, the boundary and initial conditions are:

**BC-1 (Surface):** $C_A(R, t) = C_s$ for $t > 0$
*Justification:* The fluid phase maintains the surface concentration constant (Dirichlet condition, corresponds to $Bi_m \to \infty$ or very small external resistance).

**BC-2 (Centre):** $C_A(0, t) = $ finite for $t > 0$
*Justification:* Physically, concentration cannot be infinite at the centre. Mathematically, this requires boundedness of the Bessel/sine series at $r = 0$.

**IC-1 (Initial):** $C_A(r, 0) = C_0$ for $0 \le r \le R$
*Justification:* The sphere is initially at uniform concentration before being immersed.

**Symmetry:** $\partial C_A/\partial r \big\vert_{r=0} = 0$ (Neumann at the centre), equivalent to the finiteness condition.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing cylindrical and spherical steady-state profiles** (logarithmic vs $1/r$). Always check geometry first!
> 2. **Using $n\pi/R$ as eigenvalues for cylinders** — these apply to *spheres*, not cylinders! Cylinders need $\lambda_n R = 2.4048, 5.5201, \dots$ from Bessel function $J_0$.
> 3. **Forgetting the $1/r$ Jacobian** when computing average concentration in a sphere: $\bar{C} = \frac{3}{R^3}\int_0^R C(r,t) r^2 \, dr$, not $\frac{1}{R}\int_0^R C(r,t)\,dr$.
> 4. **Losing marks** by not stating units of $D_{AB}$ (m²/s), or by skipping the dimensional analysis step.
> 5. **Substitution trick for sphere**: students forget the substitution $u = rC$ which linearises the spherical PDE.

---

#### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PECHT525]` (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Derive the steady-state concentration profile for species A diffusing radially through a hollow cylindrical tube of inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$, given that $C_A = C_{A,1}$ at $r_1$ and $C_A = C_{A,2}$ at $r_2$. Hence obtain an expression for the molar flux and the total molar flow rate per unit length. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A gas mixture containing $H_2$ and $N_2$ diffuses through the wall of a porous cylindrical tube of inner radius $1.0$ cm and outer radius $2.0$ cm. The mole fraction of $H_2$ at the inner surface is $0.80$ and at the outer surface is $0.20$. The total pressure is $1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa and the temperature is $298$ K. The diffusivity $D_{H_2-N_2} = 7.12 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the molar flux of $H_2$ at the mid-radius (1.5 cm) and the total molar flow rate of $H_2$ per metre length of the tube. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The derivation proceeds as in **Section 3.1, Steps 1–10**:

Starting from $\frac{d}{dr}(r dC_A/dr) = 0$, integrate twice:

$$C_A(r) = A_1 \ln(r) + A_2$$

Applying BCs and solving:

$$A_1 = \frac{C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}}{\ln(r_1/r_2)}, \quad A_2 = C_{A,1} - A_1 \ln(r_1)$$

$$\boxed{C_A(r) = C_{A,1} - \left(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}\right)\frac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

Molar flux:

$$N_{A,r} = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dr} = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})}{r \ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

Total molar flow per unit length:

$$\dot{n}_A = 2\pi r \cdot N_{A,r} = \frac{2\pi D_{AB}(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

**(b)** Numerical computation:

Total molar concentration using ideal gas law:

$$C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5}{8.314 \times 298} = 40.88 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

Concentrations:

$$C_{A,1} = 0.80 \times 40.88 = 32.70 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

$$C_{A,2} = 0.20 \times 40.88 = 8.176 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

Log-ratio: $\ln(r_2/r_1) = \ln(2.0/1.0) = \ln 2 = 0.6931$

Concentration difference: $C_{A,1} - C_{A,2} = 32.70 - 8.176 = 24.524 \text{ mol/m}^3$

**Molar flux at $r = 1.5$ cm = $0.015$ m:**

$$N_{A,r} = \frac{7.12 \times 10^{-5} \times 24.524}{0.015 \times 0.6931} = \frac{1.7461 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0397 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$\boxed{N_{A,r} = 1.679 \times 10^{-1} \text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

**Total molar flow per metre length:**

$$\dot{n}_A = \frac{2\pi \times 7.12 \times 10^{-5} \times 24.524}{0.6931} = \frac{1.0969 \times 10^{-2}}{0.6931}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{n}_A = 1.583 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol/s per metre}}$$

---

#### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PECHT525]` (14 Marks, **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**)

> **(a)** Derive the unsteady-state concentration profile $C(r,t)$ for a solid sphere of radius $R$ initially at uniform concentration $C_0$, suddenly immersed in a fluid that maintains the surface concentration at $C_s$. Use the substitution $u = rC$ to linearise the governing PDE. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A spherical pharmaceutical bead of radius $3$ mm is loaded with drug at uniform concentration $C_0 = 100$ mg/cm³. It is immersed in a dissolution fluid that maintains $C_s = 5$ mg/cm³ at the surface. The effective diffusivity of the drug through the bead is $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s. Calculate the average concentration in the bead after (i) 1 hour, and (ii) 10 hours, using the Newman series solution for a sphere. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** As derived in **Section 3.3, Steps 1–10**:

The governing PDE $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\left[\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial C}{\partial r}\right]$ is linearised by the substitution $u(r,t) = r \cdot C(r,t)$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial r^2}$$

with BCs $u(0,t) = 0$ and $u(R,t) = R C_s$, and IC $u(r,0) = rC_0$.

Separation of variables yields:

$$\boxed{\frac{C_A - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{2R}{\pi r}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n}\sin\left(\frac{n\pi r}{R}\right)\exp\left(-\frac{n^2\pi^2 D_{AB} t}{R^2}\right)}$$

**(b)** Numerical application:

Given: $R = 3$ mm $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s; $C_0 = 100$ mg/cm³; $C_s = 5$ mg/cm³.

Mass Fourier number: $Fo_m = \frac{Dt}{R^2}$

**Case (i): $t = 1$ hour $= 3600$ s:**

$$Fo_m = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-10} \times 3600}{(3 \times 10^{-3})^2} = \frac{5.4 \times 10^{-7}}{9 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.060$$

Series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^2}\exp(-n^2 \pi^2 Fo_m)$:

- $n=1$: $\exp(-\pi^2 \times 0.060) = \exp(-0.592) = 0.5531$ → $\frac{1}{1} \times 0.5531 = 0.5531$
- $n=2$: $\exp(-4\pi^2 \times 0.060) = \exp(-2.369) = 0.0936$ → $\frac{1}{4} \times 0.0936 = 0.0234$
- $n=3$: negligible ($< 0.001$)

Sum $\approx 0.576$

$$\frac{\bar{C} - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{6}{\pi^2} \times 0.576 = 0.6079 \times 0.576 = 0.350$$

$$\boxed{\bar{C} = C_s + 0.350(C_0 - C_s) = 5 + 0.350 \times 95 = 38.3 \text{ mg/cm}^3}$$

**Case (ii): $t = 10$ hours $= 36000$ s:**

$$Fo_m = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-10} \times 36000}{9 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.600$$

- $n=1$: $\exp(-\pi^2 \times 0.6) = \exp(-5.92) = 0.00267$ → $0.00267$
- $n=2$: negligible

Sum $\approx 0.00267$

$$\frac{\bar{C} - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = 0.6079 \times 0.00267 = 0.00162$$

$$\boxed{\bar{C} = 5 + 0.00162 \times 95 = 5.15 \text{ mg/cm}^3}$$

**Interpretation:** After 10 hours, the bead has released ~95% of its drug, approaching equilibrium with the dissolution fluid. This demonstrates the square-root-of-time release kinetics characteristic of spherical diffusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mass Transfer with Axial Symmetry**

**Conceptual Anchors:**
- [ ] Axial symmetry = $\partial/\partial \theta = 0$, $\partial/\partial z = 0$; system is 1-D in $r$.
- [ ] Cylinder area $\propto r$; Sphere area $\propto r^2$ → these geometric factors dictate the ODE form.

**Equations You MUST Memorise:**
- [ ] Steady cylinder: $C(r) = A_1 \ln r + A_2$
- [ ] Steady sphere: $C(r) = B_1/r + A_2$
- [ ] Unsteady cylinder: Bessel eigenfunctions $J_0(\lambda_n r)$, eigenvalues $\lambda_n R = 2.4048, 5.5201, \dots$
- [ ] Unsteady sphere: linearised via $u = rC$, eigenvalues $n\pi/R$

**Boundary Conditions Checklist:**
- [ ] Dirichlet: $C(R,t) = C_s$ (constant surface conc.)
- [ ] Neumann: $-D\partial C/\partial r \vert_R = k_c(C_s - C_\infty)$ (finite external resistance)
- [ ] Symmetry: $\partial C/\partial r \vert_{r=0} = 0$

**Dimensionless Groups:**
- [ ] Mass Fourier number $Fo_m = Dt/L_c^2$
- [ ] Mass Biot number $Bi_m = k_c L_c / D$
- [ ] Thiele modulus $\phi = R\sqrt{k/D}$ (for reactive systems)

**Engineering Shortcuts:**
- [ ] Cylinder total flow: $\dot n_A = 2\pi D (C_1 - C_2)/\ln(r_2/r_1)$
- [ ] Sphere total flow: $\dot n_A = 4\pi D r_1 r_2 (C_1 - C_2)/(r_2 - r_1)$
- [ ] Sphere Newman series: $\frac{\bar{C} - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{6}{\pi^2}\sum \frac{1}{n^2}e^{-n^2\pi^2 Fo_m}$

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- [ ] Do not confuse cylinder $\lambda_n$ with sphere $n\pi/R$.
- [ ] Do not omit the $1/r$ in Jacobian when averaging spherical concentration.
- [ ] Always verify the geometry — slab vs cylinder vs sphere changes the answer completely.
- [ ] When applying Fick's law, the gradient is $\partial C/\partial r$, not $\partial C/\partial x$.

**Cross-Connect to Heat Transfer:**
- [ ] Heat-mass transfer analogy: $D \leftrightarrow \alpha$, $C \leftrightarrow T$, $k_c \leftrightarrow h$.
- [ ] All solutions derived here have exact heat-transfer analogues (Newman's cooling of a sphere, cooling of a long cylinder).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Continuity Equation** is the mathematical statement of the **Law of Conservation of Mass** applied to a flowing fluid or a chemical species in a process. It asserts that mass (or moles of a species) can neither be created nor destroyed within a control volume — only accumulated, depleted, transported, or generated by chemical reaction.

In its most general differential (point) form, the continuity equation for a fluid of density $\rho$ moving with a velocity vector $\vec{v}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

For a chemical species $A$ with concentration $c_A$ participating in a reaction of rate $R_A$:

$$\frac{\partial c_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (c_A \vec{v}) + \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_A - R_A = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The continuity equation is the cornerstone of Module 2. Expect derivation questions (7–14 marks) in every End Semester Examination (ESE). Master both integral and differential forms in **Cartesian, Cylindrical, and Spherical** coordinate systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Pipe Thought Experiment"

Imagine a section of a garden hose with water flowing through it.

- **Steady flow:** Whatever mass of water enters one end per second **must** exit the other end per second. The hose never fills up or empties.
- **Unsteady flow:** If the inlet valve is opened wider, water begins to **accumulate** inside the hose. The mass entering is greater than the mass leaving — the difference is *stored* inside.
- **Leakage / Generation:** If a small hole in the hose exists, mass is "lost" (or, in a chemical reactor, a reaction *generates* new species mass).

The continuity equation is the precise mathematical translation of this mass bookkeeping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants and Units to Remember:**
> - Density $\rho$: **kg/m³**
> - Velocity $\vec{v}$: **m/s**
> - Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$: **kg/s**
> - Molar concentration $c_A$: **kmol/m³**
> - Molar flow rate $\dot{n}_A$: **kmol/s**
> - Diffusive flux $\vec{J}_A$: **kmol/(m²·s)**

### 1.3 Types of Continuity Equations in Process Engineering

| Type | Governing Quantity | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| Total mass continuity | Mass | Fluid mechanics, CFD |
| Species (component) continuity | Mass or moles of species $A$ | Reaction engineering, separations |
| Volume continuity (incompressible) | Volume | Liquid flow, hydraulics |
| Energy continuity (1st Law) | Energy | Heat transfer, thermodynamics (related, not identical) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Logic — Six Logical Steps

The continuity equation is built upon a single, immovable principle. Below is the structured logic flow:

1. **Define a control volume (CV):** A fixed region in space through which mass flows. The boundary is the **control surface (CS)**.
2. **Identify three contributions to mass change inside the CV:**
   - *Accumulation* — rate of mass build-up inside the CV.
   - *Net inflow* — net mass flux crossing the CS.
   - *Generation / Consumption* — mass produced or destroyed (only by chemical reaction; classical mass is never generated).
3. **Apply the conservation principle:**
   *Accumulation = Inflow − Outflow + Generation*
4. **Translate the CS integral into a divergence integral** using the Divergence Theorem.
5. **Localize** the resulting equation by shrinking the CV to an infinitesimal point, producing the differential form.
6. **Specialize** to the geometry (Cartesian, cylindrical, or spherical) of the problem.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` (not `|`) for absolute values in the table below to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Equation | Name / Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ | Differential, general | Total mass, any fluid |
| 2 | $\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) = 0$ | Material derivative form | Incompressible $\Rightarrow \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\partial c_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (c_A \vec{v}) + \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_A - R_A = 0$ | Species continuity | Reaction engineering, Fick's law |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$ | Cartesian coordinates | Rectangular reactors, ducts |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$ | Cylindrical coordinates | Pipes, tubular reactors |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial (r^2 \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r \sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial (\rho v_\theta \sin\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{1}{r \sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial (\rho v_\phi)}{\partial \phi} = 0$ | Spherical coordinates | Droplets, packed spheres |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \sum_k \nu_k M_k r_k$ | With reaction | Reactive systems (per unit volume) |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} = \dfrac{dm_{CV}}{dt}$ | Integral form | Control-volume balances |
| 9 | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | Incompressible, 1-D | Pipe flow continuity |
| 10 | $\dot{n}_{A,in} - \dot{n}_{A,out} + \dot{n}_{A,gen} = \dfrac{dn_{A,CV}}{dt}$ | Molar species balance | Reactor design |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Reactor Design (CSTR/PFR):** The species continuity equation, integrated along the reactor length, gives the **design equation** $V = F_{A0} \int \dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$.
- **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics):** Every commercial CFD solver (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM) solves the continuity equation as one of the Navier–Stokes governing equations.
- **Distillation Columns:** Total and component material balances on each tray are macroscopic (integral) continuity equations.
- **Piping Networks:** Conservation of volumetric flow gives the node equation $\sum_{in} Q_i = \sum_{out} Q_j$ at every junction (Kirchhoff's law analogue for fluids).
- **Membrane Separations:** Continuity of each species across the membrane determines selectivity and flux.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Integral Form from a Control Volume

**Starting premise (Reynolds Transport Theorem applied to mass):**

The system (Lagrangian) rate of change of mass is identically zero (mass is conserved).

$$\frac{D m_{sys}}{Dt} = 0$$

**Step 1:** Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem linking system and control volume:

$$\frac{D m_{sys}}{Dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

**Step 2:** Set the LHS to zero (mass conservation):

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange into a "word equation" form:

$$\text{Accumulation} + \text{Net Outflow} = 0$$

Equivalently, in the form KTU examiners love:

$$\frac{d m_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen}$$

**Step 4:** If chemical reactions occur, the generation term $\dot{m}_{gen}$ appears (positive for products, negative for reactants).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential Form from the Integral Form

**Step 1:** Start with the integral continuity equation:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 2:** Convert the surface integral to a volume integral using the **Divergence Theorem**:

$$\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back:

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 4:** For this integral to vanish for *any* arbitrarily chosen control volume, the integrand itself must vanish (localization principle):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0}$$

**Step 5:** Expand the divergence using the product rule:

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho$$

**Step 6:** Combine with the material derivative definition $\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho$:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) = 0$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cartesian Coordinate Expansion

**Step 1:** Write the velocity vector in Cartesian components $\vec{v} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the divergence $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$:

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the general differential form:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Cylindrical Coordinate Expansion

**Step 1:** In cylindrical coordinates, the divergence operator is:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r F_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial F_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

**Step 2:** Apply to $\vec{F} = \rho \vec{v}$ with $\vec{v} = v_r \hat{e}_r + v_\theta \hat{e}_\theta + v_z \hat{e}_z$:

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3:** The full cylindrical continuity equation becomes:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Incompressible Flow Specialization

**Step 1:** For a liquid (incompressible), $\rho = $ constant, so $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ and $\vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho = 0$.

**Step 2:** The continuity equation reduces to:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

**Step 3:** In 3-D Cartesian form:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** Many students wrongly write $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ for incompressible flow. The correct simplification is $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ because $\rho$ is a constant that can be factored out and cancelled.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Water ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) flows steadily through a converging pipe. At section 1, the diameter is $D_1 = 0.10$ m and velocity is $v_1 = 2$ m/s. At section 2, the diameter is $D_2 = 0.05$ m. Find the velocity at section 2 and the mass flow rate.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Apply the integral continuity equation for steady, incompressible, 1-D flow:

$$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2$$

Step 2: Compute cross-sectional areas:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 3: Solve for $v_2$:

$$v_2 = v_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 2 \times \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 4: Compute the mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_1 v_1 = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 = 15.71 \text{ kg/s}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mass Flow Through a Control Volume

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mass Inlet mdot_in] --> B[Control Volume CV]
    B --> C[Mass Outlet mdot_out]
    D[Generation mdot_gen] --> B
    B --> E[Accumulation dmCV over dt]
    F[Reynolds Transport Theorem] --> B
    G[Divergence Theorem] --> H[Differential Form d rho dt plus div rho v equals 0]
    B --> G
    H --> I[Cartesian Form]
    H --> J[Cylindrical Form]
    H --> K[Spherical Form]
    I --> L[Incompressible div v equals 0]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Logical Flow — From Physical Principle to Solvable PDE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Conservation of Mass Statement] --> stepB[Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem]
    stepB --> stepC[Obtain Integral Form]
    stepC --> stepD[Apply Divergence Theorem]
    stepD --> stepE[Localize via Arbitrary CV]
    stepE --> stepF[General Differential Form]
    stepF --> stepG[Choose Geometry]
    stepG --> stepG1[Cartesian]
    stepG --> stepG2[Cylindrical]
    stepG --> stepG3[Spherical]
    stepF --> stepH[Apply Assumptions]
    stepH --> stepH1[Steady State]
    stepH --> stepH2[Incompressible]
    stepH --> stepH3[1 D flow]
    stepH1 --> stepI[Solvable Engineering Equation]
    stepH2 --> stepI
    stepH3 --> stepI
```
