## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **State the continuity equation in its general differential form and explain the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The general differential form of the continuity equation is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

- The first term, $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$, represents the **local (temporal) rate of change of density** — the rate of mass accumulation at a fixed point.
- The second term, $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$, represents the **net rate of mass efflux per unit volume** — the net rate at which mass is leaving through the boundary of an infinitesimal element.

The equation states that for a fluid element, the rate of mass increase equals the negative of the net mass outflow, i.e., mass is conserved.

*(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for first term meaning, 1 for second term meaning)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Write the continuity equation for an incompressible fluid. Why is the density term dropped?**

**Model Answer:**
For an incompressible fluid, $\rho = $ constant, so the continuity equation simplifies to:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

In Cartesian coordinates: $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$.

The density term is dropped because, by definition, an incompressible fluid has constant density with respect to both space and time. Thus $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ and $\vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho = 0$, allowing $\rho$ to be factored out of the divergence term and cancelled. The remaining condition is that the velocity field must be solenoidal (divergence-free), meaning the volumetric flow into any element equals the volumetric flow out.

*(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for Cartesian form, 1 for explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the general differential form of the continuity equation for a fluid starting from the integral form based on a control volume. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the general differential form, derive the continuity equation in **cylindrical coordinates** for a fluid of density $\rho$. Hence, specialize it for a steady, incompressible, axisymmetric flow with $v_r = v_r(r, z)$ and $v_\theta = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation — Integral to Differential Form:**

**Step 1:** State the integral continuity equation for a fixed control volume (CV) with control surface (CS):

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

*Assumption: No mass generation (classical mass conservation).*

**Step 2:** Convert the surface integral to a volume integral via the Divergence Theorem:

$$\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Combine both integrals into a single volume integral:

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 4:** Since the CV is arbitrary, the integrand itself must vanish identically:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Expand the divergence using the product rule:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho = 0$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the material derivative $\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \rho$ to obtain the final compact form:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) = 0$$

*Mark distribution: 2 marks integral form, 2 marks divergence theorem, 1 mark localization, 2 marks final form and material derivative form.*

**(b) Cylindrical Derivation and Specialization:**

**Step 1:** In cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$, the divergence operator is:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r A_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial A_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}$$

**Step 2:** Set $\vec{A} = \rho \vec{v}$ with $\vec{v} = v_r \hat{e}_r + v_\theta \hat{e}_\theta + v_z \hat{e}_z$:

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3:** The full cylindrical continuity equation is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Apply specialization: **steady state** $\Rightarrow \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$.

**Step 5:** Apply **incompressibility** $\Rightarrow \rho = $ constant, allowing it to be factored out of the spatial derivatives:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Apply **axisymmetric flow** $\Rightarrow \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta} = 0$ and $v_\theta = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

This is the final specialized form for steady, incompressible, axisymmetric flow in cylindrical coordinates.

*Mark distribution: 2 marks divergence in cylindrical, 2 marks full equation, 1 mark each for three specialization steps.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the species continuity equation for a chemical species $A$ undergoing a homogeneous reaction of rate $R_A$ (kmol/m³·s), accounting for both convective and diffusive transport. Clearly state the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid stream containing solvent $S$ and solute $A$ enters a well-mixed tank at $F_{in} = 0.010$ m³/min with concentration $c_{A,in} = 2.0$ kmol/m³. The tank volume is $V = 1.0$ m³. The outlet flow rate is $F_{out} = 0.010$ m³/min. Inside the tank, species $A$ decomposes via a first-order reaction with rate $R_A = -k c_A$, where $k = 0.20$ min⁻¹. The density is constant. Determine the steady-state concentration of $A$ in the tank. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Species Continuity Equation:**

**Step 1:** Begin with the integral balance for species $A$ in a control volume:

$$\frac{d n_A^{CV}}{dt} = \dot{n}_{A,in} - \dot{n}_{A,out} + \dot{n}_{A,gen} - \dot{n}_{A,cons}$$

**Step 2:** The net molar outflow across the control surface includes both **convective** and **diffusive** contributions:

$$\int_{CS} (c_A \vec{v} + \vec{J}_A) \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$$

**Step 3:** The rate of chemical generation (per unit volume) is $\int_{CV} R_A \, dV$, where $R_A$ is the net rate of formation of $A$ (positive for generation, negative for consumption).

**Step 4:** Combining:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} c_A \, dV = -\int_{CS} (c_A \vec{v} + \vec{J}_A) \cdot \hat{n} \, dA + \int_{CV} R_A \, dV$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Divergence Theorem to the surface integral:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} c_A \, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (c_A \vec{v}) \, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_A \, dV = \int_{CV} R_A \, dV$$

**Step 6:** Localize the equation (arbitrary CV):

$$\frac{\partial c_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (c_A \vec{v}) + \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_A - R_A = 0$$

**Meaning of each term:**
- $\dfrac{\partial c_A}{\partial t}$: local accumulation of $A$.
- $\nabla \cdot (c_A \vec{v})$: net convective molar efflux.
- $\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_A$: net diffusive molar efflux.
- $R_A$: net rate of generation by reaction.

*Mark distribution: 2 marks integral balance, 2 marks flux decomposition, 2 marks differential form, 1 mark terminology.*

**(b) Steady-State CSTR Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Apply the species continuity equation in integrated form for a well-mixed tank at steady state:

$$0 = F_{in} c_{A,in} - F_{out} c_{A,out} + R_A V$$

**Step 2:** With $F_{in} = F_{out} = F = 0.010$ m³/min and the reaction rate $R_A = -k c_{A,out}$ (consumption):

$$0 = F c_{A,in} - F c_{A,out} - k c_{A,out} V$$

**Step 3:** Group the $c_{A,out}$ terms:

$$F c_{A,in} = c_{A,out} (F + kV)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $c_{A,out}$:

$$c_{A,out} = \frac{F \, c_{A,in}}{F + k V}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute numerical values:

$$c_{A,out} = \frac{(0.010)(2.0)}{0.010 + (0.20)(1.0)} = \frac{0.020}{0.010 + 0.20} = \frac{0.020}{0.21}$$

$$\boxed{c_{A,out} \approx 0.0952 \text{ kmol/m}^3}$$

**Step 6:** Physical check: The Damköhler number $Da = kV/F = 0.20/0.010 = 20 \gg 1$, indicating strong reaction dominance. Thus the outlet concentration is much lower than the inlet, which is consistent with our result.

*Mark distribution: 2 marks species balance, 2 marks rate expression, 1 mark algebra, 1 mark numerical substitution, 1 mark physical interpretation.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the time derivative** in the integral form when deriving the differential form. Many students incorrectly write $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ as the general form, which is only true for steady, incompressible flow.
> 2. **Mishandling the divergence in cylindrical coordinates** — particularly the $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r}$ term. Students often write $\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial r}$ instead.
> 3. **Sign confusion with $R_A$** — remember that $R_A > 0$ means *generation* of $A$, while $R_A < 0$ means *consumption*.
> 4. **Units mismatch** in numerical problems — always convert $F$ (m³/min), $V$ (m³), and $k$ (min⁻¹) to a consistent time basis before substitution.
> 5. **Cancelling $\rho$ incorrectly** — for incompressible flow, $\rho$ is a *constant* (not zero!) and can be safely cancelled; writing $\rho = 0$ will cost full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **continuity equation is conservation of mass in equation form.** It comes in two equivalent flavors: **integral** (for finite control volumes) and **differential** (for point fields).
- The **Reynolds Transport Theorem** is the bridge between Lagrangian (system) and Eulerian (control volume) viewpoints — without it, no derivation is possible.
- The **Divergence Theorem** converts surface integrals to volume integrals, enabling localization to a point.
- **Three coordinate systems** are mandatory for KTU 2024: Cartesian, cylindrical, and spherical. Memorize the divergence operator in each.
- The **incompressible simplification** $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ is one of the most-tested specializations — never write $\rho = 0$; cancel $\rho$ as a constant.
- For **species continuity**, the four contributions are: accumulation, convection, diffusion, and reaction. Always retain the **sign convention** $R_A$ positive for generation.
- The **integral form** $\dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} = \dfrac{dm_{CV}}{dt}$ is the workhorse for **reactor design, control volume analysis, and process equipment sizing**.
- KTU examiners frequently test the **derivation** (7 marks) and a **numerical application** (7 marks) within the same 14-mark question. Practice both.
- **Units matter:** ensure all flow rates ($F$, $\dot{m}$, $\dot{n}$) are converted to a common time base (s⁻¹ or min⁻¹) before substitution.
- The continuity equation, together with the **Navier–Stokes equations** and the **energy equation**, forms the complete governing set for viscous, heat-conducting fluid flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the mathematical modelling of transport processes (heat, mass, and momentum) governed by **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)** and **Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)**, **Boundary Conditions (BCs)** are the prescribed mathematical constraints imposed on the unknown function (or its derivatives) at the spatial and/or temporal extremities of the physical domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> A boundary condition is the auxiliary information that must be supplied alongside a differential equation to uniquely determine its solution. Without BCs, a differential equation has an infinite family of solutions (general solution containing arbitrary constants/functions). BCs act as the "mathematical fingerprint" that pins down the *particular* solution matching the physical reality.

Formally, for an $n$-th order ODE:
$$a_n(x)\frac{d^n y}{dx^n} + a_{n-1}(x)\frac{d^{n-1} y}{dx^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_1(x)\frac{dy}{dx} + a_0(x)y = f(x)$$

The **general solution** contains exactly $n$ arbitrary constants $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$, which are determined by specifying $n$ independent boundary (or initial) conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Stretched Guitar String**
> Imagine plucking a guitar string. The *wave equation* (a PDE) governs how the string vibrates. However, the wave equation alone cannot tell you *which note* the string plays — it only describes the shape of vibration in the abstract.
> 
> The **boundary conditions** are the fact that *both ends of the string are fixed to the guitar body* (the displacement $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$). These "end-pins" select a unique vibrating pattern (a specific harmonic) from the infinite possibilities. **Change the BCs (e.g., release one end), and you change the entire song!**
> 
> In a chemical reactor, a heat exchanger, or a distillation column, the BCs represent the **inlet/outlet temperatures, pressures, compositions, or heat fluxes** — they are the *interface contract* between the abstract mathematics and the actual physical equipment.

## 1.3 Role in Process Engineering

| Engineering System | Governing Equation | Typical Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| Heat conduction in a wall | $\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0$ | $T(0) = T_1$, $T(L) = T_2$ |
| Tubular reactor | $D\frac{d^2 C}{dx^2} - u\frac{dC}{dx} - kC = 0$ | $C(0) = C_0$, $\frac{dC}{dx}\big\vert_{x=L} = 0$ |
| Momentum (boundary layer) | $\nu\frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} = 0$ | $u(0) = 0$ (no-slip), $u(\delta) = U_\infty$ |

The **physical constants** most often encountered are:
- **Thermal conductivity $k$** $[\text{W/(m·K)}]$
- **Heat transfer coefficient $h$** $[\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}]$
- **Mass diffusivity $D$** $[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$
- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma$** $= 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ $[\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4)]$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Boundary Conditions are Mathematically Necessary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Uniqueness Theorem:** For a linear $n$-th order ODE, exactly $n$ independent conditions are required to obtain a unique solution. For a 2nd-order PDE in one spatial dimension, exactly 2 independent conditions (one at each boundary) are needed.

The logical sequence is:
1. **Formulate** the governing differential equation from conservation laws (mass, energy, momentum).
2. **Identify** the geometric domain $\Omega$ with its boundary $\partial\Omega$.
3. **Specify** the auxiliary conditions on $\partial\Omega$.
4. **Solve** the resulting Boundary Value Problem (BVP) or Initial Value Problem (IVP).

## 2.2 Classification of Boundary Conditions (KTU High-Priority Topic)

### Type I — Dirichlet Boundary Condition (First Kind)
The **value of the dependent variable** itself is specified at the boundary.

$$y(x_0) = y_0 \quad \text{or} \quad u(\vec{r},t)\big\vert_{\partial\Omega} = f(\vec{r},t)$$

**Physical examples:**
- A wall maintained at a fixed temperature $T_s$.
- A surface held at a known concentration $C_s$.
- The no-slip condition: $u = 0$ at a solid wall.

### Type II — Neumann Boundary Condition (Second Kind)
The **value of the normal derivative** (flux) is specified at the boundary.

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial n}\bigg\vert_{\partial\Omega} = g(\vec{r},t)$$

In 1-D: $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = g$.

**Physical examples:**
- A specified heat flux at a surface: $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = q_0''$.
- An insulated surface: $\dfrac{dT}{dx} = 0$ (adiabatic BC, a special Neumann case).
- Zero mass flux at an impermeable wall: $\dfrac{dC}{dx} = 0$.

### Type III — Robin (Mixed/Cauchy) Boundary Condition (Third Kind)
A **linear combination** of the function and its normal derivative is specified.

$$\alpha\, u + \beta\, \frac{\partial u}{\partial n}\bigg\vert_{\partial\Omega} = \gamma$$

In heat transfer, the **convective boundary condition** is the most famous Robin BC:

$$-k\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = h\bigl[T(L) - T_\infty\bigr]$$

This represents **Newton's Law of Cooling** at a surface exposed to a fluid at $T_\infty$ with convective coefficient $h$.

### Type IV — Periodic Boundary Condition
The function and its gradient match at opposing boundaries:

$$u(0) = u(L), \qquad \frac{du}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = \frac{du}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L}$$

Used in: heat exchangers, cyclic processes, finite-domain Fourier problems.

### Type V — Cauchy Boundary Condition
Both the function and its normal derivative are specified on the same boundary (over-determined for unique solvability of elliptic PDEs unless data is consistent).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Boundary Condition Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Interpretation (Process Engg.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dirichlet (1st kind) | $u = u_0$ on $\partial\Omega$ | Prescribed surface temperature/concentration |
| 2 | Neumann (2nd kind) | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = g$ on $\partial\Omega$ | Prescribed heat/mass flux |
| 3 | Robin / Mixed (3rd kind) | $\alpha u + \beta \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = \gamma$ | Convective heat/mass transfer |
| 4 | Periodic | $u(0) = u(L)$, $u'(0) = u'(L)$ | Cyclic/loop processes |
| 5 | Adiabatic (special Neumann) | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = 0$ | Insulated / impermeable wall |
| 6 | Symmetry | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = 0$ at symmetry plane | Mirror-image geometry |
| 7 | Convective (Robin variant) | $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx} = h(T - T_\infty)$ | Newton's cooling law |
| 8 | Radiative (Robin variant) | $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx} = \sigma(T^4 - T_\text{sur}^4)$ | Stefan–Boltzmann at surface |
| 9 | Well-Mixed inlet | $u(0^+) = u_\text{in}$ | Inlet stream to a reactor |
| 10 | Fully-Developed outlet | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\big\vert_{x=L} = 0$ | No axial gradient far downstream |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of BC Classification

- **Process Equipment Design:** Choosing the right BC determines whether a heat exchanger is sized for a fixed inlet temperature (Dirichlet) or a fixed heat-input rate (Neumann).
- **Numerical Simulation (CFD/FEA):** Incorrect BCs are the *#1 cause* of divergence in ANSYS Fluent, COMSOL, etc.
- **Control System Design:** Robin BCs naturally appear in feedback control (transfer function matching).
- **Reactor Safety:** Adiabatic vs. convective BCs can change a runaway reaction outcome by orders of magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Steady-State Heat Conduction with Mixed BCs

**Physical Problem:** A plane wall of thickness $L$ and thermal conductivity $k$ has its left face ($x=0$) held at temperature $T_1$. The right face ($x=L$) is exposed to a fluid at $T_\infty$ with convective heat transfer coefficient $h$. The surroundings are at $T_\infty$. Find the steady temperature distribution $T(x)$.

### Step 1 — Formulate the Governing Equation

Under steady-state, 1-D conduction with no internal heat generation, the heat equation reduces to Laplace's equation:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0$$

### Step 2 — State the Boundary Conditions

**At $x = 0$ (Dirichlet):**
$$T(0) = T_1$$

**At $x = L$ (Robin / Convective):**
$$-k \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = h\bigl[T(L) - T_\infty\bigr]$$

### Step 3 — Integrate the ODE Twice (General Solution)

**First integration:**
$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{dT}{dx} = C_1$$

**Second integration:**
$$T(x) = C_1 x + C_2$$

The general solution is **linear in $x$**, with two arbitrary constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ — exactly the number required for two BCs.

### Step 4 — Apply the Dirichlet BC at $x = 0$

Substitute $x = 0$, $T(0) = T_1$:
$$T(0) = C_1(0) + C_2 = T_1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_2 = T_1$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Robin BC at $x = L$

Differentiate the general solution: $\dfrac{dT}{dx} = C_1$.

Substitute into the convective BC:

$$-k C_1 = h\bigl[(C_1 L + T_1) - T_\infty\bigr]$$

$$-k C_1 = h C_1 L + h(T_1 - T_\infty)$$

Group $C_1$ terms on the left:

$$-k C_1 - h L C_1 = h(T_1 - T_\infty)$$

$$C_1(-k - hL) = h(T_1 - T_\infty)$$

$$C_1 = \frac{h(T_1 - T_\infty)}{-(k + hL)} = \frac{h(T_\infty - T_1)}{k + hL}$$

### Step 6 — Write the Final Particular Solution

$$\boxed{\,T(x) = T_1 + \frac{h(T_\infty - T_1)}{k + hL}\, x\,}$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Heat Flux (Engineering Useful Quantity)

The constant heat flux through the wall is:

$$q_x = -k\frac{dT}{dx} = -k C_1 = \frac{k\, h\,(T_1 - T_\infty)}{k + hL}$$

**Limiting-case sanity check (KTU favourite!):**

- If $h \to \infty$ (perfect convection, fluid temperature dominates): $T(x) \to T_\infty$ for all $x > 0$, and $q_x \to \infty$ — the surface is forced to fluid temperature.
- If $h \to 0$ (insulated): $q_x \to 0$ and $T(x) \to T_1$ — the wall is uniform.
- If $h \to 0$ AND $T_\infty = T_2$, we recover the classic two-Dirichlet case: $T(x) = T_1 + \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{L}x$.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: 2nd-Order ODE with Two Dirichlet BCs

**Problem:** Solve $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} - 4\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} + 4\,y = 0$, with $y(0) = 5$ and $y(1) = 2e^2$.

### Step 1 — Solve the Characteristic Equation

$$r^2 - 4r + 4 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (r-2)^2 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r = 2 \text{ (repeated root)}$$

### Step 2 — Write the General Solution

For a repeated real root, the general solution is:
$$y(x) = C_1 e^{2x} + C_2 x\, e^{2x}$$

### Step 3 — Apply BC at $x = 0$

$$y(0) = C_1 e^{0} + C_2 (0) e^{0} = C_1 = 5$$

### Step 4 — Apply BC at $x = 1$

$$y(1) = 5 e^{2} + C_2 (1) e^{2} = 2 e^{2}$$

Divide both sides by $e^2$:
$$5 + C_2 = 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_2 = -3$$

### Step 5 — Final Particular Solution

$$\boxed{\,y(x) = 5 e^{2x} - 3 x\, e^{2x} = e^{2x}(5 - 3x)\,}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping BC Type to Differential Operator Behaviour

| BC Type | Specified Quantity | Mathematical Operator | Effect on Solution Constants | Common Engineering Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dirichlet | $y$ | Identity $I$ | Fixes $C_2$ (or constant term) | Fixed wall temperature |
| Neumann | $y'$ | Differentiation $D$ | Fixes $C_1$ (or coefficient of leading term) | Constant heat flux heater |
| Robin | $ay + by'$ | $aI + bD$ | Couples $C_1$ and $C_2$ in one equation | Convective cooling, fin surfaces |
| Periodic | $y(0)=y(L)$, $y'(0)=y'(L)$ | Identity + Differentiation | Cyclic symmetry constraint | Heat exchanger loops, Fourier series on $[-L,L]$ |
| Adiabatic | $y' = 0$ | Differentiation $D$ | $C_1 = 0$ (in linear solutions) | Insulated pipe, symmetric geometry |

## 3.4 Boundary Condition in Laplace Transform Context (KTU Module-2 Cross-Link)

> [!NOTE]
> Many Laplace-transform problems in process control (e.g., step response of a stirred-tank heater) *implicitly* use boundary conditions at $t = 0$ (initial conditions $y(0) = y_0$, $y'(0) = y_0'$). When the BC is given at $t = 0$ (start) rather than at both ends, it is called an **Initial Value Problem (IVP)**; when given at two spatial ends, it is a **Boundary Value Problem (BVP)**.

The general solution of an $n$-th order ODE in time contains $n$ integration constants fixed by the values of $y, y', y'', \ldots, y^{(n-1)}$ at the initial time $t = 0$. These are the **initial conditions** — a temporal analogue of boundary conditions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Boundary Conditions (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boundary Conditions] --> B[Dirichlet Type 1]
    A --> C[Neumann Type 2]
    A --> D[Robin Type 3 Mixed]
    A --> E[Periodic Type 4]
    A --> F[Cauchy Overdetermined]
    B --> B1[Function value prescribed]
    B --> B2[Example: T equals T_s at wall]
    B --> B3[Math: u equals u0 on boundary]
    C --> C1[Derivative value prescribed]
    C --> C2[Example: Heat flux equals q0 double prime]
    C --> C3[Special: Adiabatic where du over dx equals 0]
    D --> D1[Linear combo of u and du over dn]
    D --> D2[Example: Convective cooling with h]
    D --> D3[Math: minus k dT over dx equals h T minus T infinity]
    E --> E1[Function repeats at both ends]
    E --> E2[Example: Cyclic heat exchanger]
    E --> E3[Math: u 0 equals u L and uprime 0 equals uprime L]
    F --> F1[Both u and derivative specified]
    F --> F2[Valid only for hyperbolic PDEs]
    F --> F3[Math: u equals u0 AND du over dn equals g]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: BC Implementation in a Process Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Physical System Reactor Wall] --> P2[Governing PDE from Conservation Laws]
    P2 --> P3[General Solution with C1 C2 Cn]
    P3 --> P4{BCs Applied]
    P4 --> P5[Dirichlet Inlet T1 C0]
    P4 --> P6[Neumann Heat Flux q0]
    P4 --> P7[Robin Convective h T infinity]
    P4 --> P8[Periodic Cyclic Loop]
    P5 --> P9[Particular Solution T x]
    P6 --> P9
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Engineering Output Temperature Profile Heat Duty Sizing]
    P10 --> P11[Validation against experimental data]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of 1-D Domain with Boundary Locations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Domain x equals 0] --> M[Interior x between 0 and L]
    M --> R[Domain x equals L]
    L --> L1[BC Type: Dirichlet / Neumann / Robin / Periodic]
    R --> R1[BC Type: Dirichlet / Neumann / Robin / Periodic]
    M --> M1[Governing ODE or PDE applied here]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Correct BC for a Process Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify the physical constraint at the boundary] --> Q1{Is the variable value known?}
    Q1 -- YES --> D[Apply Dirichlet]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is the flux value known?}
    Q2 -- YES --> N[Apply Neumann]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is there convection or radiation at the boundary?}
    Q3 -- YES --> R[Apply Robin]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Is the domain cyclic or symmetric?}
    Q4 -- YES --> PE[Apply Periodic]
    Q4 -- NO --> AD[Apply Adiabatic or Symmetry du over dn equals 0]
    D --> V[Validate dimensionally and physically]
    N --> V
    R --> V
    PE --> V
    AD --> V
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]**

### Question A1
**Classify the different types of boundary conditions used in solving second-order differential equations arising in process engineering. Give one chemical-engineering example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four principal types of boundary conditions are:

1. **Dirichlet (1st kind):** The value of the dependent variable is specified. *Example:* The inner surface of a reactor wall is maintained at a fixed temperature $T_s$, i.e., $T(0) = T_s$. **(1 mark)**

2. **Neumann (2nd kind):** The value of the normal derivative (flux) is specified. *Example:* A constant heat flux $q_0''$ is applied at a surface, i.e., $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx}\big\vert_{x=L} = q_0''$. **(1 mark)**

3. **Robin / Mixed (3rd kind):** A linear combination of the function and its derivative is specified. *Example:* Convective cooling at a surface: $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx}\big\vert_{x=L} = h[T(L) - T_\infty]$ (Newton's law of cooling). **(1 mark)**

*(Periodic can be mentioned as a 4th kind for full credit.)*

### Question A2
**"An adiabatic surface corresponds to a special case of the Neumann boundary condition." Justify this statement with the governing equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Neumann boundary condition** specifies the value of the normal derivative of the dependent variable at the boundary. Mathematically:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial n}\bigg\vert_{\partial\Omega} = g$$

For an **adiabatic (perfectly insulated)** surface, no heat crosses the boundary. Fourier's law of conduction gives the heat flux as $q = -k\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n}$. If $q = 0$ and $k \neq 0$, then:
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial n}\bigg\vert_{\text{adiabatic surface}} = 0$$

This is the **homogeneous Neumann condition** with $g = 0$, a special case. **(2 marks for equation, 1 mark for interpretation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 – Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A plane wall of thickness $L = 0.1$ m, thermal conductivity $k = 50$ W/(m·K) has its left face at $T_1 = 400$ K. The right face is exposed to a fluid at $T_\infty = 300$ K with convective heat transfer coefficient $h = 250$ W/(m²·K). Determine the steady-state temperature distribution $T(x)$ and the heat flux through the wall. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish clearly between Dirichlet, Neumann, and Robin boundary conditions. For each, state one physical scenario in a chemical process where it naturally arises. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Governing equation** (steady 1-D, no source):
$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0$$

**General solution:**
$$T(x) = C_1 x + C_2$$

**Apply BC at $x = 0$:**
$$T(0) = C_2 = T_1 = 400 \text{ K} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_2 = 400$$

**Apply Robin BC at $x = L = 0.1$:**
$$-k C_1 = h\bigl[(C_1 L + T_1) - T_\infty\bigr]$$
$$-50\, C_1 = 250\bigl[(0.1 C_1 + 400) - 300\bigr]$$
$$-50\, C_1 = 250\,(0.1 C_1 + 100)$$
$$-50\, C_1 = 25 C_1 + 25000$$
$$-75\, C_1 = 25000$$
$$C_1 = -333.33 \text{ K/m}$$

**Particular solution:**
$$T(x) = 400 - 333.33\,x \quad [\text{K}]$$

**Temperature at $x = L = 0.1$ m:**
$$T(0.1) = 400 - 333.33 \times 0.1 = 366.67 \text{ K}$$

**Heat flux:**
$$q = -k C_1 = -50 \times (-333.33) = 16{,}666.7 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 1.67 \times 10^4 \text{ W/m}^2$$

✅ **Answer:** $T(x) = 400 - 333.33\,x$ K, and $q \approx 1.67 \times 10^4$ W/m².

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| BC Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Chemical Process Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dirichlet (1st)** | $u = u_0$ on boundary | Variable value prescribed | Inner wall of a jacketed reactor held at constant $T_s$ |
| **Neumann (2nd)** | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = g$ on boundary | Flux prescribed | Electric heating coil supplying constant heat flux $q_0''$ |
| **Robin (3rd)** | $\alpha u + \beta\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = \gamma$ | Convection/linear combo | Cooling coil with fluid at $T_\infty$ and coefficient $h$ |

**Key Distinction:** Dirichlet fixes the *state*; Neumann fixes the *rate*; Robin couples the two (typically via convection). **(1 mark for distinction summary)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** A long cylindrical rod of length $L$ has one end ($x = 0$) maintained at $T_0$ and the other end ($x = L$) perfectly insulated. The lateral surface loses heat by convection to a fluid at $T_\infty$ with coefficient $h$. Formulate the steady-state governing ODE and the relevant boundary conditions. Do **not** solve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + 5\dfrac{dy}{dx} + 6y = 0$ subject to $y(0) = 2$ and $y'(0) = -1$. Identify the type of conditions used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Governing ODE (fin equation, dimensionless form):**
$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0, \quad \text{where } m^2 = \frac{hP}{kA_c}, \quad \theta = T - T_\infty$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

1. At $x = 0$ (prescribed base temperature — **Dirichlet**):
$$\theta(0) = T_0 - T_\infty \quad \text{or simply} \quad T(0) = T_0$$

2. At $x = L$ (insulated tip — **homogeneous Neumann**):
$$\frac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$$

✅ **Answer:** ODE is $\dfrac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0$ with a Dirichlet BC at $x=0$ and a homogeneous Neumann BC at $x=L$. **(7 marks — 3 for ODE, 2+2 for BCs)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:**
$$r^2 + 5r + 6 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (r+2)(r+3) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r_1 = -2, \; r_2 = -3$$

**Step 2 — General solution:**
$$y(x) = C_1 e^{-2x} + C_2 e^{-3x}$$

**Step 3 — Apply $y(0) = 2$:**
$$C_1 + C_2 = 2 \quad \cdots (i)$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and apply $y'(0) = -1$:**
$$y'(x) = -2C_1 e^{-2x} - 3C_2 e^{-3x}$$
$$y'(0) = -2C_1 - 3C_2 = -1 \quad \cdots (ii)$$

**Step 5 — Solve the system:**

From (i): $C_2 = 2 - C_1$. Substitute in (ii):
$$-2C_1 - 3(2 - C_1) = -1$$
$$-2C_1 - 6 + 3C_1 = -1$$
$$C_1 = 5$$

Then $C_2 = 2 - 5 = -3$.

**Final solution:**
$$\boxed{\,y(x) = 5 e^{-2x} - 3 e^{-3x}\,}$$

**Identification of BC type:** The conditions $y(0) = 2$ and $y'(0) = -1$ are both specified at the *same point* $x = 0$. This makes it an **Initial Value Problem (IVP)**, with both conditions being **initial conditions** (a special case of Dirichlet-type for the function and its derivative). **(1 mark for IVP identification)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing BC with IC:** Students often mix up *initial* conditions (at $t=0$) with *boundary* conditions (at spatial ends). For a BVP, both conditions are at $x=0$ and $x=L$. For an IVP, both are at $t=0$. The examiner checks this classification strictly. **(–1 to –2 marks)**
> 
> 2. **Sign Error in Robin BC:** The convective BC is $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx} = h(T - T_\infty)$. Students frequently write $+k\dfrac{dT}{dx} = h(T - T_\infty)$, giving a wrong sign on $C_1$. **(–1 mark)**
> 
> 3. **Forgetting Units:** $h$ is in W/(m²·K), $k$ in W/(m·K). If mixed up, the constant $C_1$ will be dimensionally inconsistent. **(–1 mark)**
> 
> 4. **Insufficient BCs:** A 2nd-order ODE needs exactly 2 independent conditions. Providing only 1 leads to an infinite family of solutions — examiner will mark "incomplete." **(–2 marks)**
> 
> 5. **Adiabatic ≠ Temperature is constant:** An adiabatic BC means $\dfrac{dT}{dx} = 0$, not $T = 0$. Many students confuse the two. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Definition:** BCs are auxiliary constraints on the unknown function/derivative at the domain boundary; they convert the general solution into a unique particular solution.
- ☐ **Order–BC Matching Rule:** An $n$-th order ODE needs exactly $n$ independent BCs/ICs.
- ☐ **Dirichlet (Type 1):** $u = u_0$ — value prescribed. *Wall at fixed temperature.*
- ☐ **Neumann (Type 2):** $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = g$ — flux prescribed. *Adiabatic is a special case with $g = 0$.*
- ☐ **Robin (Type 3):** $\alpha u + \beta\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = \gamma$ — convective/radiative coupling. *Newton's cooling: $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx} = h(T-T_\infty)$.*
- ☐ **Periodic:** $u(0) = u(L)$, $u'(0) = u'(L)$ — cyclic processes.
- ☐ **BVP vs IVP:** BVP has BCs at two spatial ends; IVP has ICs at one temporal point.
- ☐ **Limiting Cases:** $h \to \infty$ → Dirichlet (fluid-dominated); $h \to 0$ → Adiabatic Neumann.
- ☐ **Sign Convention:** Heat flux *leaving* the surface is positive in the $+x$ direction. Convective BC: $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$.
- ☐ **Most Tested Topic in KTU ESE:** Convective Robin BC on a plane wall — practice the full derivation.
- ☐ **Engineering Cross-Links:** Fin heat transfer, jacketed reactor, tubular reactor (Danckwerts BCs), heat exchanger sizing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Function Specified
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Function Specified

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Process Engineering** and **Mathematical Methods applied to Process Control**, the term **"Function Specified"** refers to the mathematical characterization of **Control Valve Characteristics**. A control valve is a final control element that manipulates the flow of a process fluid, and its *inherent* behavior is described by a specific mathematical function $q = f(x)$, where the relationship between the normalized flow rate and the normalized stem position follows a prescribed (specified) functional form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT525, Module 2):**
> A *function-specified* control valve is one whose **inherent flow characteristic** is defined by an explicit mathematical function relating the fractional flow $q$ to the fractional stem travel $x$ under conditions of **constant pressure drop** across the valve.

The three principal function-specified characteristics universally studied in KTU-process-engineering modules are:

| # | Function Name | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear** | $q = x$ |
| 2 | **Equal Percentage** | $q = R^{(x-1)}$ |
| 3 | **Quick Opening** | $q = \sqrt{x}$ |

where:
- $q = \dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}}$ → **normalized flow** (dimensionless, $0 \le q \le 1$)
- $x = \dfrac{l}{L}$ → **normalized stem position** (dimensionless, $0 \le x \le 1$)
- $R$ → **rangeability** (typical value $\mathbf{50}$, dimensionless)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Faucet-Tap Analogy** 🚰

Imagine a household water tap (faucet). When you turn the handle:

- A **Quick-Opening tap** delivers *almost full flow* within the first $10\%$ of handle rotation — useful when you need water *instantly* (e.g., garden hose, fire-fighting valve).
- A **Linear tap** delivers flow *proportional* to the handle rotation — $50\%$ rotation gives $50\%$ flow.
- An **Equal-Percentage tap** gives *tiny flow* initially but the flow grows *exponentially* as you keep turning — perfect for *fine-tuning* (e.g., a laboratory needle valve regulating reagent flow).

The "function specified" simply means: **the manufacturer has mathematically designed the internal plug/disc geometry so that the tap behaves according to one of these three mathematical functions**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in Process Engineering?**
> Because the choice of function determines how *sensitive* the flow is to small stem movements at different operating points. This directly affects **loop stability, controllability, and range of operation** of chemical reactors, distillation columns, and heat exchangers.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard values are mandated by **ISA-75.11 / IEC 60534** standards and should be memorized for KTU examinations:
>
> - **Standard Rangeability $R$ = $50$** (most common for equal-percentage valves)
> - **Maximum $C_v$** at $x = 1$ (fully open)
> - **Minimum controllable $C_v$** at $x = 0$ (fully closed)
> - **Inherent characteristic** = measured at *constant* $\Delta P$
> - **Installed characteristic** = measured under *actual* process pressure drop variations
> - The quantity $C_v$ (valve flow coefficient) has units of **US gal/min per $\sqrt{\text{psi}}$**.

---

## 1.4 Module 2 — Topic Map (KTU 2024 Context)

This topic sits within **Module 2: Process Control & Dynamic Response** and directly connects to:

1. **First-order and second-order system response** (Laplace domain analysis)
2. **Transfer function** of a control loop containing a valve
3. **Stability criteria** — Routh-Hurwitz / Bode plot
4. **Controller tuning** — Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon (where the choice of valve characteristic affects the *effective process gain*)

Mastering function-specified characteristics is therefore a **prerequisite for Module 3 (Controller Design)** and the **end-semester design problem** typically worth $14$ marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Function-Specified Characteristics — Step-by-Step Logic

### **Case 1: Linear Characteristic**

The **simplest possible function**. The flow varies *directly proportional* to the stem position.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Define the function: $f(x) = x$
2. At $x = 0$ → $q = 0$ (valve fully closed)
3. At $x = 1$ → $q = 1$ (valve fully open)
4. At $x = 0.5$ → $q = 0.5$ (half travel = half flow)
5. Slope is *constant*: $\dfrac{dq}{dx} = 1$ everywhere

**Mathematical Form:**

$$q = x$$

$$\frac{dq}{dx} = 1 = \text{constant}$$

**Engineering Use Case:** Used when the process gain is **constant** over the operating range — for example, in *level control* of a tank with constant cross-section, or *flow control* of an incompressible fluid in short pipes.

---

### **Case 2: Equal Percentage Characteristic**

The **most widely used in process industries**. Each equal increment of stem travel produces an equal *percentage* change in flow.

**Logic Steps:**
1. The differential equation governing the function is: $\dfrac{dq}{dx} = k \cdot q$, where $k$ is a constant.
2. Integrating: $\ln q = kx + C$
3. Applying boundary condition $q = 1$ at $x = 1$ gives $C = -k$, so: $\ln q = k(x-1)$
4. Defining rangeability $R = e^k$, we get the final form.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$q = R^{(x-1)}$$

$$\frac{dq}{dx} = R^{(x-1)} \cdot \ln R$$

**Engineering Use Case:** Used when the **process gain varies widely with operating conditions** — for example, in *exothermic reactors* where reaction rate sensitivity to temperature changes drastically, or in *pH control*, or when the manipulated variable has a large turndown ratio.

---

### **Case 3: Quick Opening Characteristic**

Also called the **on-off / square-root characteristic**. Flow rises *sharply* at small stem positions.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Define the function: $q = \sqrt{x} = x^{1/2}$
2. At $x = 0$ → $q = 0$
3. At $x = 0.25$ → $q = 0.5$ (i.e., only $25\%$ travel gives $50\%$ flow)
4. At $x = 1$ → $q = 1$
5. Slope: $\dfrac{dq}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$, which is *infinite* at $x = 0$ and *small* at $x = 1$.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$q = \sqrt{x}$$

$$\frac{dq}{dx} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$$

**Engineering Use Case:** Used in **safety valves, relief valves, blow-down valves, and on-off service** where rapid full flow is needed with minimal stem motion. Also used in some **level control** loops in pressure vessels.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — These equations are tested repeatedly in KTU university exams.**

| # | Parameter | Linear | Equal Percentage | Quick Opening |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Function form** | $q = x$ | $q = R^{(x-1)}$ | $q = \sqrt{x}$ |
| 2 | **Slope $\dfrac{dq}{dx}$** | $1$ | $R^{(x-1)} \ln R$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$ |
| 3 | **$q$ at $x=0.5$** | $0.5$ | $R^{-0.5}$ | $0.7071$ |
| 4 | **$q$ at $x=0.1$** | $0.1$ | $R^{-0.9}$ | $0.3162$ |
| 5 | **$q$ at $x=0.9$** | $0.9$ | $R^{-0.1}$ | $0.9487$ |
| 6 | **Sensitivity at small $x$** | Low | Lowest | Highest |
| 7 | **Sensitivity at large $x$** | Constant | Highest | Lowest |
| 8 | **Slope at $x=1$** | $1$ | $\ln R$ | $0.5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For numerical work, **rangeability $R = 50$** unless stated otherwise. Then $\ln R = \ln 50 = 3.912$.

---

## 2.3 Selection Criteria — Why One Function Over Another?

The "why" of choosing a function-specified valve is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. Use the following decision logic:

> **Step 1:** Identify if the *process gain* is constant across the operating range.
> - If **YES** → use **Linear** valve.
> - If **NO** (gain varies by more than 4:1) → use **Equal Percentage** to compensate.

> **Step 2:** Identify if the application is *safety-critical* or *on-off*.
> - If **YES** → use **Quick Opening** valve.

> **Step 3:** Verify that the *installed characteristic* (after pipe friction effects) still gives linear loop behavior.

This selection logic is the **practical engineering utility** of function-specified valves in chemical, petroleum, and power-plant process loops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equal-Percentage Characteristic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **single most tested derivation** in KTU Module 2 and carries a guaranteed 7 marks whenever it appears.

**Given:** The defining physical property of an equal-percentage valve is that *equal increments of stem travel produce equal percentage changes in flow capacity*. In other words, the *relative sensitivity* $\dfrac{1}{q} \dfrac{dq}{dx}$ is a constant.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Start with the defining differential equation:**

We require the relative rate of change of flow with stem position to be a constant. This means:

$$\frac{1}{q} \cdot \frac{dq}{dx} = k = \text{constant}$$

where $k$ is a positive constant determined by the valve's design.

**Step 2 — Separate the variables:**

Rearranging the above equation to put all $q$-terms on the left and all $x$-terms on the right:

$$\frac{dq}{q} = k \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides:**

Performing indefinite integration on both sides:

$$\int \frac{dq}{q} = \int k \, dx$$

$$\ln q = kx + C$$

where $C$ is the constant of integration.

**Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition at fully-open position:**

The valve is **fully open** at $x = 1$, at which point the flow $q$ attains its maximum value of $1$ (i.e., $100\%$ flow). Substituting $x = 1$, $q = 1$:

$$\ln 1 = k(1) + C$$

$$0 = k + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = -k$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the explicit form:**

Putting $C = -k$ back into the integrated equation:

$$\ln q = kx - k = k(x - 1)$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$q = e^{k(x-1)}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the rangeability parameter $R$:**

The **rangeability** $R$ is defined as the ratio of maximum to minimum controllable flow, occurring between $x = 1$ and $x = 0$:

$$R = \frac{q(x=1)}{q(x=0)} = \frac{e^{k(1-1)}}{e^{k(0-1)}} = \frac{1}{e^{-k}} = e^{k}$$

Therefore $k = \ln R$, and the function form becomes:

$$\boxed{\,q = R^{(x-1)}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical verification (with $R = 50$):**

| $x$ | $q = 50^{(x-1)}$ |
|---|---|
| $0.0$ | $50^{-1} = 0.0200$ |
| $0.2$ | $50^{-0.8} = 0.0373$ |
| $0.4$ | $50^{-0.6} = 0.0696$ |
| $0.6$ | $50^{-0.4} = 0.1299$ |
| $0.8$ | $50^{-0.2} = 0.2425$ |
| $1.0$ | $50^{0} = 1.0000$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** At $x = 0.2$ the flow is only $\approx 3.7\%$ — confirming that the equal-percentage valve passes very little flow at low stem positions, but as stem moves toward full open, flow grows rapidly. This is the *fine-then-fast* control behavior that makes it ideal for wide-range processes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Installed vs. Inherent Characteristic Relationship

The *inherent* characteristic is measured at constant pressure drop. In the *installed* condition, the pressure drop across the valve changes with flow because of piping friction losses. The mathematical relationship is:

**Let:**
- $\Delta P_v$ = pressure drop across the valve
- $\Delta P_{total}$ = total system pressure drop (constant, from pump)
- $\Delta P_p$ = pressure drop in piping (proportional to $Q^2$)
- $F_L$ = pressure recovery factor

**Installed flow equation:**

$$Q_{installed} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_v}{\rho \cdot G_f}}$$

**Piping pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P_p = \Delta P_{total} - \Delta P_v$$

Substituting the inherent flow relation and using $x = Q_{installed}/Q_{max}$:

$$x^2 = q^2 \left[ 1 - \frac{\Delta P_{p,max}}{\Delta P_{total}} (1 - q^2) \right]$$

Solving for the installed characteristic requires numerical methods, but the key takeaway is:

$$\boxed{\,\text{An inherent equal-percentage valve, when installed in a piping system with significant friction, behaves approximately linearly.}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is a frequently asked KTU conceptual question:** *"Why does an installed equal-percentage valve often appear linear?"*
> **Answer:** Because piping friction "distorts" the convex exponential curve into a near-straight line, making the installed characteristic approximately linear for moderate friction ratios.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Three Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this analytical comparison — questions of the form "Compare and contrast the three characteristics" appear every KTU cycle.**

| Property | Linear | Equal Percentage | Quick Opening |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Curve shape** | Straight line | Convex exponential | Concave square-root |
| **Slope trend** | Constant | Increases with $x$ | Decreases with $x$ |
| **Gain at low flow** | High | Low | Very high |
| **Gain at high flow** | High | Very high | Low |
| **Turndown range** | Poor | Excellent | Poor |
| **Rangeability** | Limited | $30$–$50$ typical | $10$–$20$ |
| **Best suited for** | Constant-gain processes | Variable-gain processes | Safety / on-off |
| **Industry usage** | Rare | $\approx 70\%$ of loops | $\approx 5\%$ of loops |
| **Control quality at small flows** | Poor (over-sensitive) | Excellent (under-sensitive) | Very poor |
| **Mathematical form** | $q=x$ | $q=R^{(x-1)}$ | $q=\sqrt{x}$ |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For an equal-percentage valve with rangeability $R = 50$, calculate the flow at stem positions $x = 0.3$ and $x = 0.7$. Comment on the *gain* at these positions.

**Solution:**

At $x = 0.3$:

$$q_1 = 50^{(0.3-1)} = 50^{-0.7} = e^{-0.7 \cdot \ln 50} = e^{-0.7 \cdot 3.9120} = e^{-2.7384} = 0.0647$$

At $x = 0.7$:

$$q_2 = 50^{(0.7-1)} = 50^{-0.3} = e^{-0.3 \cdot 3.9120} = e^{-1.1736} = 0.3092$$

**Gain (slope) at $x = 0.3$:**

$$\frac{dq}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0.3} = 0.0647 \times \ln 50 = 0.0647 \times 3.9120 = 0.2531$$

**Gain (slope) at $x = 0.7$:**

$$\frac{dq}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0.7} = 0.3092 \times \ln 50 = 0.3092 \times 3.9120 = 1.2096$$

**Comment:** The gain at $x = 0.7$ is approximately **$4.78$ times** the gain at $x = 0.3$. This non-constant gain is exactly why *equal-percentage valves are paired with PID controllers having integral action* — to compensate for the variable loop gain across the operating range.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "function-specified" as applied to control valves used in process industries. List the three standard function-specified characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A control valve is termed *function-specified* when its inherent flow characteristic — the relationship between the fractional flow $q = Q/Q_{max}$ and the fractional stem travel $x = l/L$ under constant pressure drop — is defined by an explicit mathematical function $q = f(x)$.
The three standard function-specified characteristics are:
1. **Linear** characteristic: $q = x$
2. **Equal-percentage** characteristic: $q = R^{(x-1)}$
3. **Quick-opening** characteristic: $q = \sqrt{x}$

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State any three reasons for preferring an equal-percentage control valve over a linear valve in process control loops.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Wide rangeability:** Equal-percentage valves have a typical rangeability of $R = 30$–$50$, allowing precise control over a much wider range of flows than linear valves.
2. **Compensation of variable process gain:** When process gain varies significantly across the operating range, the *increasing* slope of the equal-percentage curve compensates the *decreasing* process gain, yielding a more uniform overall loop gain.
3. **Tight shut-off and fine throttling at low flow:** At small stem positions, the flow is extremely small (e.g., $q = 0.02$ at $x = 0$ for $R = 50$), giving excellent resolution for low-flow service such as reagent dosing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries one *internal-choice* question of 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must attempt **either OR** — not both. Practice both alternatives below.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) Derive the mathematical expression for the equal-percentage control valve characteristic, starting from its defining differential equation. State clearly the meaning of the rangeability parameter $R$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** The defining property of an equal-percentage valve is that equal increments of stem travel produce equal *percentage* changes in flow. In differential form:

$$\frac{1}{q} \cdot \frac{dq}{dx} = k = \text{constant} \tag{1}$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging and integrating:

$$\int \frac{dq}{q} = \int k \, dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln q = kx + C \tag{2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply boundary condition $q = 1$ at $x = 1$ (fully open):

$$C = -k \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln q = k(x-1) \tag{3}$$

**Step 4:** Define the rangeability $R$ as the ratio $q(x=1)/q(x=0) = e^k$, so $k = \ln R$:

$$\boxed{\,q = R^{(x-1)}\,}$$

**Rangeability $R$** is the ratio of the maximum controllable flow to the minimum controllable flow. For commercial equal-percentage valves, $R = 30$–$50$ (commonly $50$).

---

**(b) An equal-percentage valve with $R = 50$ is installed in a flow control loop. Calculate the flow $q$ and the sensitivity $\dfrac{dq}{dx}$ at stem positions $x = 0.2$ and $x = 0.8$. Comment on the variation in sensitivity.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Given $R = 50$, so $\ln R = \ln 50 = 3.9120$.

**At $x = 0.2$:**

$$q_1 = 50^{(0.2-1)} = 50^{-0.8} = e^{-0.8 \times 3.9120} = e^{-3.1296} = 0.0438$$

$$\left.\frac{dq}{dx}\right|_{x=0.2} = q_1 \cdot \ln 50 = 0.0438 \times 3.9120 = 0.1713$$

**At $x = 0.8$:**

$$q_2 = 50^{(0.8-1)} = 50^{-0.2} = e^{-0.2 \times 3.9120} = e^{-0.7824} = 0.4573$$

$$\left.\frac{dq}{dx}\right|_{x=0.8} = q_2 \cdot \ln 50 = 0.4573 \times 3.9120 = 1.7890$$

**Step 2 — Comment:**
The sensitivity at $x = 0.8$ is approximately **$10.44$ times** the sensitivity at $x = 0.2$. This shows that the equal-percentage valve has *low sensitivity* at small openings and *high sensitivity* at large openings — an *intentionally non-linear* design feature used to compensate for processes whose gain decreases at higher throughputs.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Compare and contrast the three function-specified control valve characteristics (linear, equal-percentage, quick-opening) with the help of a table covering at least six properties. State one typical application of each.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Property | Linear | Equal Percentage | Quick Opening |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Function** | $q = x$ | $q = R^{(x-1)}$ | $q = \sqrt{x}$ |
| **Slope $dq/dx$** | $1$ (constant) | Increases with $x$ | Decreases with $x$ |
| **Curve shape** | Straight line | Convex exponential | Concave square-root |
| **Sensitivity at low flow** | High (excessive) | Low (desirable) | Very high |
| **Rangeability** | Poor ($< 10$) | Excellent ($30$–$50$) | Poor ($< 20$) |
| **Best application** | Level control in constant-area tank | Temperature control of exothermic reactor | Safety relief and blow-down valves |

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

- The **linear** characteristic has constant gain — unsuitable when the process gain is variable. Used in level control of cylindrical tanks.
- The **equal-percentage** characteristic has *increasing* gain that compensates for *decreasing* process gain in reactors, distillation columns, and pH loops.
- The **quick-opening** characteristic has very high gain near closure — used in safety shut-off and on-off service where full flow is needed urgently.

---

**(b) Show that for an installed equal-percentage valve, the relationship between the installed and inherent characteristics is governed by the equation $x^2 = q^2[1 - (1 - q^2)/\Delta P_{total}]$. Discuss why an installed equal-percentage valve often behaves approximately linearly.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Let the system have total pressure drop $\Delta P_{total}$ = constant, and let the piping friction loss at maximum flow be $\Delta P_{p,max}$. The pressure drop available across the valve at any installed flow $q$ is:

$$\Delta P_v = \Delta P_{total} - \Delta P_{p,max} \cdot q^2$$

**Step 2:** The installed flow (in terms of $x$) is:

$$x^2 = \frac{\Delta P_v}{\Delta P_{total}} = 1 - \frac{\Delta P_{p,max}}{\Delta P_{total}} \cdot q^2$$

For a normalized system where $\Delta P_{p,max} = 1$:

$$x^2 = 1 - q^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q^2 = 1 - x^2$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the inherent characteristic $q_{inh} = R^{(x-1)}$ into the installed-flow expression yields a complex non-linear relationship, but for moderate friction ratios (typically $0.3$–$0.6$), the convex exponential of the inherent curve is *distorted* into a near-straight line.

**Step 4 — Discussion:**
The piping friction causes the *effective* pressure drop across the valve to *decrease* as flow increases. This reduction in driving pressure flattens the high-flow end of the inherent curve, pulling the convex exponential into a near-linear shape. Hence, an installed equal-percentage valve frequently exhibits a **near-linear installed characteristic**, which is one of the main reasons it is the industry default — the inherent non-linearity *self-corrects* in the real piping environment.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2–4 marks each in KTU valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the rangeability formula** $R = e^k$ when deriving equal-percentage — most common $1$ mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up quick-opening and equal-percentage** when plotting curves. Quick-opening is *concave* (bulges upward), equal-percentage is *convex* (bulges downward).
> 3. **Using the wrong boundary condition.** Always $q = 1$ at $x = 1$ for the open end, *not* $q = 0$.
> 4. **Confusing inherent with installed characteristics.** The functions $q = f(x)$ are *inherent*. Any reference to piping friction produces the *installed* characteristic — examiners deduct marks if you don't distinguish.
> 5. **Failing to state the standard $R = 50$** in numerical problems when no value is given.
> 6. **Writing $q = R^{x}$ instead of $q = R^{(x-1)}$.** The exponent must be $(x-1)$, not just $x$, because the boundary condition $q(x=1) = 1$ is part of the definition.

---

## 5.4 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Glance at this list 5 minutes before entering the exam hall — it covers every high-frequency KTU point on Function-Specified Valves.**

- ✅ **Function Specified** = the inherent flow characteristic $q = f(x)$ is defined by an explicit mathematical function under constant $\Delta P$.
- ✅ The three characteristics are: **Linear** ($q = x$), **Equal-Percentage** ($q = R^{(x-1)}$), and **Quick-Opening** ($q = \sqrt{x}$).
- ✅ **Rangeability** $R$ is the ratio of max-to-min controllable flow; for equal-percentage valves $R = 50$ unless otherwise stated.
- ✅ The **equal-percentage** characteristic is derived from the differential equation $\dfrac{1}{q}\dfrac{dq}{dx} = \text{constant}$.
- ✅ The **boundary condition** is $q = 1$ at $x = 1$ (valve fully open).
- ✅ **Equal-percentage** has *low* sensitivity at low flow and *high* sensitivity at high flow — used for processes with variable gain.
- ✅ **Linear** has *constant* sensitivity — used for processes with constant gain.
- ✅ **Quick-opening** has *very high* sensitivity at low flow — used for safety relief and on-off service.
- ✅ The **installed** characteristic differs from the **inherent** characteristic due to piping friction; an installed equal-percentage valve often appears **near-linear** in real plants.
- ✅ The selection criterion is: **rangeability of process > 4:1 → Equal Percentage; constant process gain → Linear; safety service → Quick Opening.**
- ✅ Always remember $\ln 50 = 3.912$ for quick numerical work in the exam.
- ✅ The equal-percentage curve is **convex** (bulges downward) and the quick-opening curve is **concave** (bulges upward) — opposite curvatures.
- ✅ The sensitivity of equal-percentage at any $x$ is $dq/dx = q \ln R$.
- ✅ The sensitivity of linear at any $x$ is $dq/dx = 1$.
- ✅ The sensitivity of quick-opening at any $x$ is $dq/dx = 1/(2\sqrt{x})$.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Function Specified, Module 2, PECHT525**

topics.title: Derivative Specified and Mixed Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Derivative Specified and Mixed Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the mathematical modeling of transport processes (heat, mass, and momentum transfer), a **Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** governing a physical field $u(x,t)$ must always be accompanied by **boundary conditions (BCs)** that specify what happens at the spatial boundaries of the domain. While a **Dirichlet (specified) condition** prescribes the value of the dependent variable at the boundary, **Derivative Specified** and **Mixed Conditions** prescribe the derivative (or a combination of the variable and its derivative) at the boundary.

The three principal categories encountered in Module 2 are:

1. **Derivative Specified (Neumann) Condition** — The normal derivative of $u$ at the boundary is prescribed:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\text{boundary}} = g(x,t)$$

2. **Mixed (Robin / Convective) Condition** — A linear combination of $u$ and its normal derivative is prescribed:
$$a\,u + b\,\frac{\partial u}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\text{boundary}} = c$$

3. **Cauchy (Over-specified) Condition** — Both $u$ and its normal derivative are prescribed on the same boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In process engineering, the most physically relevant non-Dirichlet condition is the **convective (Newton's law of cooling) boundary condition**:
> $$-k\frac{\partial u}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\text{boundary}} = h(u - u_\infty)$$
> which is a *Robin-type mixed condition* and is the standard model for heat/mass loss from a process equipment surface to the surrounding fluid.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heated Rod Story"

Imagine a long metal rod being used as a process heater. We want to know the temperature $T(x,t)$ at every point $x$ along the rod at any time $t$.

- **Dirichlet (specified value):** You clamp the left end of the rod into a bath of boiling water — the temperature *value* at $x=0$ is fixed at $T_s$ (e.g., **100 °C = 373.15 K**). This is a *value-specified* condition.

- **Neumann (derivative specified):** You wrap the right end in thick insulation. No heat flux can escape. By Fourier's law, the heat flux is $q = -k\,\partial T/\partial x$, so insulation forces:
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} = 0$$
  The *rate of change* of temperature is fixed at zero — not the temperature itself.

- **Mixed (Robin/convective):** You expose the right end to a flowing air stream. Heat is lost by convection at rate $h(T - T_\infty)$:
$$-k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} = h\big(T(L,t) - T_\infty\big)$$
  Now both $T$ *and* its derivative appear together on the boundary — that is "mixed."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the derivative appear?** Because **Fourier's law of heat conduction**, **Fick's law of diffusion**, and **Newton's law of viscosity** all state that a *flux* is proportional to a *spatial gradient* (derivative) of the driving potential. Whenever a physical flux is prescribed at a wall, you end up with a derivative-specified boundary condition.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants frequently appear in problems involving derivative/mixed conditions for process engineering:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (Engineering Range) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity | **15 W/(m·K)** (stainless steel) to **237 W/(m·K)** (aluminum) |
| $h$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | **5 W/(m²·K)** (natural air) to **10⁴ W/(m²·K)** (forced water) |
| $D_{AB}$ | Binary mass diffusivity | **10⁻⁵ m²/s** (gas) to **10⁻⁹ m²/s** (liquid) |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity | **10⁻³ Pa·s** (water at 20 °C) |
| $T_\infty$ | Ambient/far-field temperature | **298 K** (standard reference) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Classification of Boundary Conditions

Consider a second-order linear PDE defined on a domain $\Omega$ with boundary $\partial \Omega$ and outward unit normal vector $\mathbf{n}$. For the dependent variable $u$:

### Type I — Dirichlet (Value Specified)
$$u(\mathbf{x},t)\big|_{\partial \Omega} = f(\mathbf{x},t)$$
*Physical meaning:* The boundary is held at a *fixed potential* (e.g., a perfectly conducting wall, a concentration reservoir).

### Type II — Neumann (Derivative Specified)
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\partial \Omega} = g(\mathbf{x},t)$$
*Physical meaning:* The boundary *flux* is prescribed (insulated end, prescribed current, prescribed heat input).

### Type III — Robin / Mixed
$$a\,u + b\,\frac{\partial u}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\partial \Omega} = c$$
*Physical meaning:* The boundary exchanges energy/mass with the surroundings by convection or radiation (e.g., Newton's law of cooling).

### Type IV — Cauchy (Over-determined)
Both $u$ and $\partial u/\partial n$ specified on the same boundary. Used only in well-posedness analysis; not common in single-process physical problems.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Type

- **Why specify a derivative?** Engineering processes are usually controlled by **fluxes** (heat input from a heater, mass feed from a pipe, shear from a wall), not by the field value itself. Flux = $-k\,\partial u/\partial n$ is the natural control variable.
- **Why mixed?** Almost every *real* industrial boundary is not perfectly insulated and not perfectly clamped — heat is lost to air, mass is lost to a purge stream, momentum is lost to wall friction. The mixed condition captures this reality.
- **Why a compatibility condition?** A Neumann problem on a closed domain (e.g., a circular rod) requires the *total input flux* to equal the *total output flux* for steady state, otherwise the problem is physically inconsistent.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Condition Type | General Form | Physical Origin | Engineering Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Dirichlet | $u = f$ | Surface at fixed potential | Clamped end of heat exchanger tube |
| 2 | Homogeneous Neumann | $\partial u/\partial n = 0$ | Insulated / impermeable | Insulated reactor wall |
| 3 | Non-homogeneous Neumann | $\partial u/\partial n = g$ | Prescribed flux | Electric heater at $x=L$ |
| 4 | Robin (convective) | $-k\,\partial u/\partial n = h(u - u_\infty)$ | Newton's law of cooling | Fin losing heat to flowing air |
| 5 | Biot-number form | $\partial u/\partial n = -\mathrm{Bi}\,(u - u_\infty)$ | Dimensionless convective BC | Compact representation of (4) |
| 6 | Compatibility (steady) | $\int_{\partial \Omega} g\,dS = 0$ | Conservation on closed domain | Required for solvability of Neumann |
| 7 | Radiation (nonlinear) | $-k\,\partial u/\partial n = \varepsilon\sigma(u^4 - u_\infty^4)$ | Stefan-Boltzmann law | High-temperature furnace wall |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Biot number** $\mathrm{Bi} = hL/k$ is a dimensionless group that compares internal conduction resistance to external convection resistance. When $\mathrm{Bi} \to 0$, the Robin condition reduces to a Neumann condition (insulated). When $\mathrm{Bi} \to \infty$, it reduces to a Dirichlet condition (fixed surface temperature).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Heat exchangers:** Mixed conditions model the heat loss from finned tubes to the surroundings.
- **Chemical reactors:** Neumann conditions represent prescribed mass flux (e.g., controlled injection of reactant at a port).
- **Distillation columns:** Mixed conditions at column ends model partial cooling/condensation.
- **Pipe flow:** Derivative-specified conditions on the velocity field represent prescribed wall shear stress (non-slip is the limit of infinite resistance).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Problem Setup

We consider the **one-dimensional transient heat conduction equation** in a uniform rod of length $L$ and thermal diffusivity $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha\,\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}, \quad 0 < x < L, \quad t > 0$$

subject to initial condition $u(x,0) = \phi(x)$ and various combinations of boundary conditions at $x=0$ and $x=L$. The variable $u(x,t)$ represents temperature deviation from a reference (or concentration, with $\alpha$ replaced by $D_{AB}$ for mass transfer).

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Steady-State Problem with Two Neumann Conditions

**Problem Statement:** Find the steady-state temperature distribution $u(x)$ in a rod of length $L$ when the heat flux is prescribed at *both* ends:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} = q_0, \qquad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} = q_L$$
There is no source term, so $\partial^2 u/\partial x^2 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Write the steady-state governing equation.**
At steady state, $\partial u/\partial t = 0$, so the PDE reduces to:
$$\frac{d^{2} u}{d x^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once.**
Integrating once with respect to $x$ gives the first derivative:
$$\frac{d u}{d x} = C_1$$
where $C_1$ is a constant of integration.

**Step 3 — Integrate a second time.**
$$u(x) = C_1\,x + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition at $x=0$.**
$$\frac{d u}{d x}\bigg|_{x=0} = C_1 = q_0$$

**Step 5 — Apply the boundary condition at $x=L$.**
$$\frac{d u}{d x}\bigg|_{x=L} = C_1 = q_L$$

**Step 6 — Compatibility check.**
For a non-trivial solution to exist, we require:
$$q_0 = q_L$$
This is the **compatibility condition** for the Neumann problem: *the flux entering one end must equal the flux leaving the other end*. Physically, energy must be conserved.

**Step 7 — Final solution.**
With $C_1 = q_0$ and $C_2$ remaining as a free constant (we cannot determine the absolute temperature from a pure Neumann problem — we need a reference):
$$\boxed{u(x) = q_0\,x + C_2}$$
The constant $C_2$ is the temperature at $x=0$, which is *not* fixed by the problem. We can only determine $u(x)$ up to an additive constant — a hallmark of the Neumann problem.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Mixed (Robin) Condition on a Semi-infinite Slab

**Problem Statement:** A semi-infinite solid initially at uniform temperature $T_\infty$ has its surface at $x=0$ suddenly exposed to convection with a fluid at $T_s$. Find $T(x,t)$ for $t>0$, $x>0$:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha\,\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$
Initial condition: $T(x,0) = T_\infty$
Convective BC at $x=0$: $\;-k\,\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} = h\big(T(0,t) - T_s\big)$
BC at infinity: $\;T(x,t) \to T_\infty$ as $x \to \infty$

**Step 1 — Non-dimensionalise.** Define:
$$\theta = \frac{T - T_s}{T_\infty - T_s}, \quad \eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}$$

**Step 2 — Transform the PDE using the similarity variable $\eta$.**
Using the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \frac{d\theta}{d\eta}\,\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = -\frac{\eta}{2t}\,\frac{d\theta}{d\eta}$$
$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x} = \frac{d\theta}{d\eta}\,\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\,\frac{d\theta}{d\eta}$$
$$\frac{\partial^{2}\theta}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{4\alpha t}\,\frac{d^{2}\theta}{d\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the PDE.**
$$-\frac{\eta}{2t}\,\frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{4\alpha t}\,\frac{d^{2}\theta}{d\eta^{2}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $4t$:
$$-2\eta\,\frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = \frac{d^{2}\theta}{d\eta^{2}}$$

This is the classical **Biot-type error function ODE**:
$$\frac{d^{2}\theta}{d\eta^{2}} + 2\eta\,\frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the ODE by letting $p = d\theta/d\eta$.**
$$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta\,p = 0$$
$$\frac{dp}{p} = -2\eta\,d\eta$$
$$\ln \vert p \vert = -\eta^{2} + C$$
$$p = A\,e^{-\eta^{2}}$$
where $A$ is a constant.

**Step 5 — Integrate to find $\theta(\eta)$.**
$$\theta(\eta) = A\int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-s^{2}}\,ds + B = A\cdot\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,\mathrm{erf}(\eta) + B$$

**Step 6 — Apply the condition at infinity.**
As $\eta \to \infty$, $T \to T_\infty$, so $\theta \to 1$ and $\mathrm{erf}(\infty) = 1$:
$$1 = A\cdot\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 1 - A\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the convective boundary condition at $\eta = 0$ (i.e., $x = 0$).**
First compute the derivative:
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} = \frac{T_\infty - T_s}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \cdot A \cdot e^{0} = \frac{(T_\infty - T_s)\,A}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}$$
The convective BC reads:
$$-k \cdot \frac{(T_\infty - T_s)\,A}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} = h\big(T_s - T_s\big) = h\cdot \theta(0)\cdot(T_\infty - T_s)$$

Since $\theta(0) = B = 1 - A\sqrt{\pi}/2$:
$$-\frac{kA}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} = h\left(1 - A\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\right)$$

Defining the **Biot number** $\mathrm{Bi} = h\sqrt{\pi\,\alpha t}/k$ at the surface and rearranging:
$$A = -\frac{h\,\sqrt{\pi\,\alpha t}}{k} = -\mathrm{Bi}$$
Substituting back:
$$B = 1 + \mathrm{Bi}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\cdot...$$
After algebra (and absorbing a sign convention), the standard form is:

$$\boxed{\frac{T(x,t) - T_s}{T_\infty - T_s} = \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right) + \frac{h\sqrt{\pi\alpha t}}{k}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}}{4\alpha t}\right)\cdot...}$$

The exact closed form is recorded in classical texts (Carslaw & Jaeger). The key engineering insight is:

> [!NOTE]
> The solution is *not* a pure error function (which would be the case for a surface at fixed $T_s$). The convective BC introduces an **additional exponential term** weighted by $\mathrm{Bi}$, representing the finite-rate heat removal at the surface.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Separation of Variables with a Mixed BC

**Problem Statement:** Solve
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha\,\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}, \quad 0 < x < L$$
with $u(0,t) = 0$ (Dirichlet) and a Robin condition at $x=L$:
$$-k\,\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} = h\,u(L,t)$$

**Step 1 — Assume a product solution** $u(x,t) = X(x)\,T(t)$. Substituting:
$$X\,T' = \alpha\,X''\,T \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{T'}{\alpha T} = \frac{X''}{X} = -\lambda$$

**Step 2 — Spatial eigenvalue problem.**
$$X'' + \lambda X = 0, \quad X(0) = 0, \quad X'(L) = -\frac{h}{k}\,X(L)$$

**Step 3 — General solution.** For $\lambda = \beta^{2} > 0$:
$$X(x) = A\sin(\beta x) + B\cos(\beta x)$$

**Step 4 — Apply $X(0) = 0$:** $B = 0$, so $X(x) = A\sin(\beta x)$.

**Step 5 — Apply the Robin condition.**
$$X'(L) = A\beta\cos(\beta L) = -\frac{h}{k}\,A\sin(\beta L)$$

This yields the **transcendental eigenvalue equation**:
$$\boxed{\beta\cos(\beta L) + \frac{h}{k}\sin(\beta L) = 0}$$
or equivalently, with $\mu = \beta L$:
$$\tan(\mu) = -\frac{\mu k}{hL} = -\frac{\mu}{\mathrm{Bi}_{L}}$$

The eigenvalues $\mu_n$ (and hence $\beta_n = \mu_n/L$) are the roots of this equation — they are *not* the simple integer multiples $n\pi$ that occur in the Dirichlet-Dirichlet case.

**Step 6 — Time part.**
$$T_n(t) = \exp(-\alpha \beta_n^{2}\,t)$$

**Step 7 — Superposition.**
$$u(x,t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} c_n \sin(\beta_n x)\,\exp(-\alpha \beta_n^{2}\,t)$$

The coefficients $c_n$ are determined by the initial condition $u(x,0) = \phi(x)$ using the orthogonality of the eigenfunctions in the weighted inner product:
$$\int_{0}^{L} \sin(\beta_m x)\sin(\beta_n x)\,dx + \frac{k}{h}\sin(\beta_m L)\sin(\beta_n L) = 0 \text{ for } m \neq n$$

## 3.5 Summary of the Analytical Workflow

| Step | Action | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Identify the type of BC at each end | Dirichlet / Neumann / Robin |
| 2 | Check compatibility for closed-domain Neumann | $\int g\,dS = 0$ |
| 3 | Choose solution method | Separation of variables / Laplace transform / Similarity |
| 4 | For Robin BC: derive the transcendental eigenvalue equation | $\tan(\mu) = f(\mathrm{Bi})$ |
| 5 | Apply initial condition via orthogonality | Determine $c_n$ |
| 6 | Express final solution as a series | $u(x,t) = \sum c_n \phi_n(x) e^{-\alpha\beta_n^{2}t}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The structural flow confirms the **unifying principle**: regardless of the physical transport mechanism, the boundary condition is dictated by the corresponding *constitutive law* (Fourier, Fick, Newton). This is why the same mathematical machinery (separation of variables, eigenvalue problems, eigenfunction expansions) is reused across all three transport processes.
