# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme follows **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Each question maps to specific Course Outcomes (CO) and Bloom's Taxonomy Levels (BTL). Model answers below reflect the depth expected for full marks.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define parameter estimation. State the principle of least squares as applied to fitting algebraic models in heat and mass transfer.**

**Model Answer:**

Parameter estimation is the statistical procedure of determining the unknown constants of a mathematical model from experimental data. In heat and mass transfer, it is used to find empirical coefficients in correlations such as $\text{Nu} = C\,\text{Re}^m\,\text{Pr}^n$.

**Principle of Least Squares:** The unknown parameters are chosen such that the sum of the squares of the deviations (residuals) between the observed values $y_i$ and the model predictions $\hat{y}_i$ is minimized.

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \text{minimum}$$

The resulting estimators are *unbiased* and have *minimum variance* among all linear unbiased estimators (Gauss–Markov theorem). **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Explain with an example why non-linear models in heat and mass transfer are often linearized before parameter estimation.**

**Model Answer:**

Non-linear models (e.g., $y = ax^b$ or $y = ae^{bx}$) cannot be solved by direct application of the linear least-squares normal equations. By applying a suitable mathematical transformation (logarithm, reciprocal), the model can be cast in the linear form $Y = A + bX$, allowing use of the closed-form LLS formulas.

**Example:** The convective correlation $\text{Nu} = C\,\text{Re}^m$ is linearized as $\ln \text{Nu} = \ln C + m\ln \text{Re}$, which is a straight line in $(\ln \text{Re}, \ln \text{Nu})$ space with slope $m$ and intercept $\ln C$. This yields both $C$ and $m$ analytically without iterative numerical search. **[3 marks]**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> **ESE Structure (Per KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Internal choice** is provided between two complete alternatives (Q-A or Q-B).

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the normal equations for the linear least-squares fit of the model $y = a + bx$ to a set of $n$ data points $(x_i, y_i)$. Show that the slope is given by $b = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** In a double-pipe heat exchanger, the following steady-state data are recorded:

| $\Delta T$ (K) | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 24 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Q$ (W) | 180 | 270 | 350 | 420 | 510 |

Fit the model $Q = U A\,\Delta T$ using the method of least squares. Determine $UA$ and the goodness of fit. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation of Normal Equations**

The sum of squared residuals is:

$$S(a,b) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[y_i - (a + bx_i)\right]^2$$

For a minimum, the first-order partial derivatives vanish:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - a - bx_i\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i\left(y_i - a - bx_i\right) = 0$$

Expanding the first equation:

$$na + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i \quad \text{...(Normal Eq. 1)}$$

Expanding the second:

$$a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i \quad \text{...(Normal Eq. 2)}$$

Solving Eq. (1) for $a$:

$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$$

Substituting into Eq. (2):

$$(\bar{y} - b\bar{x})\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i$$

Since $\sum x_i = n\bar{x}$:

$$n\bar{x}\bar{y} - bn\bar{x}^2 + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i$$

$$b\left(\sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2\right) = \sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $n$ and using the identity $\sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\left[n\sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2\right]$:

$$\boxed{\;b = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2}\;}$$

and

$$\boxed{\;a = \frac{\sum y_i - b\sum x_i}{n}\;}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Fit**

Compute the necessary summations:

- $n = 5$
- $\sum x_i = 8 + 12 + 16 + 20 + 24 = 80$
- $\sum y_i = 180 + 270 + 350 + 420 + 510 = 1730$
- $\sum x_i y_i = (8)(180) + (12)(270) + (16)(350) + (20)(420) + (24)(510) = 1440 + 3240 + 5600 + 8400 + 12240 = 30920$
- $\sum x_i^2 = 64 + 144 + 256 + 400 + 576 = 1440$

Slope:

$$b = UA = \frac{(5)(30920) - (80)(1730)}{(5)(1440) - (80)^2} = \frac{154600 - 138400}{7200 - 6400} = \frac{16200}{800} = 20.25\;\text{W/K}$$

Intercept:

$$a = \frac{1730 - (20.25)(80)}{5} = \frac{1730 - 1620}{5} = \frac{110}{5} = 22.0\;\text{W}$$

Fitted model:

$$\boxed{\;Q = 22.0 + 20.25\,\Delta T\;\text{W}\;}$$

Goodness-of-fit check using the residuals:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i = 22 + 20.25x_i$ | $y_i - \hat{y}_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | 180 | 184.0 | $-4.0$ |
| 12 | 270 | 265.0 | $+5.0$ |
| 16 | 350 | 346.0 | $+4.0$ |
| 20 | 420 | 427.0 | $-7.0$ |
| 24 | 510 | 508.0 | $+2.0$ |

$\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i) = 0$ ✓ (confirms arithmetic).

Residual sum of squares:

$$SS_{res} = (-4)^2 + 5^2 + 4^2 + (-7)^2 + 2^2 = 16 + 25 + 16 + 49 + 4 = 110$$

Total sum of squares:

$$SS_{tot} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2, \quad \bar{y} = 346$$

$$SS_{tot} = (180-346)^2 + (270-346)^2 + (350-346)^2 + (420-346)^2 + (510-346)^2$$
$$= 27556 + 5776 + 16 + 5476 + 26896 = 65720$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{110}{65720} = 1 - 0.00167 = 0.9983$$

A very high $R^2$ confirms an excellent linear fit. The overall heat transfer conductance $UA = 20.25$ W/K.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the linearization procedure for fitting a non-linear model $y = a\,x^b$ to experimental data. Apply this to the following heat transfer data and find the constants $a$ and $b$:

| $x$ (Re) | 5000 | 10000 | 15000 | 20000 | 25000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ (Nu) | 21.0 | 38.5 | 53.0 | 67.5 | 81.0 |

**(b)** The drying rate of a solid is observed to follow $X(t) = X^* + (X_0 - X^*)e^{-kt}$. Show how the parameters $X^*$ and $k$ can be estimated by linearization, given a set of moisture-content vs. time data. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Linearization of the Power Law**

Take logarithms of $y = a x^b$:

$$\ln y = \ln a + b\ln x$$

Define $Y = \ln y$, $X = \ln x$, $A = \ln a$. The model becomes $Y = A + bX$ — a straight line.

Tabulate the transformed data:

| $x$ | $y$ | $X = \ln x$ | $Y = \ln y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5000 | 21.0 | 8.5172 | 3.0445 |
| 10000 | 38.5 | 9.2103 | 3.6507 |
| 15000 | 53.0 | 9.6158 | 3.9703 |
| 20000 | 67.5 | 9.9035 | 4.2121 |
| 25000 | 81.0 | 10.1266 | 4.3944 |

Summations:

- $n = 5$
- $\sum X = 47.3734$
- $\sum Y = 19.2720$
- $\sum X^2 = 449.61$
- $\sum XY = (8.5172)(3.0445) + (9.2103)(3.6507) + (9.6158)(3.9703) + (9.9035)(4.2121) + (10.1266)(4.3944)$
  - $= 25.930 + 33.626 + 38.179 + 41.715 + 44.504 = 183.954$

Slope $b$:

$$b = \frac{(5)(183.954) - (47.3734)(19.2720)}{(5)(449.61) - (47.3734)^2} = \frac{919.770 - 912.954}{2248.05 - 2244.24} = \frac{6.816}{3.81} = 0.789$$

Intercept $A$:

$$\bar{X} = 9.4747, \quad \bar{Y} = 3.8544$$

$$A = 3.8544 - (0.789)(9.4747) = 3.8544 - 7.4755 = -3.6211$$

Back-transform:

$$a = e^{A} = e^{-3.6211} = 0.0267$$

**Fitted correlation:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Nu} = 0.0267\,\text{Re}^{0.789}\;}$$

This is close to the classical Sieder–Tate form, validating the method.

---

**(b) Drying Rate Linearization**

The drying model is $X(t) = X^* + (X_0 - X^*)e^{-kt}$. Subtracting $X^*$:

$$X(t) - X^* = (X_0 - X^*)e^{-kt}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln[X(t) - X^*] = \ln(X_0 - X^*) - kt$$

For an *initial guess* of $X^*$, plot $\ln(X - X^*)$ vs. $t$ — the result should be a straight line with slope $-k$ and intercept $\ln(X_0 - X^*)$.

**Practical Procedure:**

1. **Trial value of $X^*$:** Plot $X$ vs. $t$. As $t \to \infty$, $X \to X^*$. So the asymptote of the curve gives an initial estimate of $X^*$.

2. **Linearization check:** For a given $X^*$, compute $Y_i = \ln(X_i - X^*)$ and $X_i = t_i$. Then fit $Y = \alpha + \beta X$ using LLS:

$$\beta = -k, \quad \alpha = \ln(X_0 - X^*)$$

3. **Iteration:** Because $X^*$ appears inside the logarithm, the value of $X^*$ must be guessed and refined. A common technique:
   - Try several values of $X^*$ and compute $R^2$ for each linear fit.
   - Choose the $X^*$ that maximizes $R^2$ (or minimizes the residual sum of squares).
   - This is a one-dimensional search; the optimum can be found by trial or by a simple bracketing method.

4. **Final parameter extraction:**

$$k = -\beta, \quad X_0 = e^{\alpha} + X^*$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners value explicit mention of the **iterative refinement** of $X^*$ — it is the key difference from a "pure" linear least-squares problem. Students who treat $X^*$ as a known constant lose significant marks.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Five Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting back-transformation** after linearization. If the linearized form has $A = \ln a$, you must compute $a = e^A$ at the end. The same applies to Arrhenius ($A = e^{\text{intercept}}$ and $E = -b \cdot R$).
>
> 2. **Using the wrong denominator** in the slope formula. Common errors include writing $n\sum x_i^2 - n(\sum x_i)^2$ (extra $n$) or $(\sum x_i)^2 - n\sum x_i^2$ (sign flipped).
>
> 3. **Mixing up units of the slope** (e.g., reporting $hA$ in W when the units are W/K).
>
> 4. **Not stating the linearized equation explicitly** before tabulating transformed variables. Examiners look for: "Take $\ln$ of both sides: $\ln y = \ln a + b \ln x$. Let $X = \ln x$, $Y = \ln y$." Skipping this step costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring goodness-of-fit metrics.** KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests $R^2$ and standard error. A model without validation is considered incomplete.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- **Definition:** Parameter estimation determines unknown constants in algebraic models by minimizing $\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$.
- **Linear LLS slope formula:**
$$b = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2}$$
- **Linear LLS intercept formula:**
$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = \frac{\sum y_i - b\sum x_i}{n}$$
- **Correlation coefficient $r$** lies in $[-1, +1]$; $R^2 = r^2$ for single-variable linear fit.
- **Standard error** uses $n - 2$ degrees of freedom.
- **Four Linearization Transformations** (must memorize):
  * Power law: $\ln y$ vs. $\ln x$ → slope = $b$, intercept = $\ln a$
  * Exponential: $\ln y$ vs. $x$ → slope = $b$, intercept = $\ln a$
  * Langmuir: $x/y$ vs. $x$ → slope = $b$, intercept = $1/a$
  * Arrhenius: $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ → slope = $-E/R$, intercept = $\ln A$
- **Always back-transform** intercepts of logarithmic fits using $a = e^A$.
- **Residuals must sum to zero** for an unbiased fit — use this as a self-check.
- **Engineering applications** to remember: $Q = hA\Delta T$, $\text{Nu} = C\text{Re}^m\text{Pr}^n$, $\text{Sh} = C\text{Re}^m\text{Sc}^n$, $k = Ae^{-E/RT}$, $X(t) = X^* + (X_0 - X^*)e^{-kt}$.
- **Examiners expect:** statement of the linearized form, tabulation of transformed variables, calculation of summations, evaluation of slope and intercept, back-transformation, and goodness-of-fit assessment.
- **Real-world tie-in:** A well-fitted correlation directly enables safe and economical design of heat exchangers, cooling towers, absorbers, dryers, and chemical reactors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinetics and Thermodynamics by the Method of Averages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kinetics and Thermodynamics by the Method of Averages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Method of Averages (Point-Slope Grouping Method):**
> The Method of Averages is a **deterministic, linear-regression-free parameter estimation technique** used in Chemical Reaction Engineering and Thermodynamics to evaluate the slope and intercept of a straight line fitted to experimental data of the form $Y = a + bX$. The dataset is partitioned into two (or more) groups; the mean of $X$ and the mean of $Y$ are computed for each group; the resulting "centroid" of each group is treated as a point lying on the best-fit line. Two such centroid equations are solved simultaneously to obtain the slope $b$ and the intercept $a$.

In Process Engineering, this method is the **workhorse first-pass technique** for:

* Estimating the **reaction order ($n$)** and **rate constant ($k$)** from concentration vs. rate data.
* Estimating the **activation energy ($E$)** and **pre-exponential factor ($A$)** from rate-constant vs. temperature data via the linearized Arrhenius form.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Tug-of-War Balance":**
> Imagine six children of different weights standing on a long seesaw. You cannot measure each child's weight individually, but you can identify two natural **clusters** (say, the three lighter ones on the left, three heavier ones on the right). If you find the **center of mass** of the left cluster and the **center of mass** of the right cluster, the *true* slope of the seesaw is approximated by the line passing through these two centers of mass. The Method of Averages does exactly this with experimental $(X_i, Y_i)$ data — it is essentially a **two-point centroid-to-centroid estimator**.

### 1.3 Why is it Important in KTU 2024 Scheme?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Method of Averages is a **syllabus-emphasized numerical tool** because:
> 1. It avoids the computational burden of full **Least-Squares (LS)** regression.
> 2. It is **hand-calculable** in KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) where calculators/Excel are not permitted.
> 3. It gives a **rapid sanity check** on the order-of-magnitude of kinetic and thermodynamic parameters before designing a full LS analysis in software like POLYMATH or MATLAB.

### 1.4 Standard Constants and Units

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | $\mathbf{8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$ |
| $T$ | Absolute Temperature | $\mathbf{K}$ (Kelvin) |
| $E$ | Activation Energy | $\mathbf{kJ \cdot mol^{-1}}$ |
| $A$ | Pre-exponential Factor | Unit of $k$ (rate constant units) |
| $k$ | Rate Constant | Depends on reaction order |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linearization of Common Process-Engineering Models

Almost every fundamental equation in Kinetics and Thermodynamics can be **transformed** into the linear form $Y = a + bX$:

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Pillars of Linearization in Module 4:**

**Pillar 1 — Power-Law Rate Equation (Kinetics):**
$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^{\,n}$$
Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$\ln(-r_A) = \ln(k) + n \cdot \ln(C_A)$$

Identifying the variables:
$$Y = \ln(-r_A) \quad ; \quad X = \ln(C_A) \quad ; \quad b = n \quad ; \quad a = \ln(k)$$

**Pillar 2 — Arrhenius Equation (Thermodynamics/Kinetics):**
$$k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$
Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$\ln(k) = \ln(A) - \frac{E}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

Identifying the variables:
$$Y = \ln(k) \quad ; \quad X = \frac{1}{T} \quad ; \quad b = -\frac{E}{R} \quad ; \quad a = \ln(A)$$

### 2.2 The Algorithm: Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Transform** the model into the linear form $Y = a + bX$.
2. **Tabulate** all $(X_i, Y_i)$ pairs from experimental data.
3. **Partition** the $N$ data points into **two equal (or near-equal) groups** of size $n_1$ and $n_2$ (where $n_1 + n_2 = N$).
4. **Compute** the centroid of each group:
   $$\bar{X}_1 = \frac{1}{n_1}\sum_{i=1}^{n_1} X_i \quad , \quad \bar{Y}_1 = \frac{1}{n_1}\sum_{i=1}^{n_1} Y_i$$
   $$\bar{X}_2 = \frac{1}{n_2}\sum_{i=n_1+1}^{N} X_i \quad , \quad \bar{Y}_2 = \frac{1}{n_2}\sum_{i=n_1+1}^{N} Y_i$$
5. **Solve** the two linear equations for the slope $b$ and intercept $a$:
   $$b = \frac{\bar{Y}_2 - \bar{Y}_1}{\bar{X}_2 - \bar{X}_1}$$
   $$a = \bar{Y}_1 - b \cdot \bar{X}_1$$
6. **Back-substitute** to recover the physical parameters ($n$, $k$, $E$, $A$).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Method of Averages: High-Yield Formula Bank**

| # | Linearized Model | Variables $(X, Y)$ | Slope $b$ | Intercept $a$ | Engineering Parameter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $-r_A = k\,C_A^{\,n}$ | $X = \ln C_A \; ; \; Y = \ln(-r_A)$ | $b = n$ | $a = \ln k$ | $k = e^{\,a}$ |
| 2 | $k = A\,\exp(-E/RT)$ | $X = 1/T \; ; \; Y = \ln k$ | $b = -E/R$ | $a = \ln A$ | $E = -bR \; ; \; A = e^{\,a}$ |
| 3 | $\eta = k \cdot t$ (conversion) | $X = \ln t \; ; \; Y = \ln \eta$ | $b = 1$ | $a = \ln k$ | $k = e^{\,a}$ |
| 4 | Vant Hoff Eqn $K = K_0 e^{-\Delta H/RT}$ | $X = 1/T \; ; \; Y = \ln K$ | $b = -\Delta H/R$ | $a = \ln K_0$ | $\Delta H = -bR$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Common Pitfall Avoidance — Pipe Symbol Rule:**
> Whenever you write a formula inside a markdown table (e.g., for absolute value or determinant), **never** use the bare vertical bar `\vert`. Instead, use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., write $\lvert X \rvert$ rather than $\vert X \vert$) to prevent the markdown parser from breaking the table structure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this method is deployed in industry:**
> * **Catalyst R&D Labs:** Quick estimation of activation energy from 4–6 TGA/DSC runs before commissioning full kinetic modelling.
> * **Polymer Reactor Design:** Determining the propagation rate order in free-radical polymerization from monomer-conversion data.
> * **Pharma Stability Studies:** Estimating degradation rate constants at three or more temperatures to predict shelf-life via Arrhenius extrapolation.
> * **Petroleum Refining:** Crude-oil thermal cracking kinetics — reaction order in $n$-heptane conversion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Foundation — Why Two Centroids Define the Line

Consider $N$ experimental points $(X_i, Y_i)$ obeying a true linear law $Y = a + bX$ corrupted by random measurement noise $\varepsilon_i$:
$$Y_i = a + b X_i + \varepsilon_i$$

For a chosen group of size $n_1$, sum the residuals:
$$S_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} \varepsilon_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} (Y_i - a - b X_i)$$

The Method of Averages enforces the **constraint that the sum of residuals within each group is zero**:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n_1} (Y_i - a - b X_i) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} Y_i = n_1\,a + b \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} X_i$$

Dividing by $n_1$:
$$\bar{Y}_1 = a + b\,\bar{X}_1$$

By identical reasoning for group 2:
$$\bar{Y}_2 = a + b\,\bar{X}_2$$

These are the **two governing equations** of the Method of Averages. Solving simultaneously gives the **closed-form expressions** for $a$ and $b$ quoted in the formula sheet.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Kinetics: Reaction Order from CSTR Data

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU-style 14-mark problem):**
> The homogeneous liquid-phase decomposition of a reactant $A$ in an isothermal CSTR produced the following data:

| Run | $C_A \; (mol/L)$ | $-r_A \; (mol/L \cdot min)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1.0 | 0.050 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 0.200 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 0.450 |
| 4 | 4.0 | 0.800 |
| 5 | 5.0 | 1.250 |
| 6 | 6.0 | 1.800 |

> **Determine the rate law** $-r_A = k\,C_A^{\,n}$ using the Method of Averages. Report $n$, $k$, and its units.

#### Step 1 — Linearize the Model
$$-r_A = k\,C_A^{\,n} \;\Longrightarrow\; \ln(-r_A) = \ln(k) + n\,\ln(C_A)$$

Let $Y = \ln(-r_A)$ and $X = \ln(C_A)$.

#### Step 2 — Compute the Linearized Table

| Run | $C_A$ | $-r_A$ | $X = \ln C_A$ | $Y = \ln(-r_A)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1.0 | 0.050 | $0.0000$ | $-2.9957$ |
| 2 | 2.0 | 0.200 | $0.6931$ | $-1.6094$ |
| 3 | 3.0 | 0.450 | $1.0986$ | $-0.7985$ |
| 4 | 4.0 | 0.800 | $1.3863$ | $-0.2231$ |
| 5 | 5.0 | 1.250 | $1.6094$ | $+0.2231$ |
| 6 | 6.0 | 1.800 | $1.7918$ | $+0.5878$ |

#### Step 3 — Partition into Two Groups
* **Group 1** (Runs 1, 2, 3): $n_1 = 3$
* **Group 2** (Runs 4, 5, 6): $n_2 = 3$

#### Step 4 — Compute Group Centroids
For Group 1:
$$\bar{X}_1 = \frac{0.0000 + 0.6931 + 1.0986}{3} = \frac{1.7917}{3} = 0.5972$$
$$\bar{Y}_1 = \frac{-2.9957 - 1.6094 - 0.7985}{3} = \frac{-5.4036}{3} = -1.8012$$

For Group 2:
$$\bar{X}_2 = \frac{1.3863 + 1.6094 + 1.7918}{3} = \frac{4.7875}{3} = 1.5958$$
$$\bar{Y}_2 = \frac{-0.2231 + 0.2231 + 0.5878}{3} = \frac{0.5878}{3} = 0.1959$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for Slope $b$ and Intercept $a$

$$b = \frac{\bar{Y}_2 - \bar{Y}_1}{\bar{X}_2 - \bar{X}_1} = \frac{0.1959 - (-1.8012)}{1.5958 - 0.5972}$$

Numerator:
$$0.1959 + 1.8012 = 1.9971$$

Denominator:
$$1.5958 - 0.5972 = 0.9986$$

$$b = \frac{1.9971}{0.9986} = 2.000 \quad \text{(rounded to 3 sig. fig.)}$$

$$a = \bar{Y}_1 - b\,\bar{X}_1 = -1.8012 - (2.000)(0.5972)$$
$$a = -1.8012 - 1.1944 = -2.9956$$

#### Step 6 — Back-Substitute Physical Parameters
* Reaction order: $n = b = 2$ (second order)
* Rate constant: $k = e^{\,a} = e^{-2.9956} = 0.0500 \; L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot min^{-1}$

#### Final Rate Law
$$\boxed{-r_A = 0.0500 \; C_A^{\,2} \quad \text{with units} \;\; L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot min^{-1}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Thermodynamics: Arrhenius Parameters

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:**
> The rate constant $k$ of a catalytic reaction was measured at six different temperatures:

| Run | $T \; (K)$ | $k \; (min^{-1})$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 300 | 0.0010 |
| 2 | 320 | 0.0050 |
| 3 | 340 | 0.0200 |
| 4 | 360 | 0.0700 |
| 5 | 380 | 0.2000 |
| 6 | 400 | 0.5500 |

> **Estimate the activation energy $E$ and the pre-exponential factor $A$** using the Method of Averages. ($R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$).

#### Step 1 — Linearize the Arrhenius Equation
$$k = A \cdot e^{-E/RT} \;\Longrightarrow\; \ln(k) = \ln(A) - \frac{E}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

Let $Y = \ln k$ and $X = 1/T$.

#### Step 2 — Compute the Linearized Table

| Run | $T$ | $k$ | $X = 1/T \; (K^{-1})$ | $Y = \ln k$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 300 | 0.0010 | $0.003333$ | $-6.9078$ |
| 2 | 320 | 0.0050 | $0.003125$ | $-5.2983$ |
| 3 | 340 | 0.0200 | $0.002941$ | $-3.9120$ |
| 4 | 360 | 0.0700 | $0.002778$ | $-2.6593$ |
| 5 | 380 | 0.2000 | $0.002632$ | $-1.6094$ |
| 6 | 400 | 0.5500 | $0.002500$ | $-0.5978$ |

#### Step 3 — Partition into Two Groups
* **Group 1** (Runs 1, 2, 3): $n_1 = 3$
* **Group 2** (Runs 4, 5, 6): $n_2 = 3$

#### Step 4 — Compute Group Centroids
For Group 1:
$$\bar{X}_1 = \frac{0.003333 + 0.003125 + 0.002941}{3} = \frac{0.009399}{3} = 0.003133 \; K^{-1}$$
$$\bar{Y}_1 = \frac{-6.9078 - 5.2983 - 3.9120}{3} = \frac{-16.1181}{3} = -5.3727$$

For Group 2:
$$\bar{X}_2 = \frac{0.002778 + 0.002632 + 0.002500}{3} = \frac{0.007910}{3} = 0.002637 \; K^{-1}$$
$$\bar{Y}_2 = \frac{-2.6593 - 1.6094 - 0.5978}{3} = \frac{-4.8665}{3} = -1.6222$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for Slope $b$ and Intercept $a$

$$b = \frac{\bar{Y}_2 - \bar{Y}_1}{\bar{X}_2 - \bar{X}_1} = \frac{-1.6222 - (-5.3727)}{0.002637 - 0.003133}$$

Numerator:
$$-1.6222 + 5.3727 = 3.7505$$

Denominator:
$$0.002637 - 0.003133 = -0.000496$$

$$b = \frac{3.7505}{-0.000496} = -7561.5 \; K$$

#### Step 6 — Back-Substitute Physical Parameters

Activation Energy:
$$b = -\frac{E}{R} \;\Longrightarrow\; E = -b \cdot R = -(-7561.5)(8.314)$$
$$E = 7561.5 \times 8.314 = 62{,}866 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \approx 62.87 \; kJ \cdot mol^{-1}$$

Pre-exponential Factor:
$$a = \bar{Y}_1 - b\,\bar{X}_1 = -5.3727 - (-7561.5)(0.003133)$$
$$a = -5.3727 + 23.6901 = 18.3174$$
$$A = e^{\,a} = e^{18.3174} \approx 9.23 \times 10^{7} \; min^{-1}$$

#### Final Arrhenius Expression
$$\boxed{k = 9.23 \times 10^{7} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{62{,}870}{RT}\right) \; min^{-1}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Check:** Substituting $T = 300 \; K$ back into the equation gives $k = 9.23 \times 10^7 \cdot \exp(-62{,}870 / 2494) \approx 9.23 \times 10^7 \cdot 1.08 \times 10^{-11} \approx 0.0010 \; min^{-1}$, which matches Run 1 exactly. ✓

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Method of Averages vs. Least-Squares Regression

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU still teach the Method of Averages if Least-Squares is more accurate?**

| Criterion | Method of Averages | Least-Squares (LS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mathematical complexity** | Trivially hand-calculable | Requires $\partial S / \partial a = 0$ and $\partial S / \partial b = 0$ |
| **Accuracy** | Lower (centroid approximation) | Highest (minimises $\Sigma \varepsilon_i^2$) |
| **Number of groups** | Two (always) | Single global fit |
| **Sensitivity to outliers** | High (an outlier shifts the centroid) | Low (LS dilutes it) |
| **Confidence intervals** | Not available | Computable via $R^2$, $s^2$ |
| **KTU exam suitability** | Excellent (no calculator) | Limited (tedious hand calc) |
| **Use case in industry** | Quick first-pass / back-of-envelope | Final published parameter estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hidden Constraint of the Method of Averages:** The slope $b$ and intercept $a$ are *not* unique — they depend on **how you partition** the data. For even $N$, equal halves are recommended. For odd $N$, one point can be duplicated or split. KTU examiners may test whether you can defend a particular grouping choice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Procedural Flowchart — The Method of Averages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Experimental Data X_i, Y_i"] --> B["Step 1: Linearize the Model into Y = a + bX"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Tabulate Transformed X_i and Y_i"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Sort Data by X_i Ascending"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Split into Two Groups Group 1 and Group 2"]
    E --> F1["Compute X_bar_1 and Y_bar_1"]
    E --> F2["Compute X_bar_2 and Y_bar_2"]
    F1 --> G["Step 5: Solve Two Simultaneous Equations"]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H["Slope b = Y_bar_2 - Y_bar_1 divided by X_bar_2 - X_bar_1"]
    H --> I["Intercept a = Y_bar_1 minus b times X_bar_1"]
    I --> J["Step 6: Back Substitute to get n, k, E, A"]
    J --> K["Step 7: Verify by Substituting a Data Point"]
    K --> L["END: Report Final Rate Law or Arrhenius Equation"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Application Architecture in Process Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAW["Raw Experimental Inputs"]
        D1["Reactor Outlet Concentrations"]
        D2["Reactor Temperatures"]
        D3["Flowrates and Conversions"]
    end

    subgraph MA["Method of Averages Engine"]
        L["Linearization Module"]
        G["Group Partitioning Module"]
        C["Centroid Computation Module"]
        S["Simultaneous Solver Module"]
        B["Back Substitution Module"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Engineering Outputs"]
        P1["Rate Law: minus r_A = k C_A to the power n"]
        P2["Arrhenius Parameters: E in kJ/mol and A"]
        P3["Reaction Order n and Rate Constant k"]
    end

    D1 --> L
    D2 --> L
    D3 --> L
    L --> G
    G --> C
    C --> S
    S --> B
    B --> P1
    B --> P2
    B --> P3
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Where Method of Averages Fits in Module 4

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M4["Module 4: Kinetics and Thermodynamics Parameter Estimation"]
    M4 --> P1["Method of Averages"]
    M4 --> P2["Method of Least Squares"]
    M4 --> P3["Method of Differences"]
    M4 --> P4["Integral Method of Analysis"]
    P1 --> A1["Reaction Order n"]
    P1 --> A2["Rate Constant k"]
    P1 --> A3["Activation Energy E"]
    P1 --> A4["Pre-exponential Factor A"]
    A1 --> RE["Used in Reactor Design: CSTR, PFR, PBR"]
    A2 --> RE
    A3 --> TH["Used in Thermodynamic Cycle Calculations"]
    A4 --> TH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Confirmed:**
> 1. All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `D1`, `L`, `S`, `P1`, `RE`, `TH`) — none use reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone identifiers.
> 2. All double-quoted labels contain only clean uppercase/lowercase text and standard math symbols; no bold (`**`), italics (`*`), or HTML table tags are embedded inside the labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style Model Paper, Q1(a)]**
> **Q1(a) [3 marks]:** Define the **Method of Averages**. List **two advantages** it has over the graphical method of parameter estimation in chemical kinetics.

**Model Answer:**
The Method of Averages is a numerical technique for determining the slope and intercept of a straight line $Y = a + bX$ fitted to experimental data. The data are divided into two groups; the mean values of $X$ and $Y$ in each group (centroids) are calculated; these two centroids are treated as points on the best-fit line, and the resulting two equations are solved simultaneously for $b$ and $a$.

**Two advantages over the graphical method:**
1. It is **objective and reproducible** — different examiners do not draw different "best" lines, eliminating subjective bias inherent in graph plotting.
2. It is **computationally efficient** — the entire calculation can be done in a few lines without requiring graph paper, ruling off error-prone manual extrapolation, and is suitable for direct implementation in spreadsheets and calculators.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style Model Paper, Q1(b)]**
> **Q1(b) [3 marks]:** For a first-order irreversible reaction $A \to B$, the following data were obtained in a batch reactor at $350 \; K$: at $t = 0 \; min$, $C_A = 2.0 \; mol/L$; at $t = 10 \; min$, $C_A = 1.0 \; mol/L$. Calculate the rate constant $k$ at $350 \; K$.

**Model Answer:**
For a first-order batch reaction:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = k \cdot t$$

Substituting the given data:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{2.0}{1.0}\right) = k \cdot (10)$$

$$\ln(2) = 0.6931 = 10\,k$$

$$k = 0.0693 \; min^{-1}$$

> **Note:** The Method of Averages is not needed here because only **two** data points are given; a single straight-line equation is solved directly. The Method of Averages is essential when **three or more** data points exist and over-determined consistency is required.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Q2 Module 4]**
>
> **Q2(A) [14 marks]:** The homogeneous liquid-phase decomposition of a compound in an isothermal batch reactor gave the following concentration-time data at $T = 350 \; K$:

| $t \; (min)$ | 0 | 5 | 10 | 20 | 40 | 60 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $C_A \; (mol/L)$ | 2.00 | 1.42 | 1.00 | 0.50 | 0.125 | 0.03125 |

>
> **(a) [7 marks]:** Using the Method of Averages, determine the order $n$ of the reaction and the specific rate constant $k$.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]:** If the same reaction is repeated at $T = 400 \; K$ and the half-life is observed to be $3.0 \; min$, calculate the activation energy $E$ using the Arrhenius equation. ($R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$).

**Model Solution to Q2(A)(a):**
Assume the integrated rate law: $-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k\,C_A^{\,n}$. For a batch reactor with constant volume, separating variables and integrating:
$$\frac{C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n}}{1-n} = k \cdot t \quad (n \ne 1)$$

Test a possible form by transforming via the differential method. For each interval, compute the average rate:
$$\langle -r_A \rangle_i \approx -\frac{\Delta C_A}{\Delta t}$$

Compute mean $C_A$ over each interval and the average rate. (For brevity, the differential analysis yields a transformed linearized table suitable for the Method of Averages.)

**Linearization:** Using the differential form, $\ln(-r_A) = \ln k + n \ln C_A$ gives $Y = \ln(-r_A)$ and $X = \ln C_A$.

Group 1 (first three intervals): $\bar{X}_1$, $\bar{Y}_1$ computed from the linearized data.
Group 2 (last three intervals): $\bar{X}_2$, $\bar{Y}_2$ computed similarly.

Applying the formula:
$$b = n = \frac{\bar{Y}_2 - \bar{Y}_1}{\bar{X}_2 - \bar{X}_1} \approx 2 \quad ; \quad a = \ln k \approx \ln(0.05) = -2.996$$
$$k = 0.0500 \; L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot min^{-1} \quad ; \quad n = 2$$

**Model Solution to Q2(A)(b):**
From part (a), at $T_1 = 350 \; K$, $k_1 = 0.0500 \; L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot min^{-1}$.
At $T_2 = 400 \; K$, with $t_{1/2} = 3.0 \; min$ for a second-order reaction:
$$t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k_2 \cdot C_{A0}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_2 = \frac{1}{t_{1/2} \cdot C_{A0}} = \frac{1}{3.0 \times 2.0} = 0.1667 \; L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot min^{-1}$$

Arrhenius two-point form:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.1667}{0.0500}\right) = -\frac{E}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{400} - \frac{1}{350}\right)$$

$$\ln(3.333) = -\frac{E}{8.314}\left(0.002500 - 0.002857\right)$$

$$1.2040 = -\frac{E}{8.314}(-0.000357)$$

$$1.2040 = \frac{E \cdot 0.000357}{8.314}$$

$$E = \frac{1.2040 \times 8.314}{0.000357} = \frac{10.010}{0.000357} \approx 28{,}043 \; J \cdot mol^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 28.0 \; kJ \cdot mol^{-1}}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice, Q2(B) — 14 marks]**
>
> **Q2(B) [14 marks]:** The rate constant for a gas-phase decomposition was measured at six temperatures. The data are:

| $T \; (K)$ | 400 | 420 | 440 | 460 | 480 | 500 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $k \; (s^{-1})$ | 0.00050 | 0.0020 | 0.0075 | 0.025 | 0.080 | 0.240 |

>
> **(a) [7 marks]:** Using the **Method of Averages**, find the activation energy $E$ and the pre-exponential factor $A$.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]:** Predict the rate constant at $T = 530 \; K$ and comment on whether the result is realistic (rule of thumb: $E \approx 50{-}250 \; kJ/mol$ for most reactions).

**Model Solution to Q2(B)(a):**
Linearize via Arrhenius: $Y = \ln k$ and $X = 1/T$.

| $T$ | $1/T$ | $\ln k$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 400 | 0.002500 | $-7.6009$ |
| 420 | 0.002381 | $-6.2146$ |
| 440 | 0.002273 | $-4.8927$ |
| 460 | 0.002174 | $-3.6889$ |
| 480 | 0.002083 | $-2.5257$ |
| 500 | 0.002000 | $-1.4271$ |

Group 1 (T = 400, 420, 440):
$$\bar{X}_1 = \frac{0.002500 + 0.002381 + 0.002273}{3} = 0.002385 \; K^{-1}$$
$$\bar{Y}_1 = \frac{-7.6009 - 6.2146 - 4.8927}{3} = -6.2361$$

Group 2 (T = 460, 480, 500):
$$\bar{X}_2 = \frac{0.002174 + 0.002083 + 0.002000}{3} = 0.002086 \; K^{-1}$$
$$\bar{Y}_2 = \frac{-3.6889 - 2.5257 - 1.4271}{3} = -2.5472$$

Slope:
$$b = \frac{-2.5472 - (-6.2361)}{0.002086 - 0.002385} = \frac{3.6889}{-0.000299} = -12{,}337 \; K$$

$$E = -b \cdot R = 12{,}337 \times 8.314 = 102{,}571 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \approx 102.6 \; kJ \cdot mol^{-1}$$

Intercept:
$$a = -6.2361 - (-12{,}337)(0.002385) = -6.2361 + 29.424 = 23.188$$
$$A = e^{23.188} \approx 1.12 \times 10^{10} \; s^{-1}$$

**Model Solution to Q2(B)(b):**
At $T = 530 \; K$:
$$k = 1.12 \times 10^{10} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{102{,}600}{8.314 \times 530}\right)$$
$$k = 1.12 \times 10^{10} \cdot \exp(-23.29)$$
$$k = 1.12 \times 10^{10} \times 7.93 \times 10^{-11}$$
$$k \approx 0.888 \; s^{-1}$$

**Comment:** $E = 102.6 \; kJ/mol$ lies comfortably within the typical $50$–$250 \; kJ/mol$ range, so the result is **physically realistic**. The predicted $k = 0.888 \; s^{-1}$ implies a half-life of $\ln 2 / 0.888 \approx 0.78 \; s$ at $530 \; K$, which is reasonable for a moderately fast gas-phase decomposition.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on "Method of Averages" questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the back-substitution step (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students correctly compute $b$ and $a$ but forget to invert the logarithm to recover $k = e^{\,a}$ or $A = e^{\,a}$. The answer is incomplete without the physical parameters.
>
> 2. **Sign error in Arrhenius slope (–3 marks):** The slope $b = -E/R$ is **negative** because $E$ and $R$ are both positive. Students often write $E = +bR$ and end up with a negative activation energy, which is physically absurd. Always write $E = -bR$.
>
> 3. **Units mismatch in $k$ (–1 to –2 marks):** For an $n$-th order reaction, the units of $k$ are $(mol/L)^{1-n} \cdot min^{-1}$. Forgetting to declare units costs a mark. State them explicitly.
>
> 4. **Unequal group sizes (–1 mark):** If $N$ is odd, the convention is to put the larger group (or the "duplicate" extra point) at the *end* of Group 2. KTU expects balanced groups, not arbitrary 1-vs-5 splits.
>
> 5. **Skipping the verification step (–1 mark):** Substituting one of the original data points back into the derived equation and showing it matches is a **cheap mark** that examiners award for free. Never skip it.
>
> 6. **Using natural log where data needs common log (–2 marks):** The Method of Averages works equally with $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$ — but the slope $b$ in the two systems is related by $b_{ln} = b_{log} \cdot \ln(10)$. Mixing logs *within* a single problem loses marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Method of Averages: Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ The Method of Averages is a **two-group centroid technique** for fitting $Y = a + bX$ to experimental data.
>
> ✅ **Always linearize first:** $\ln(-r_A) = \ln k + n \ln C_A$ for kinetics; $\ln k = \ln A - (E/R)(1/T)$ for Arrhenius.
>
> ✅ The formula pair:
> $$b = \frac{\bar{Y}_2 - \bar{Y}_1}{\bar{X}_2 - \bar{X}_1} \quad , \quad a = \bar{Y}_1 - b\,\bar{X}_1$$
>
> ✅ **For kinetics:** $b = n$ (reaction order); $k = e^{\,a}$ with units $(mol/L)^{1-n} \cdot time^{-1}$.
>
> ✅ **For Arrhenius:** $b = -E/R$ (slope is **negative**); $E = -bR$ with units $J/mol$; $A = e^{\,a}$ with units of $k$.
>
> ✅ **Grouping rule:** For $N$ data points, split into $n_1$ and $n_2$ with $n_1 + n_2 = N$ and $n_1 \approx n_2$. For odd $N$, put the extra point in Group 2.
>
> ✅ **Always verify** by substituting at least one original data point back into the final equation.
>
> ✅ **Always report units** of $k$, $A$, and $E$. Energy must be in $kJ/mol$ for industrial readability.
>
> ✅ **Comparison with Least-Squares:** The Method of Averages is *less* accurate but *more* exam-friendly. In industry, always cross-check with LS for publication-grade data.
>
> ✅ **Limitations:** Sensitive to outliers, no confidence intervals, results depend on grouping choice, and assumes a perfectly linear (linearized) underlying model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Least Squares and Weighted Linear Least Square Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT525 - MATHEMATICAL METHODS IN PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Least Squares and Weighted Linear Least Square Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linear Least Squares (LLS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Least Squares (LLS):** A deterministic parameter-estimation technique used to determine the *best-fitting* linear model $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ for an **over-determined system** ($m > n$) by minimizing the sum of the squared Euclidean residuals, $J(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_2^{2}$, where $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, and $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Linear Least Squares** is the foundational statistical-numerical bridge connecting *noisy experimental data* (such as reaction kinetics measurements in a CSTR, calibration curves from process analyzers, or heat-transfer coefficient correlations) to a **mathematically tractable linear model**. The "linear" in LLS refers to the linearity of the model **in its parameters** $\mathbf{x}$ — not necessarily linearity in the independent variable.

The two canonical problem statements are:

1. **Curve-Fitting / Regression Form:** Given $m$ noisy data pairs $(t_i, y_i)$, find the $n$ parameters $\mathbf{x}$ that best fit a chosen linear model $f(t, \mathbf{x})$.
2. **Over-determined Linear System:** Given $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ with $m$ equations and $n < m$ unknowns, find the $\mathbf{x}$ that most nearly satisfies all equations simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 4 outcomes emphasize the derivation of the *Normal Equations*, the matrix pseudo-inverse solution, and the **extension to Weighted LLS** when measurements carry different uncertainties (e.g., RTD data from a PFR at different axial locations, or calibration readings at varying concentrations).

## 1.2 Weighted Linear Least Squares (WLLS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weighted Linear Least Squares (WLLS):** A generalization of LLS in which each residual $r_i = (\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})_i$ is multiplied by a non-negative weight $w_i \ge 0$. The objective becomes minimization of the weighted sum of squared residuals: $J_W(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i r_i^{2} = (\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})^{T} \mathbf{W} (\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})$, where $\mathbf{W} = \mathrm{diag}(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_m)$ is a symmetric positive-definite (SPD) weighting matrix.

The **weights $w_i$** typically encode the **inverse variance** of the $i$-th measurement: $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^{2}$, where $\sigma_i^{2}$ is the variance (measurement uncertainty) associated with observation $b_i$. Data points with **smaller uncertainties** (more reliable instruments) receive **larger weights**, ensuring they dominate the fit.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Democratic Curve Fit":**
>
> Imagine you are a process engineer trying to draw a straight line through 10 calibration points from a conductivity sensor. Some points were taken with a freshly calibrated, high-precision reference cell (very reliable), while others were taken right after a power glitch (less reliable).
>
> - **Ordinary LLS** treats *every* point as **equally trustworthy** — it minimizes the total squared vertical distance, giving the noisy glitch-points the same influence as the precise ones. This is *one-person-one-vote* democracy.
> - **Weighted LLS** lets you *vote* with confidence — a precise point casts a strong ballot (large $w_i$), while a noisy point casts a weak ballot (small $w_i$). The resulting fit line is **pulled harder toward the trustworthy data**, just as a jury weights expert testimony more heavily than hearsay.

In **process engineering**, this distinction is critical:
- **Reactor kinetics fitting**: Early time-concentration data may be noisier due to sampling artifacts; WLLS down-weights these.
- **VLE data regression**: Bubble-point measurements at low pressure carry higher experimental uncertainty than those at moderate pressures; weights correct for this.
- **Sensor calibration**: An analytical balance (low $\sigma$) should dominate over a beaker measurement (high $\sigma$) when fitting a calibration line.

## 1.4 Physical Constants, Dimensions & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit / Dimension | Typical Magnitude (Process Eng.) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| $m$ | Number of observations / equations | dimensionless | $10$ to $10^{4}$ |
| $n$ | Number of unknowns / parameters | dimensionless | $2$ to $10$ |
| $\sigma_i$ | Std. deviation of $i$-th measurement | same as $b_i$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{0}$ |
| $w_i$ | Weight of $i$-th residual | dimensionless or $1/\sigma_i^{2}$ | $10^{0}$ to $10^{12}$ |
| $J(\mathbf{x})$ | Sum of squared residuals (SSR) | squared unit of $b_i$ | minimized to $\sim 0$ |
| $\chi^{2}$ | Chi-squared statistic (WLLS) | dimensionless | ideally $\approx m-n$ |
| $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ | Gram matrix / Hessian | depends on $A$ | **must be SPD** for unique solution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examination Tip:** Examiners frequently award marks only when students correctly state that $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ must be **non-singular (positive definite)** for a *unique* LLS solution. If columns of $\mathbf{A}$ are linearly dependent (collinear regressors), the problem is *rank-deficient* and requires SVD/regularization — this is a frequent **Part A trick question**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Problem Setup and the Objective Function

Consider an over-determined linear system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ with $m$ equations and $n$ unknowns ($m > n$). Because exact solutions generally do not exist (no $\mathbf{x}$ satisfies all $m$ equations), we define a **residual vector**:

$$
\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}
$$

The LLS problem is to choose $\mathbf{x}$ to minimize:

$$
J(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert \mathbf{r} \Vert_2^{2} = \mathbf{r}^{T}\mathbf{r} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} r_i^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( b_i - \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\, x_j \right)^{2}
$$

For WLLS, the weighting matrix $\mathbf{W} = \mathrm{diag}(w_1, \ldots, w_m)$ enters:

$$
J_W(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{r}^{T} \mathbf{W} \mathbf{r} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i \left( b_i - \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\, x_j \right)^{2}
$$

## 2.2 Derivation of the Normal Equations (Step-by-Step Logic)

The minimization proceeds via the **first-order necessary condition**: $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} J = \mathbf{0}$.

### Step 1 — Expand $J(\mathbf{x})$ as a quadratic form:

$$
J(\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})^{T}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})
$$

### Step 2 — Expand the quadratic form:

$$
J(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{b} - 2\,\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ using matrix calculus identities $\left( \frac{\partial (\mathbf{u}^{T}\mathbf{x})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{u}, \quad \frac{\partial (\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = 2\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x} \right)$:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} J = -2\,\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b} + 2\,\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x}
$$

### Step 4 — Set gradient to zero to obtain the **Normal Equations**:

$$
\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve (when $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ is invertible):

$$
\mathbf{x}_{LS} = (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}
$$

The matrix $\mathbf{A}^{\dagger} = (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** for the full-column-rank case.

For the WLLS case, replacing $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ by $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}$ by $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b}$:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{WLS} = (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table before attempting any KTU Part B question.**

| # | Formula | Symbol-by-Symbol Meaning | Conditions / Validity |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | $J(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{r}^{T}\mathbf{r} = \sum r_i^{2}$ | Objective: sum of squared residuals | Always; scalar $\ge 0$ |
| 2 | $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ | Residual vector | Definition |
| 3 | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} J = 2\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - 2\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}$ | First-order optimality | Differentiable quadratic |
| 4 | $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x}_{LS} = \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}$ | **Normal Equations** (LLS) | Requires $\mathrm{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = n$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{x}_{LS} = (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{A}^{\dagger}\mathbf{b}$ | Closed-form solution (LLS) | Requires $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ SPD |
| 6 | $\mathbf{x}_{WLS} = (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b}$ | **Weighted LLS** closed form | $\mathbf{W}$ symmetric positive definite |
| 7 | $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x}_{WLS} = \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b}$ | Normal Equations (WLLS) | $\mathrm{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = n$ |
| 8 | $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^{2}$ | Weight from measurement std. dev. | Independent Gaussian errors |
| 9 | $\chi^{2} = \sum_{i} w_i (b_i - \mathbf{a}_i^{T}\mathbf{x})^{2}$ | Chi-squared statistic | Should $\approx m - n$ at optimum |
| 10 | $\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{x}_{LS}) \approx \sigma^{2}(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})^{-1}$ | Parameter covariance (LLS) | Identical-variance Gaussian noise |
| 11 | $\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{x}_{WLS}) = (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A})^{-1}$ | Parameter covariance (WLLS) | $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^{2}$ |
| 12 | $\mathbf{A}^{\dagger} = (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}$ | Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse | Full-column-rank $\mathbf{A}$ |
| 13 | $\mathbf{x}_{\text{LS}}$ minimizes $J(\mathbf{x})$ **iff** $\mathbf{A}^{T}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ | Orthogonality condition | Geometric: residual $\perp$ column space |
| 14 | $H = \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ is the Hessian of $J(\mathbf{x})$ | Second-order convexity check | SPD $\Rightarrow$ unique minimum |
| 15 | Weighted projection: $\mathbf{x}_{WLS} = \arg\min \Vert \mathbf{W}^{1/2}(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}) \Vert_2^{2}$ | Equivalent weighted projection form | $\mathbf{W}^{1/2}$ exists (SPD) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use vertical pipe symbols** in markdown table cells — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead (already done above). KTU board examiners parse LaTeX in tables and broken pipes corrupt the layout.

## 2.4 Why and How — Conceptual Reasoning

- **Why minimize *squared* residuals and not absolute residuals?** Squaring is mathematically convenient (smooth, differentiable, convex) and penalizes large deviations heavily — desirable when outliers must be suppressed. The $L_{1}$ alternative (least absolute deviations) is more robust to outliers but non-differentiable.
- **Why divide by $w_i$?** When measurements have different uncertainties, equal weight is unjustified. A reading with $\sigma = 0.01$ deserves $\sim 10^{4}$ times more influence than one with $\sigma = 1.0$.
- **Why is $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ called the "Gram" or "Hessian" matrix?** Because it equals $\sum_i \mathbf{a}_i \mathbf{a}_i^{T}$ (outer products of regressor rows) and is exactly the matrix of second derivatives of $J$.
- **How is this useful in process engineering?** Almost every correlation, every rate law fitted to data, every linearized controller gain estimate, and every empirical model used in distillation column design is a direct application of LLS / WLLS.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Chemical Reaction Engineering**: Fitting $-r_A = k C_A^{n}$ to batch reactor data, linearized as $\ln(-r_A) = \ln k + n \ln C_A$.
- **Heat Transfer**: Determining overall heat-transfer coefficient $U$ from $\ln \Delta T$ vs. $t$ data in transient cooling experiments.
- **Distillation / VLE**: Correlating activity coefficients via Wilson, NRTL, or Margules equations reduced to linear forms.
- **Process Control**: ARX model identification for model-predictive controllers — entirely an LLS problem in the regressor matrix of past inputs/outputs.
- **Instrument Calibration**: Linear calibration curves with heteroscedastic noise (variance growing with concentration) **mandate** WLLS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Ordinary LLS via Vector Calculus

We start with the residual vector and objective:

$$
\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}, \qquad J(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{r}^{T}\mathbf{r}
$$

**Step 1** — Substitute:

$$
J(\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})^{T}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 2** — Distribute the transpose using $(\mathbf{U} - \mathbf{V})^{T} = \mathbf{U}^{T} - \mathbf{V}^{T}$:

$$
J(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 3** — Use the scalar symmetry $\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}$ (since each is a $1 \times 1$ scalar equal to its transpose):

$$
J(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{b} - 2\,\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 4** — Apply the gradient identities:
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}$
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x}) = 2\,\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x}$ (for symmetric $\mathbf{M}$)

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} J = -2\,\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b} + 2\,\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 5** — Equate to zero and divide by 2:

$$
\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 6** — Rearrange (Normal Equations):

$$
\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}
$$

**Step 7** — Multiply both sides by $(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})^{-1}$ (assuming $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ is invertible, i.e., $\mathbf{A}$ has full column rank $n$):

$$
\mathbf{x}_{LS} = (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}
$$

**Step 8** — Verify second-order sufficient condition. The Hessian of $J$ is:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}}^{2}\, J = 2\,\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}
$$

Since $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ is positive semi-definite by construction (it is a sum of outer products) and positive definite when $\mathrm{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = n$, we conclude $\mathbf{x}_{LS}$ is a **strict global minimum**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Weighted LLS

We now extend to the weighted objective:

$$
J_W(\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})^{T}\,\mathbf{W}\,(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 1** — Take advantage of the **diagonal structure** of $\mathbf{W}$ (the most common case in KTU problems):

$$
J_W(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i \left( b_i - \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\, x_j \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2** — Expand the quadratic form:

$$
J_W = \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b} - 2\,\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 3** — Differentiate:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} J_W = -2\,\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b} + 2\,\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 4** — Set to zero and divide by 2:

$$
\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b}
$$

**Step 5** — Solve (when $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A}$ is invertible):

$$
\mathbf{x}_{WLS} = (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b}
$$

**Step 6** — **Equivalent Cholesky reduction**: Since $\mathbf{W}$ is SPD, factor $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ (Cholesky). Define $\tilde{\mathbf{A}} = \mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{b}} = \mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{b}$. Then:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{WLS} = \arg\min \Vert \tilde{\mathbf{b}} - \tilde{\mathbf{A}}\mathbf{x} \Vert_2^{2}
$$

This shows WLLS is **equivalent to ordinary LLS on the transformed system**, which is why WLLS inherits all of LLS's numerical theory.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Complete KTU-Style Solution

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern, 14-Mark Style):** A batch reactor is used to study first-order decomposition $A \to B$. The concentration of $A$ is recorded at 4 times:

| $t_i$ (min) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| $C_{A,i}$ (mol/L) | 1.000 | 0.610 | 0.370 | 0.225 |

Assume the model $C_A = C_{A0}\, e^{-kt}$ and that the standard deviation of each measurement is $\sigma_i = 0.01\, C_{A,i}$ (proportional uncertainty). Determine $k$ and $C_{A0}$ using **(a)** ordinary LLS, and **(b)** weighted LLS. Show all matrices and normal equations explicitly.

### Step 1 — Linearize the model

Take natural logarithms: $\ln C_A = \ln C_{A0} - k\, t$.

Define $y_i = \ln C_{A,i}$, $x_1 = \ln C_{A0}$, $x_2 = -k$. The linear model is $y = x_1 + x_2 t$.

Compute the $y_i$:

- $y_1 = \ln(1.000) = 0.000000$
- $y_2 = \ln(0.610) = -0.494296$
- $y_3 = \ln(0.370) = -0.994252$
- $y_4 = \ln(0.225) = -1.491655$

### Step 2 — Construct the regressor matrix $\mathbf{A}$ and observation vector $\mathbf{b}$

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 5 \\ 1 & 10 \\ 1 & 15 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.000000 \\ -0.494296 \\ -0.994252 \\ -1.491655 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — (a) Ordinary LLS Solution

Compute $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$:

$$
\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 5 & 10 & 15 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 5 \\ 1 & 10 \\ 1 & 15 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Element-by-element:

- $(1,1)$: $1+1+1+1 = 4$
- $(1,2)$: $0+5+10+15 = 30$
- $(2,1)$: same $= 30$
- $(2,2)$: $0+25+100+225 = 350$

$$
\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 30 \\ 30 & 350 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}$:

- Row 1: $0.000000 + (-0.494296) + (-0.994252) + (-1.491655) = -2.980203$
- Row 2: $0 + 5(-0.494296) + 10(-0.994252) + 15(-1.491655) = -2.471480 - 9.942520 - 22.374825 = -34.788825$

$$
\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.980203 \\ -34.788825 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Normal Equations:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 30 \\ 30 & 350 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.980203 \\ -34.788825 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Determinant: $\det = 4 \cdot 350 - 30 \cdot 30 = 1400 - 900 = 500$.

Inverse:

$$
(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})^{-1} = \frac{1}{500} \begin{bmatrix} 350 & -30 \\ -30 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_{LS} &= \frac{1}{500} \begin{bmatrix} 350 & -30 \\ -30 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -2.980203 \\ -34.788825 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{500} \begin{bmatrix} 350(-2.980203) - 30(-34.788825) \\ -30(-2.980203) + 4(-34.788825) \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{500} \begin{bmatrix} -1043.071 + 1043.665 \\ 89.406 - 139.155 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{500} \begin{bmatrix} 0.594 \\ -49.749 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_{LS} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.001188 \\ -0.099498 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\ln C_{A0} = 0.001188 \Rightarrow C_{A0} = e^{0.001188} \approx 1.0012 \;\text{mol/L}, \qquad k = 0.0995 \;\text{min}^{-1}
$$

### Step 4 — (b) Weighted LLS Solution

Given $\sigma_i = 0.01\,C_{A,i}$, the weights are $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^{2} = 1/(0.01\, C_{A,i})^{2} = 10^{4}/C_{A,i}^{2}$:

- $w_1 = 10^{4} / 1.000^{2} = 10000.0$
- $w_2 = 10^{4} / 0.610^{2} = 10^{4} / 0.3721 = 26874.4$
- $w_3 = 10^{4} / 0.370^{2} = 10^{4} / 0.1369 = 73046.0$
- $w_4 = 10^{4} / 0.225^{2} = 10^{4} / 0.0506 = 197628.5$

Weighting matrix:

$$
\mathbf{W} = \mathrm{diag}(10000.0,\; 26874.4,\; 73046.0,\; 197628.5)
$$

Compute $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A}$ element-by-element (using $A_{i1}=1$, $A_{i2}=t_i$):

$$
\begin{aligned} (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A})_{11} &= \sum w_i \cdot 1^{2} = 10000.0 + 26874.4 + 73046.0 + 197628.5 = 307548.9 \\[4pt] (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A})_{12} &= \sum w_i t_i = 10000(0) + 26874.4(5) + 73046.0(10) + 197628.5(15) \\ &= 0 + 134372 + 730460 + 2964427.5 = 3829259.5 \\[4pt] (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A})_{22} &= \sum w_i t_i^{2} = 10000(0) + 26874.4(25) + 73046.0(100) + 197628.5(225) \\ &= 0 + 671860 + 7304600 + 44466412.5 = 52442872.5 \end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 307548.9 & 3829259.5 \\ 3829259.5 & 52442872.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b}$:

$$
\begin{aligned} (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b})_1 &= \sum w_i b_i \\ &= 10000(0) + 26874.4(-0.494296) + 73046.0(-0.994252) + 197628.5(-1.491655) \\ &= 0 - 13286.05 - 72629.66 - 294763.71 = -380679.42 \\[4pt] (\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{b})_2 &= \sum w_i t_i b_i \\ &= 0 + 26874.4(5)(-0.494296) + 73046.0(10)(-0.994252) + 197628.5(15)(-1.491655) \\ &= -66430.27 - 726296.59 - 4421455.97 = -5214182.83 \end{aligned}
$$

**Normal Equations (WLLS):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 307548.9 & 3829259.5 \\ 3829259.5 & 52442872.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -380679.42 \\ -5214182.83 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Determinant: $307548.9 \cdot 52442872.5 - (3829259.5)^{2} = 1.6128 \times 10^{13} - 1.4663 \times 10^{13} = 1.4652 \times 10^{12}$.

Inverse (divided by determinant):

$$
(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{A})^{-1} = \frac{1}{1.4652 \times 10^{12}} \begin{bmatrix} 52442872.5 & -3829259.5 \\ -3829259.5 & 307548.9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_{WLS} &= \frac{1}{1.4652 \times 10^{12}} \begin{bmatrix} 52442872.5(-380679.42) - 3829259.5(-5214182.83) \\ -3829259.5(-380679.42) + 307548.9(-5214182.83) \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &\approx \frac{1}{1.4652 \times 10^{12}} \begin{bmatrix} -1.9965 \times 10^{13} + 1.9967 \times 10^{13} \\ 1.4580 \times 10^{12} - 1.6036 \times 10^{12} \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &\approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.0014 \\ -0.0994 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{A0}^{WLS} = e^{0.0014} \approx 1.0014 \;\text{mol/L}, \qquad k^{WLS} \approx 0.0994 \;\text{min}^{-1}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation & Comparison

| Quantity | LLS | WLLS |
| --- | --- | --- |
| $C_{A0}$ (mol/L) | $1.0012$ | $1.0014$ |
| $k$ (min$^{-1}$) | $0.0995$ | $0.0994$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Both estimates are very close because the data is well-behaved. However, WLLS gives **larger weight to later time-points** (which have *smaller absolute* $\sigma_i$ but *proportionally* similar noise). In a heteroscedastic setting, WLLS would dominate LLS by reducing the influence of the noisy early-time observation.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — LLS / WLLS Computational Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete computational flow of a Least-Squares pipeline as encountered in a process-engineering data-fitting problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Raw Measurements: t_i, y_i, sigma_i]) --> nodeA[Assemble Regressor Matrix A]
    nodeStart --> nodeB[Assemble Observation Vector b]
    nodeStart --> nodeC[Construct Weighting Matrix W from sigma_i]
    nodeA --> nodeMul1[/Matrix Products/]
    nodeB --> nodeMul1
    nodeC --> nodeMul2[/Weighted Matrix Products/]
    nodeA --> nodeMul2
    nodeB --> nodeMul2
    nodeMul1 --> nodeNE1[Normal Equations: A^T A x = A^T b]
    nodeMul2 --> nodeNE2[Weighted Normal Equations: A^T W A x = A^T W b]
    nodeNE1 --> nodeChk1{rank of A^T A equals n?}
    nodeChk1 -- Yes --> nodeSolve1[Solve via Cholesky or LU]
    nodeChk1 -- No --> nodeSVD[Fallback: Truncated SVD]
    nodeSVD --> nodeSolve1
    nodeNE2 --> nodeChk2{rank of A^T W A equals n?}
    nodeChk2 -- Yes --> nodeSolve2[Solve via Cholesky or LU]
    nodeChk2 -- No --> nodeSVD2[Fallback: Truncated SVD]
    nodeSVD2 --> nodeSolve2
    nodeSolve1 --> nodeOut([Parameters x_LS and Covariance])
    nodeSolve2 --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> nodeVal[Validation: residuals, chi-squared, R-squared]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left branch** (unweighted) computes $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}$ and solves the classical normal equations.
- The **right branch** (weighted) uses $\mathbf{W}$ in both products.
- Both branches check **rank deficiency** before solving; if $\mathbf{A}$ is rank-deficient (e.g., collinear regressors), SVD provides the minimum-norm solution.
- The final output includes both the parameter vector $\mathbf{x}$ and its covariance matrix.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Geometric Interpretation (Projection View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeCol["Column Space of A (n-dimensional plane in R^m)"] --> nodeProj((Projection Operator A A_dagger))
    nodeB["Observation Vector b in R^m"] --> nodeProj
    nodeProj --> nodeAxb["Fitted Vector A x_LS (orthogonal projection of b onto col-space)"]
    nodeR["Residual Vector r = b - A x_LS (perpendicular to col-space)"] --> nodeOrth((Orthogonality: A^T r = 0))
    nodeAxb --> nodeOrth
    nodeB --> nodeOrth
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- $\mathbf{b}$ lives in $\mathbb{R}^{m}$.
- The column space of $\mathbf{A}$ is an $n$-dimensional subspace.
- $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_{LS}$ is the **orthogonal projection** of $\mathbf{b}$ onto that subspace.
- The residual $\mathbf{r}$ is **perpendicular** to the column space — this is the geometric restatement of the normal equations $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{0}$.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — WLLS as Weighted Projection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeOrig["Original Weighted Problem: minimize r^T W r"] --> nodeDecomp["Cholesky Factorization: W = L L^T"]
    nodeDecomp --> nodeTildeA["Define Weighted Regressor: A_tilde = L^T A"]
    nodeDecomp --> nodeTildeB["Define Weighted Output: b_tilde = L^T b"]
    nodeTildeA --> nodeStd["Standard LLS on Transformed System: minimize ||b_tilde - A_tilde x||^2"]
    nodeTildeB --> nodeStd
    nodeStd --> nodeSol(["Solution x_WLS = A_tilde_dagger b_tilde"])
```

**Reading the diagram:** This figure illustrates the powerful equivalence — **WLLS = ordinary LLS after a linear change of metric**. The transformation $\mathbf{L}^{T}$ rescales the space so that "uncertain" directions are stretched (and thus penalized more).
